players.name: Jeremiyah Love
players.lateround_profile: Jeremiyah Love is the clear top running back in this year’s class. Projected to go in the first round — and almost definitely in the top half of it — Love brings one of the most complete production profiles we’ve seen in recent cycles. He even shared a backfield with fellow draft hopeful Jadarian Price and still stood out. Love spent all three collegiate seasons at Notre Dame, and his final two were particularly impressive. In 2025, he captured the Doak Walker Award as the nation’s top running back and finished as a Heisman finalist. From a ZAP Model standpoint, there aren’t any glaring red flags. His best-season prorated reception share sits at 12.4%. His peak adjusted total yards per team play checks in at 2.51. His receiving yards per team pass attempt comes in at 0.99. All strong numbers. If Love does land in the front half of Round 1, history becomes overwhelmingly encouraging. Since 2011, we’ve seen 10 running backs drafted in that range. Excluding Ashton Jeanty, who has only completed one NFL season, every single one delivered at least one 17-plus PPR points-per-game campaign within their first three years in the league. Six of them topped 20 PPR points per game in a season. That’s absurd. Love may not grade quite as cleanly as some of the elite first-round backs from recent classes, but zoom out and the picture is clear: he’s a well-rounded producer with first- round capital and no meaningful analytical holes. Wherever he ends up, Love projects as a major fantasy asset. His statistical comparables are ridiculous.
players.elevator_pitch:
2006 was the first year I published the RSP. Reggie Bush was the next big thing in football. Former 
pros who became analysts compared Bush to Gale Sayers. 
If you’re subscriber under 40 and came to football on your own, it’s likely you don’t know who Sayers was. One of my 
friends is 35, and I had to explain who Sayers was to the history of the game and the running back position. 
It clicked for him when I said Sayers was revered in the same way his parents (who are my age) revere Barry Sanders. 
Broach the names Sayers or Sanders to fans who saw one or both, and they begin speaking with hushed tones. 
Bush earned those comparisons on-air in sports programming before clickbait was a legitimate word. It was my first season 
evaluating players when Bush was the most popular skill player in that draft class. 
While I’m positive my current level of experience would have made the evaluation different, I’ve watched some of those old 
highlights. Bush would have had an excellent score. 
When Bush arrived in New Orleans, he pressed too hard to be the savior of the football team and the city, trying to bounce 
and cutback runs for big plays that he had approached with more discipline at USC. 
Bush needed six seasons to generate three years of production that was, at best, in the general vicinity of his rookie 
expectations. Bush missed 21 games during those first 6 years in the league, and his knees were becoming a problem. 
Bush had a good career, but it was nowhere near the expectations pre-draft. For fans and media who remember this as well 
as I, Jeremiyah Love’s style not only bears similarities to Bush, but there’s a vibe that public who knew Bush’s USC game 
are hoping Love has the leading role in “Reggie Bush II: This Time It’s for Real.” 
Considered the top skill-player in this draft class, Love is a versatile big-play threat with tremendous athletic ability for his 
size. If you could mix a lot of the stylistic elements of Bush and Alvin Kamara and blend their frames, you’d get a player like 
Love. 
Love is a slippery runner with quick feet, sudden deceleration and acceleration, hip mobility, sharp lateral cuts, and the hops 
to clear safeties aiming for his waist. He excels at making the first defender miss, even when it’s in the backfield. 
Love has NFL starter-caliber skills as a gap runner. His work with Duo and other interior tight-crease blocking could use 
more patience to set up second-level blocks. 
Although there are lapses with patience, Love has shown more than enough time’s that he will press within 1-2 steps of his 
blockers to set up creases. Once Love finds an open lane, he has good-to-great acceleration and elite speed to pull away 
from pursuit. 
He’s not just a speedster. Love wins against contact, often multiple points of contact during the same touch. 
Love bounces off hits at all three levels of the defense because he attacks collisions. A lot of backs don’t do this well 
anymore. 
61
players.pre_draft_advice:
For many, Love is the top prospect among the skill players in this class. Love is the most 
likely to have a high-ceiling impact as a rookie and to sustain starter production for consecutive years early in his career. 
If you’re looking at having the rights to a player for 8-10 years and generating starter production for most of them, 
Fernando Mendoza could have more value depending on the league. If you’re seeking 6-8 years with the most difference-
making talent relative to other skill positions, Carnell Tate is the best choice. 
Love and Tate are 2 of the first 3-5 picks in this class. If your team is a contender and needs one impactful piece right now, 
Love is the best choice. If you’re still building, Tate is the guy. Super Flex? Likely Mendoza.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Love is an explosive athlete with a remarkable blend of balance and burst to be dynamic as both a runner and in the passing game. He is one of the most talented players in the 2026 draft class and projects as a high-upside NFL weapon.

players.name: Fernando Mendoza
players.elevator_pitch:
Smart, aggressive, and an accurate downfield creator who can maneuver a pocket, Mendoza is one of the best 
quarterbacks I’ve scouted during the past 5-7 years. Mendoza and Caleb Williams have nearly identical grades although their playing styles, 
strengths, and weaknesses are different. 
Williams is an aspiring Aaron Rodgers in Rodgers’ prime. Mendoza is a mashup of Andrew Luck and Justin Herbert. 
For the casual football fan who follows a few college teams or reads college football previews during the summer, Mendoza seemingly came 
out of nowhere like his Indiana team to become one of the hottest commodities in the game. It gives Mendoza the vibe of a one-year 
wonder. 
Joe Burrow had this exact vibe from the public at LSU. Both players started with different teams the year before, but both teams lost 
enough games to avoid the limelight. Burrow lost three games at Ohio State, which is below expectations for one of the richest teams in 
talent and expectations in college football. 
Mendoza lost six games starting for the far less talented Cal Bears. Even so, all six losses were by a total margin of 17 points – five of those 
losses were by a margin of 9 points. I studied Mendoza at Cal and Indiana, and there’s no mistaking him for a one-year wonder. 
In addition to his ability to execute a passing game at a high level, Mendoza is an underrated runner who can move the chains with his 
acceleration, and he has enough speed to generate big plays. He’s not a breakaway runner, but he’s going to have runs where he’s gaining 
more than 5-15 yards. His burst and power through contact is underrated. 
Mendoza is a technically and conceptually sound quarterback who can play from center, Pistol, or shotgun. His drop game is advanced, and 
he has a full complement of play fakes. His range of accuracy and with good velocity is 55-60 yards. He can drive the deep post 56 yards 
from his release point – even with pressure bearing down. 
Mendoza is patient with unblocked edge pressure as he rolls toward it. He relies on good footwork to earn the separation and a passing 
lane. 
Mendoza is willing to take a hit to throw the ball downfield. He’s mobile enough to roll to the opposite side of a compressed pocket (but no 
pressure) after cycling through three covered reads – downfield, intermediate, and check-down – and finds a one-on-one to target – 
drawing penalties in the process. 
When unblocked edge pressure is heading where Mendoza is rolling, Mendoza decelerates well enough to create inches of separation that’s 
necessary for him to climb past and deliver a vertical-range accuracy that’s catchable at 28-30 yards to the boundary. 
He climbs quickly from bookend pressure and when there’s more pressure as he resets to fire, Mendoza is quick enough to flush and then 
break the pocket for a significant gain. He has climbed and rolled from pressure, reset, and delivered with deep accuracy. Climbing is 
Mendoza’s default reaction to pressure, which is an excellent trait even if he finds himself retreating after his first climb step due to 
secondary pressure in his path. 
Mendoza manipulates coverage with pump fakes, shoulder fakes, opening his body to one side of the field, and with his eyes. Mendoza will 
even take the extra step to manipulate the defense when considering a check-down. He’ll look at the shallow route, come of it to look 
deeper to hold the flat defender, and then target the shallow receiver. 
You’ll see Mendoza take unnecessary risks with situational game management or underestimate coverage like the rest of the prospects. 
What sets Mendoza apart from most is his ability to continue playing aggressive football while learning from the mistake. 
Some of this aggression is due to his offense forcing the issue. A lot of Mendoza’s bad decisions and sacks come on plays with long-
developing routes and no check-downs where he’s forced to buy time until the shortest route breaks open. You hope that with time and the 
right offense, Mendoza will develop the skill to adjust pre-snap and have built-in hots. 
I expect he will. As was the case with Williams, Mendoza is headed to a bad team. Don’t expect a rookie season like Justin Herbert’s. 
Mendoza will likely have a mistake-filled rookie year on a team with a bad offensive line and limited or still-developing skill talent. 
He’s good enough to survive the situation and the media onslaught that will question by Week 10 whether Mendoza even was good enough 
to be born onto this earth. As long as Tom Brady makes good enough decisions as part of the Raiders to build a good team around 
Mendoza, we should see him develop into a top-10 producer at the position with even better numbers if the front office improves the 
surrounding talent. 
Drops: Mendoza performs 1- , 2-, 3-, 5- and 7-step drops from shotgun and pistol. He’ll also set up without a drop, execute a half-roll and 
then reset and fire. He embeds a partial roll away from the pressure with his three-step drop. He does this when he spots blitzes or when he 
has a pass rusher facing mismatch and he wants to embed more time from the potential pressure. 
47
players.pre_draft_advice:
Mendoza is a top five selection in this draft, regardless of the format. He’s arguably the top selection in a 
Super Flex format. Like Caleb Williams, Mendoza can handle a bad organization for the first 1-2 years, muddle through it, and still grow 
from the experience.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Mendoza will need to prove himself with more full-field reads in the NFL, but his intuition, throwing accuracy and play confidence are superpowers and will smooth his transition to the next level. He projects as a mid-level NFL starter and a more mobile version of Bernie Kosar.

players.name: Carnell Tate
players.lateround_profile: There’s a lot of comfort with Carnell Tate. Just look at the first-round Ohio State wide receivers over the last five years: Chris Olave, Garrett Wilson, Jaxon Smith-Njigba, Marvin Harrison Jr., and Emeka Egbuka. It’s been an absolute pipeline. Tate has the traits to be a strong fantasy asset. His Breakout Score of 65.3 falls short of those Buckeye stars, but it still lands in the 86th percentile and ranked fourth- best in this year’s class. His career yards per route run finished top-10 in the class — impressive when you consider the target competition. As a freshman, he shared the field with Marvin Harrison Jr. and Emeka Egbuka. As a sophomore, it was Egbuka and Jeremiah Smith. And as a junior, he once again competed with Smith. There are no free lunches in that receiver room. Tate is also a relatively clean evaluation because he operated almost exclusively on the perimeter throughout college, running 88% of his routes from the outside. In an NFL environment that increasingly leans on heavier personnel groupings and multiple tight end sets, boundary receivers who can win outside are more likely to stay on the field as true every-down players. It’s not a flawless profile, though. Tate wasn’t a massive target earner in college, topping out at a 21.7% prorated target share in his best season, per PFF. He played with tougher competition, yes, but that figure ranked in the bottom half of this class. The saving grace is what he did with those opportunities. Tate was efficient — particularly on downfield looks — which helps soften the concern. His career contested catch rate was an impressive 68.8%, one of the better marks you’ll find in the database. Tate didn’t dominate volume, but he maximized the chances he got. The projected draft capital matters a ton here, too. Tate is tracking toward top-16 selection range — possibly even top-10. Since 2011, wide receivers drafted in the top half of Round 1 who played at least three seasons have averaged a best-season output of 13.9 PPR points per game in their first three years. When filtering for players with a Breakout Score above 60, that number jumps to 15.0. That’s a strong median outcome. Tate may not have quite the nuclear upside of some recent Ohio State stars, but analytically, this is a stable, high-probability profile.
players.elevator_pitch:
Carnell Tate might be a fake name. I have half the mind to call for an investigation of the Ohio State 
Athletic Department, because it wouldn't surprise me if Justin Jefferson went back to school and decided to play football on 
Saturdays under the stage name of "Carnell Tate." 
Carnell Tate and Justin Jefferson weigh 195 pounds. They run excellent routes, take a physical approach as run blockers, 
and make some of the most impressive catches you'll see. 
48
players.pre_draft_advice:
There are contingents who believe Tyson is the No. 1 receiver at this point of the year. 
Others believe it's Makai Lemon. Carnell Tate is my bet as the player most likely to see his stock rise during the offseason to 
challenge for that pole position. 
If you're drafting in a startup league, Tate would be my current choice for No. 1 overall. Fernando Mendoza is a compelling 
choice in Super-Flex formats. Jeremiyah Love makes sense if you’re one back away from playoff prominence that could lead 
to dominance.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Tate is a long, technically proficient receiver who can win at all three levels using

players.name: Jordyn Tyson
players.lateround_profile: The ZAP Model is in love with Jordyn Tyson. And once you open the hood, it’s easy to see why. Tyson began his collegiate career at Colorado, playing nine games and posting 22 catches for 470 yards and 4 touchdowns as a freshman. He then transferred to Arizona State, where he spent three seasons. He’s not an early-declare receiver, but that’s about the only minor nit to pick analytically. After an injury-impacted sophomore stretch, Tyson exploded as a junior in 2024. He caught 75 passes for 1,101 yards and 10 touchdowns, producing a prorated receiving yards per team pass attempt mark of 3.36. Adjusted for strength of schedule, that jumps to 3.58, the best top-season mark in this year’s class. Tyson won’t even be 22 years old on draft day, meaning he hit that production threshold at a young age. That translates to a Breakout Score of 95.1, the best among this year’s Combine invitees. That’s a 99th-percentile number. If Tyson lands as a top-20 selection — as currently projected — he’d join an exclusive group of nine ZAP Model wide receivers with a Breakout Score above 90 and top-20 draft capital: Mike Evans, Sammy Watkins, Amari Cooper, Corey Davis, CeeDee Lamb, Ja’Marr Chase, Chris Olave, Jaxon Smith-Njigba, and Malik Nabers. That’s serious company. And it doesn’t stop there. Tyson posted a top-five first downs per route run rate in the class, per PFF data, and his best season in yards per route run cleared 3.0 — a mark only seven Combine invitees reached. Aside from early-declare status, the one area where Tyson underperformed was missed tackles forced per reception — he was second-worst in the class. Since 2020, it’s been a mixed bag for players who ranked low within the metric. On the plus side, there’s Chris Olave and DeVonta Smith. On the not plus side, there’s Jalen Reagor and Jahan Dotson. It could be an issue for Tyson at the next level. Durability is also a huge concern. Tyson missed multiple games in each of his four collegiate seasons, adding a layer of risk. Teams may not want to invest heavily in him as a result — it’s been a huge talking point this draft season. But zoom out: Tyson is tagged as a Legendary Performer. Historically, players in that tier have delivered 18-plus PPR points per game in B2S at a 22.2% rate, and 84% have produced at least one WR2-or-better season within their first three years. Analytically speaking, Jordyn Tyson looks fantastic.
players.elevator_pitch:
Considered one of the best, if not the best, wide receiver prospects in this draft class among much of 
the fanbase and media, Tyson offers big-play vertical prowess, promising route skills, and flashy athletic ability at the catch 
point. 
There's a lot to like. Tyson is acrobatic, fluid, and a dynamic mover with the ball in his hands. 
76
players.pre_draft_advice:
Tyson is a first-round pick in rookie drafts. There’s a boom-bust volatility embedded into 
his grade relative to the receivers ranked ahead of him due to his injuries and more importantly his inconsistent execution 
of skills required of pro receivers. 
It’s most likely that any issues from Tyson’s bouts with lackluster technique won’t hold him back from becoming an 
impactful NFL receiver. However, it’s this lackluster work that could be an additional layer that potentially bogs down 
Tyson’s development path that the other players lack. 
Tyson will be WR1 on some boards. He’ll likely have a fair share of WR1 picks embedded in his ADP. I’d rather risk him at 
WR3 as my earliest pick. That’s my line of demarcation. Good luck determining yours.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Tyson’s injury history is concerning, but on the field, he is a sleek, twitched-up athlete who can create his own separation and win outside his frame at the catch point. He projects as an NFL starter (inside or outside), with a skill set that reminds me of Stefon Diggs.

players.name: Makai Lemon
players.lateround_profile: If you want a quick-and-dirty comp for Makai Lemon, think Amon-Ra St. Brown, another USC receiver. St. Brown is a popular stylistic comparison — both are technicians with elite short-area quickness and a relentless edge to their game. Like St. Brown, Lemon has that dog in him. Interestingly, St. Brown doesn’t surface as a direct comp in the ZAP Model, largely because Lemon’s analytical profile is actually stronger. And that’s saying something — St. Brown entered the league with a great résumé and a low-risk Draft Capital Delta tag. Lemon played three seasons at USC and improved every year. He caught just six passes as a freshman, but that jumped to 52 as a sophomore and 77 as a junior. His prorated receiving yards per team pass attempt hit 2.98 last season and climbed to 3.35 when adjusted for strength of schedule. That was the third-best top-season adjusted mark in the class. That production translated to a Breakout Score of 67.1, also third-best among Combine invitees. Lemon’s deployment is where things get interesting. The Trojans had Lemon run more than 75% of his routes from the slot in college. St. Brown was at 69%, per PFF. That deployment isn’t irrelevant. Over the last five years, Day 2 receivers with slot rates north of 70% have generally struggled. The first-rounders in that bucket, however — Jaylen Waddle, Treylon Burks, Kadarius Toney, Jaxon Smith-Njigba, and Emeka Egbuka — have had much better outcomes. And stylistically and statistically, Lemon looks nothing like Burks or Toney. There’s naturally a bit more volatility in a profile with heavy slot usage. But as was the case with Jaxon Smith-Njigba when he entered the league, draft capital tells the story. If a team invests in Lemon in Round 1 — possibly early Round 1 — they’re likely viewing him as more than just a slot specialist. And that projection isn’t blind optimism. Lemon actually posted the best career perimeter yards per route run rate among this year’s NFL Combine invites. In other words, he can win outside. Lemon is the real deal. Even if Nelson Agholor shows up as a top statistical comp.
players.elevator_pitch:
NFL Draft season -- and arguably the NFL Draft itself -- is a football beauty pageant that becomes 
hyper-focused on factors that aren't as important as the public, analysts, and teams make them. Some of you will argue 
that draft capital plays out and is an indicator of talent. 
Draft capital is a rigged game. Although considered a top, if not the top, wide receiver prospect in the 2026 NFL Draft, 
Makai Lemon does not fit the classic archetype of the NFL Draft season's beauty pageant contestant. 
53
players.pre_draft_advice:
I expect Makai Lemon will remain an early-round prospect through "draft season," but it 
won't shock me if his workout metrics don't thrill in the swimsuit competition of the Underwear Olympics. Harold Fannin Jr. 
was a beloved prospect until the Combine Accountants got a hold of his numbers and didn't understand the operational 
context of his skills. 
The Fannin love disappeared overnight, except for those who understood how to balance film with the metrics. Fast forward 
to today, and Fannin might be the best part of the Browns' lowly passing offense. 
If Makai Lemon's draft capital takes a hit, buy into the fear. You'll be happy you did.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Lemon doesn’t wow with his size or athletic profile, but he is a smooth, manipulative route runner and catches everything thrown his way. Similar in ways to fellow former Trojans receiver Amon-Ra St. Brown, he already plays like a pro and projects as an NFL starter (Z or slot).

players.name: Kevin Concepcion
players.lateround_profile: Jordyn Tyson, Carnell Tate, and Makai Lemon are getting most of the “Big 3” attention in this class, but don’t overlook KC Concepcion. Unlike the more prominent names, Concepcion brings legitimate gadget work to the table. And that’s not a knock. He spent two seasons at NC State before transferring to Texas A&M for his final year, and across those three seasons, he carried the ball 70 times for 431 yards. That production gives him a modest bump in the ZAP Model. And it’s not as though the receiving work was weak. Concepcion posted a 53.9 Breakout Score, a 76th percentile number and fifth-best in the class. The score isn’t higher because he never truly had a crazy season — his best mark in receiving yards per team pass attempt was 2.25, right around the class average. But he contributed early and consistently, which the model rewards. Concepcion isn’t huge, but he compensates with elite quickness and separation ability. Like Makai Lemon, Concepcion logged heavy slot usage throughout his collegiate journey — over 70% of his routes came from the slot. But, like Lemon, Concepcion’s yards per route run rate was actually better on the perimeter than inside. That trend became clearer after his transfer. According to PFF, Concepcion’s slot rate dropped from 93% and 82% at NC State to just 34% at Texas A&M in 2025. And his yards per route run peaked during that lower-slot season. You love to see it. There’s a chance an NFL team initially views Concepcion as a slot-heavy weapon. But the data suggests he’s capable of more than that. His movement skills and perimeter effectiveness hint at a broader deployment. If the draft capital works out — particularly if he lands solidly in Round 1 — there’s little reason to believe he can’t grow into a legitimate fantasy asset. Concepcion may not carry the same ceiling narrative as the “Big 3,” but analytically, most of the boxes are checked.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Concepcion must cut down on the drops and fine-tune areas of his game, but he is a versatile playmaker with an innate feel for getting open before the catch and creating after it. He has the receiving talent to become a starting slot or Z while also providing value as a punt returner.

players.name: Kenyon Sadiq
players.lateround_profile: Kenyon Sadiq is the consensus top tight end prospect in the 2026 class, and his NFL Combine performance solidified it — even with competition from the TE2, Eli Stowers. Tight ends as a whole tested lighter and faster than most classes at the Combine; even with that, Sadiq is pretty light — but also really fast. He makes up for his smaller frame by posting game-breaking athleticism that featured the fastest 40-yard dash ever for a tight end at the Combine (4.39 seconds), the second-best vertical leap (43.5 inches), and the third-best broad jump (133 inches). With such a highly touted prospect (his draft capital projection is trending toward the middle of the first round after the Combine), we need to consider nitpicking a bit. If we do that, we can point out that it’s not all green across the board for the Oregon Duck. Sadiq spent three seasons at Oregon after earning a four-star recruit status coming out of high school. He got off to a slow start in a crowded offense that featured Bucky Irving and Jordan James at running back, Troy Franklin and Tez Johnson at receiver, and Bo Nix under center. As a freshman, Sadiq caught just 5 passes for 24 yards. In 2024, with Irving and Franklin gone and Dillon Gabriel leading the offense, Sadiq’s role expanded; he was fifth on the Ducks in yards (308) and seventh in receptions (24). In 2025 — and playing with his third quarterback (Dante Moore) in as many years — Sadiq led the Ducks in receptions (51) and receiving touchdowns (8) and was second in yards (560). That’s growth, and once accounting for age, the Breakout Score is still great (86th percentile). Although Sadiq took three seasons to hit his stride, he had plenty of competition in college from future NFL prospects. He’s now a virtual lock to be drafted early, typically a great sign for tight ends who go on to be high-level NFL producers. The tape shows some good blocking ability, which should make the transition more seamless for Sadiq. Early draft capital and not being a blocking liability should raise the floor on Sadiq in the NFL. He shouldn’t be used as an inline tight end if teams want to maximize his receiving anyway, so as far as a fantasy football evaluation goes, Sadiq stands out from a lot of historical prospects. It would just be nice if his comparables were a little stronger — the profile has strong odds to hit, but we’ve seen some busts from this type of player before. JJuummpp TToo:: ZZAAPP MMooddeell || YYeeaarr 22 MMooddeell 110033
players.elevator_pitch:
Sadiq lit up the NFL Combine with speed, vertical leaping, and bench press performances. He’s an explosive athlete, 
which is what NFL teams seek as potential compensatory factors for a short and light move tight end. 
When you click on the highlights at the end of Sadiq’s scouting report you’ll see displays of speed and acrobatic catches. When Sadiq 
is playing to his potential, he can change the game with his big-play skills. He’s essentially a big vertical receiver who can draw 
mismatches from H-Back alignments. 
33
players.pre_draft_advice:
Pre-draft, I’d take a chance on Sadiq if I can get him in the late half of the second round in a 1.5 
PPR format. Ideally, I’d prefer the third, but that may not prove realistic due to the buzz surrounding his athletic ability. It might be 
best to avoid most tight ends until we see their post-draft landing spots and focus on receivers and backs.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Sadiq has mismatch-creating potential in the NFL, because of his ability to out-quick and out-tough defenders as both a pass catcher and blocker. He projects as a versatile, Vernon Davis-like weapon and has his best football ahead of him.

players.name: Omar Cooper Jr.
players.lateround_profile: Looking at Omar Cooper’s statistical comps gives you a good sense of the archetype. He’s a create-after-the-catch receiver who can make defenders miss. Among this year’s Combine invitees, Cooper ranks third-best in career missed tackles forced per reception. Notably, the two players ahead of him failed to record a Breakout Score. Cooper’s Breakout Score is solid, but not special at 41.7 — roughly the 54th percentile. That middling mark is largely due to the fact that he didn’t post a truly strong adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt season until 2025, his fourth and final year at Indiana. Non-receiving work is helping Cooper’s ZAP score, though. He shared the field with fellow Combine invite Elijah Sarratt, and he contributed on the ground as well, totaling 97 rushing yards and a pair of scores over his final two seasons. It’s a well-rounded profile, even if it lacks a truly dominant season. The Deebo Samuel comparison is particularly interesting. Samuel owns a thicker build, but analytically, they’re closer than you might think. Their Breakout Scores are just five points apart. Their career yards per route run marks were 2.47 (Cooper) and 2.43 (Samuel). Their first downs per route run rates were nearly identical. And their best adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt seasons were 2.63 and 2.64. The key difference was deployment. Samuel saw a lot more manufactured looks behind the line of scrimmage, and he was rarely a slot receiver at South Carolina, never topping a 20% slot rate in any season, per PFF. Cooper, meanwhile, finished his career with a 47.5% slot rate. That’s not disqualifying or anything. Cooper’s perimeter yards per route run was 2.40, actually 0.19 higher than Samuel’s collegiate mark. That outside efficiency ranks ninth- best in this class and fifth-best among wide receivers projected to go inside the top 100. There’s a lot to like here. Cooper brings the versatility the ZAP Model values, and even though he wasn’t deployed as a full-time boundary receiver, he showed he could handle it when asked. That gives us dynasty managers something to work with.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Cooper will be an attractive draft target for the NFL teams that put high value on ball skills and run-after-catch ability. He projects as an inside-outside receiver who can be weaponized when featured.

players.name: Denzel Boston
players.lateround_profile: Denzel Boston is a prototypical X receiver who spent four seasons at Washington, overlapping early in his career with Rome Odunze, Jalen McMillan, and Ja’Lynn Polk. Unsurprisingly, he produced very little during those first two years. Once that trio left, Boston emerged as one of Washington’s primary options. He posted 63 and 62 receptions over his final two seasons and scored 20 touchdowns in that span. On one hand, Boston’s Breakout Score was naturally suppressed by sharing the field with NFL-caliber wideouts early in his career. On the other hand, even after they departed, the production wasn’t overwhelming. In 2024, his receiving yards per team pass attempt mark was 2.00. Last year, it rose to 2.58 — 2.75 when adjusted for strength of schedule. That translated to a Breakout Score of 43.8, roughly the 60th percentile. That’s not bad. But for a possible first-round selection, it’s not pristine either. Within the ZAP Model database, among receivers who have played at least three NFL seasons, 12 were drafted in Round 1 with a Breakout Score between 30 and 60 — and Boston sits in the lower half of that band. That list includes Tavon Austin, Breshad Perriman, Kevin White, Phillip Dorsett, Josh Doctson, Laquon Treadwell, John Ross, Mike Williams, Calvin Ridley, Henry Ruggs, Kadarius Toney, and Zay Flowers. Not the best. You can reasonably grant Boston some leeway for the early-career target squeeze. But other advanced metrics are less forgiving. His career yards per route run, per PFF, was 2.02 — essentially average within this class. Since 2020, eight first-round wide receivers have entered the league with career yards per route runs below 2.20: Jalen Reagor, Jahan Dotson, Zay Flowers, Brian Thomas, Xavier Legette, Ricky Pearsall, Travis Hunter, and Matthew Golden. That’s not exactly elite company. All of this may sound more negative than it truly is. Boston isn’t far from the Elite Producer tier. In a slightly different career arc — with earlier opportunity — his Breakout Score likely climbs, and his ZAP score follows. That context matters. Boston could be legitimate. The size, the boundary skill set, the touchdown production — those traits will boost his draft stock. But analytically, he carries more volatility than some of the receivers ranked ahead of him. The upside is real. The range of outcomes just happens to be wide.
players.elevator_pitch:
Boston is a skilled boundary receiver with excellent acceleration for his size that gets his open early in 
a route and then he uses his size, physicality, and knowledge of positioning at (and after) the catch point to impose his will 
on defenders earn the sideline. Depending on how well Boston develops his route game, he could aspire to become a 
featured option like Nico Collins or he develops into a skilled secondary sidekick like Tee Higgins. The variable is how much 
Boston adds and improves to his promising game. 
115
players.pre_draft_advice:
A quality pick in the late half of the first or early second rounds in rookie drafts. Josh 
Cameron, Germie Bernard, Antonio Wiliams, and Chis Bell have higher ceilings, but Boston’s floor is safer. There’s a good 
argument that the only safer prospects ranked ahead of Boston are Carnell Tate and Makai Lemon. 
So, if you find that the RSP’s ranking is lower on Boston than the consensus, remember that Boston is in the same tier as 
WR6-WR13 and 3.5 points separate him from the WR4 on the board – which is the value of an upside bonus in the RSP’s 
Receiving category. If you want safety over upside and still get a high floor and high ceiling, I wouldn’t begrudge you 
considering Boston as your WR3-WR5.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Boston builds his speed with smooth, long strides and naturally adjusts with the hand strength of a ball winner, which should quickly earn him the trust of an NFL quarterback. With his ability to win inside or outside, his game has shades of 2025 Houston Texans draft pick Jayden Higgins.

players.name: Jadarian Price
players.lateround_profile: Jadarian Price is probably the most difficult analytical evaluation in this class. That’s what happens when you spend your career behind a likely first-round back. Price ruptured his Achilles as a freshman and didn’t record a touch. His second season was quiet, as you’d expect. In 2024 and 2025, he handled just 120 and 113 carries while Jeremiyah Love soaked up the majority of the workload. Across his entire college career, Price caught only 15 passes — a glaring red flag for fantasy translation. Price’s best-season prorated reception share was 2.8%. In ZAP Model history, 18 backs have fallen below the 3% threshold. None became fantasy-relevant. If you expand to backs below 5%, there are 46 of them — and only Derrick Henry produced a 10-plus PPR points-per-game season within his first three years. Draft capital could change the equation. If Price goes inside the top 100, he’d join only Henry, D’Onta Foreman, and Ty Davis-Price as backs with that level of investment and a sub-5% reception share. The problem? The two good to elite backs in that sample, Henry and Foreman, were dominant collegiate runners with strong adjusted total yards per team play marks. Price’s rate sits at 1.12 — well below that tier — in large part because he was a secondary option in his offense. The model does attempt to account for that via Teammate Score, but that adjustment is inherently imprecise. It’s much easier to evaluate a back who owned most of his backfield than one who shared it with a star. Price’s score gets a boost, but the range of outcomes around that boost is wide. There is a path. If the draft capital hits, Price becomes an outlier bet. And that’s where subjectivity can reasonably enter the process. But the athletic profile complicates things. Price entered the Combine with the second- best career 15-plus-yard run rate among invites, suggesting real big-play juice. But at 203 pounds with a 4.49 40-yard dash, his Speed Score came in at 99.9 — solid, not special. And here’s the historical hurdle: no back in the ZAP Model database has reached 10 PPR points per game in the NFL after posting a sub-105 Speed Score, an adjusted total yards per team play below 1.20 (Price: 1.12), and an adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt below 0.40 (Price: 0.30). There are contextual reasons for those numbers. But at some point, context stops being an explanation and starts being an excuse.
players.elevator_pitch:
The talented sidekick to a star player in a backfield often gets a pre-draft bump from media 
evaluators. Often, it has proven unwarranted. I don’t know if I have a good feel for the dynamics driving this bias, but Felix 
Jones, Roschon Johnson, and Sony Michel come to mind. 
Jadarian Price could join this list, although I’m inclined to believe Price will transcend it. I don’t take many liberties with the 
RSP’s grading system. There’s only one point separating RB2-RB6 on the board, and they are all in the same tier. 
There are projected improvements baked into the scoring system already, but I tend to underestimate the value of those 
likely improvements, because it’s my grading philosophy to make a player prove what he can do instead of presuming he 
will. 
In Price’s case, a player who is slated to have a higher NFL Draft capital than most of the names above him on the RSP’s 
board, I have no problem endorsing Price as one of your top three RBs. He’ll get an early opportunity due to the capital and 
that will facilitate experience and potentially quicker growth. 
Price is a scheme versatile runner who doesn’t take creative liberties that could hurt the offensive game script, but he is 
adept at finding cutback lanes, and he’s creative in space. 
Price has mobile hips, he adjusts stride length and pace to set up rushing lanes, he keeps his knees and feet high as he runs 
past low shots, and he drops his weight deep enough for strong deceleration. Pace also possesses curvilinear speed so he 
doesn’t have to make hard cuts with every run. 
Price must keep his feet tighter when setting up moves while tight to the line otherwise, he doesn’t operate from a place of 
balance, and it limits his skills. 
Price has the speed, acceleration, power, and ability to bounce off glancing shots like a starting NFL runner. 
Other than his occasional lapses with his wide stance, Price has all the tools necessary for him to develop into a high-end 
contributor. This includes blocking, routes, and catching the ball. All three have minor issues that need refining, but they 
aren’t fatal flaw on their own. 
If Price can add 10-15 pounds to his frame and either maintain or increase his speed, he’ll eventually start in the NFL. If 
Price doesn’t add the muscle, he could still deliver as a productive RB2 in an offense. 
Vision: Price reads the leverage of a double team and the unblocked end and sticks tight to the interior block to maximize 
his space away from the unblocked end. He identifies interior penetration as he’s taking the exchange and finds the bounce 
or cutback lane. 
Price sets up zone and gap blocking effectively. He finds cutback lanes between the tackles and hits the decisively. 
Price doesn’t try reversals of field, taking what the design and the defense gives him if there are no nearby cut back or 
bounce lanes. He’s creative in the open field and finds cutback lanes in the second and third level for big plays. 
Still, Price doesn’t take unnecessary risks and manages the game well. He identifies good leverage in dense traffic and hits 
the crease decisively. 
84
players.pre_draft_advice:
Although Price’s ranking is slightly lower than RBs 2-5, his ceiling is as high and his 
opportunity due to NFL Draft capital will be better. If you want to consider Price as a top three RB pick, I have no argument 
with you. He’ll likely find his way there in the RSP Post-Draft if he earns the draft capital and a good fit.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Price isn’t a proven workhorse, but he has NFL starting-caliber talent, with his natural instincts and contact balance. Ball-security issues and a lack of pass-catching production will be areas he needs to address during the draft process.

players.name: Ty Simpson
players.elevator_pitch:
Simpson has as much potential to become a good NFL starter as any quarterback from the 2025 class. He would have 
edged Cam Ward for the No. 1 spot if Simpson had this grade last year and declared for the draft. Simpson would have been QB3 for the 
2024 RSP, just a sliver ahead of Bo Nix. 
Simpson has a good arm, he maneuvers pressure well, and he follows up with strong accuracy in the short, intermediate, and often in the 
vertical ranges of the field after avoiding defenders. Other than some leaning through his weight transfer during releases that might impact 
his post routes, Simpson has clean enough mechanics to start in the NFL and refine them as he goes. 
Simpson’s arm strength is good enough to make all the throws one would reasonably expect from a starting quarterback. It’s not a plus-
arm, but it’s better than average. He can drive the ball 50-55 yards on the deep post with pinpoint accuracy. Beyond that, the accuracy and 
velocity become erratic. 
Simpson is a capable scrambler who can break the pocket and earn chain-moving gains. He’s agile enough to extend some of these gains 
for big plays. He’s not a breakaway threat, so we’re talking about 15-25 yards. 
Then NFL will likely regard Simpson as a first-year starter and co-starring with Fernando Mendoza on the draft-day billing. This reminds me 
of the 2009 NFL Draft when Mark Sanchez was the co-star with Matthew Stafford. 
Just as I thought Stafford was the clear No. 1 option in that QB class, there’s a healthy gap separating Mendoza from Simpson for me. The 
differentiating factors are Simpson’s issues with reading/processing coverage and situational issues with his pocket clock and reactions to 
pressure. 
51
players.pre_draft_advice:
Simpson is a second-round value in one-QB drafts and a mid-to-late first-round option in Super Flex. If 
Simpson goes in the NFL Draft before Mendoza or in the same range, he’ll be overpriced.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Simpson has the command and process of an NFL quarterback, but he needs valuable experience to be more efficient in his reads, and to better understand what is open and what isn’t. He projects as a low-level NFL starter, with the ceiling of a mid-level starter and floor of a backup (reminiscent of Daniel Jones with lesser physical traits).

players.name: Eli Stowers
players.lateround_profile: Eli Stowers made a statement at the NFL Combine. He ran a 4.51-second 40-yard dash, he broke the tight end record for the broad jump, and he broke the all-time record for the vertical leap. Already considered the TE2 in the class by most big boards, Stowers’ stock is on the rise after a showing like that, jumping up his pre-draft ZAP Score. But it’s not just athleticism in the profile. Far from it. In the current NCAA landscape, traditional career arcs are getting less common, but even with that, Stowers’ path needs some dissection. Especially for someone with this much spotlight on him. A track athlete (who won the 2019 Texas 6A high jump state title), Stowers was a four-star recruit…at quarterback. He began his college career at Texas A&M for two years without making an impact before transferring to New Mexico State and becoming a tight end for a different Aggies squad. Stowers racked up 35 catches and 366 yards as a receiver — and ran 28 times for 108 yards and 2 scores — in his first (and only) season with New Mexico State. He then transferred to Vanderbilt for two years, logging 111 receptions, 1,407 yards, and 9 touchdowns in his final two college seasons with Diego Pavia under center. Despite five college seasons and a position change, Stowers still had a solid Breakout Score — one that was slightly higher than Kenyon Sadiq’s — and will be drafted at an average age, as well. He added weight throughout his college career, growing from 215 pounds as a high school prospect to 235 in his final season, ultimately weighing in at 239 pounds at the NFL Combine. So, yes, it was quite an unconventional career trajectory, but the reality is that Stowers has a dominant mark in max age- and schedule-adjusted yards per team attempt, plus that Breakout Score we mentioned already. In terms of pure fantasy football upside, tight end is often dominated by elite athletes, and that’s how Stowers projects. Not every tight end with a similar profile to Stowers’ hits, but Stowers really checks every box. Undersized but incredibly athletic, Stowers is more of a “big receiver” and is most likely slated for a receiving-first role on Sundays, making him a fantasy-friendly option even if he might lose snaps in short-yardage, heavy-package formations. JJuummpp TToo:: ZZAAPP MMooddeell || YYeeaarr 22 MMooddeell 110044
players.elevator_pitch:
Formerly the No.12 high school quarterback in the country, Stowers had offers to play quarterback for UGA, LSU, 
Clemson, and Auburn. A knee injury during his last game as a high school junior began a series of events that changed his future. 
Stowers wound up at New Mexico State as their future quarterback. Instead, Stowers tore the labrum of his throwing arm in 2021. 
23
players.pre_draft_advice:
Stowers is technically an instant starter with his RSP grade, but he’s still on the cusp of a rotational 
talent. His landing spot will dictate if he’s an immediate impact player. He’s the safest move tight end on the board, but is 2026 the 
most dangerous year for the offensive trends to work away from the move tight end? Probably not, but 2-3 years from now, Stowers 
might find his production declining if these changes take root.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Stowers will need to prove himself as a serviceable blocker at the NFL level, but he is explosive as a pass catcher, and I love the way he maximizes his catch radius. He has mismatch-creating potential and can eventually develop into an NFL starter.

players.name: Chris Brazzell
players.lateround_profile: The Tennessee offense hasn’t been the easiest to evaluate under Josh Heupel, and we’ve got another wideout from the program in this class: Chris Brazzell. Brazzell stands 6’4”, 198 pounds — long, lean, and built to run. He used that frame to stress defenses vertically. Among wideouts in this class, he saw deep targets at a top-15 rate, and nearly 18% of his receptions came on those throws, a top-10 figure. His career average depth of target, per PFF, ranked top-five. The usage fits the archetype. Brazzell lined up in the slot on fewer than 7% of his collegiate snaps, the lowest rate in the class. He’s a true boundary receiver whose job was to stretch the field pretty often. From a ZAP Model standpoint, the inputs are solid but not insane. After starting at Tulane, Brazzell transferred to Tennessee in 2024. His real breakout came in his final season, when he first cleared a 2.0 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt mark. That resulted in a 45.7 Breakout Score, good for the 63rd percentile. We’ve seen this type hit before. Bigger-bodied vertical receivers taken on Day 2 in recent years — Nico Collins, Tee Higgins, George Pickens, Christian Watson — show there’s a real ceiling here. The catch is that most of those hits came with stronger efficiency profiles than Brazzell’s 2.09 career yards per route run. When you look around that threshold, you start getting into names like Denzel Mims or Cedric Tillman. That’s the tension with Brazzell. The upside is obvious. So is the volatility. And his comparables do a good job capturing that.
players.elevator_pitch:
And the winner of the “Who Looks the Most Like George Pickens Contest,” is… 
There are three receivers in this draft class whose physical dimensions and style of play are like the Cowboys’ new arrival in 
2025. Brazzell has the highest score of the three. 
While the comparison may be a superficial layer of information working in his favor, his stint at Tennessee raises doubts. 
Logo scouting is frowned upon, but it also becomes a thing when information meets the desire to simplify. 
82
players.pre_draft_advice:
Brazzell is a first-round fantasy selection in rookie drafts unless an unanticipated event 
changes his value.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Brazzell still has a long way to go before becoming a complete receiver, but he is a long-striding athlete and dangerous vertical threat with surprising savvy for his size. He has the talent to become a team’s No. 2 or No. 3 option.

players.name: Elijah Sarratt
players.lateround_profile: It’s no surprise we’re seeing Indiana wide receivers in this draft class given what that program has built over the last couple of seasons. Elijah Sarratt followed Curt Cignetti from James Madison to Indiana, finishing his career there, but his climb started well before that. A zero-star recruit, Sarratt began at Saint Francis before breaking out with an 82-catch sophomore season at James Madison. He then transferred to Indiana and posted 53 receptions for 957 yards and 8 touchdowns in 2024. That season marked Sarratt’s best adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt, but the bigger takeaway is consistency. Across his three non–Saint Francis seasons, he posted marks between 2.58 and 2.75. He’s one of only three wide receivers in this class with multiple adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt seasons above 2.5 — and the only one to do it three times. The ZAP Model doesn’t fully reward that steadiness, as Sarratt lands with a 51.0 Breakout Score (71st percentile), but the broader profile is strong. Sarratt posted a 2.56 career yards per route run (top-six in the class) and gained a first down on 12.5% of his routes, trailing only Makai Lemon. There is some nuance, though. Reception Perception’s Matt Harmon has flagged separation concerns, and the data offers mild support: over 28% of Sarratt’s career targets were contested, per PFF. That’s not disqualifying — especially for a bigger-bodied receiver — but it does introduce volatility. Among Day 2 wideouts since 2020 with contested target rates above 25%, the range of outcomes includes Tee Higgins and Nico Collins on the high end, and players like Denzel Mims, Terrace Marshall, and Keon Coleman on the lower one. Alec Pierce and Michael Wilson have settled somewhere in between, but keep in mind that it took time for them to get there. The lack of separation, however, could be why we’re seeing Michael Thomas and Pat Bryant as top comps, two players who moved to more slot work in the NFL. The ZAP Model views Sarratt favorably overall. Analytically, he may be the most intriguing bigger-bodied receiver outside of Round 1 in this class. That doesn’t eliminate the variance. It just frames it.
players.elevator_pitch:
Sarratt is a well-built possession receiver who can deliver plus-yardage plays with the help of play-
action, double moves, or constraint plays that fool the tendencies of a coverage thanks to good scouting and coaching. 
Sarratt’s style of play has some aspirational comparisons to Cardinals’ receiver Michael Wilson. 
Sarratt is a more reliable pass-catcher than Wilson was at this stage of his career, but it was because Wilson’s technique 
was still a work in progress. This led to higher highs and lower lows for Wison as he was still in the early stages of 
addressing the issue with a program he created and shared with the media as a senior. 
97
players.pre_draft_advice:
Sarratt may be the safest option by the RSP’s scoring criteria among WRs 10-16 on the 
board – and you could probably expand the range to WR23 – but there are several options ranked below Sarratt with higher 
ceilings. 
As you read through the rankings, you’ll see who I’ve noted in this way so you can make an educated decision whether to 
roll with the safest picks or take a slightly larger gamble on a player with a potential WR1 profile.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Sarratt will be saddled with the “possession receiver” label, but he is an underrated athlete and plays with the urgency and toughness to own the catch point — which translates to any level of football. He has the tools to develop into an NFL starter, either on the outside or as a “big slot.”

players.name: Jonah Coleman
players.lateround_profile: Jonah Coleman is a true do-it-all back. Coleman began his collegiate career at Arizona, logging over 200 combined carries in his freshman and sophomore seasons. After transferring to Washington for his final two years, he elevated his profile even further. Coleman peaked in total yards per team play as a junior, though that season featured a reception share under 8%. As a senior, he brought everything together, posting a similar yards-per-team-play mark while nearly doubling his prorated reception share to 13.2%. Stylistically, Coleman profiles as a powerful workhorse back. According to PFF, no running back in this class posted a better missed tackles forced per attempt rate. He also ranked top-five in yards after contact per attempt and earned one of the strongest pass-blocking grades among NFL Combine invitees. Some evaluators question his long speed, but his career explosive run rate sits above the class average. He’s not a pure burner, but he’s not devoid of big-play ability, either. If Coleman lands on Day 2, his outlook meaningfully improves. Since 2011, the ZAP Model database includes 39 running backs drafted after Round 1 but inside the top 100 who weighed at least 215 pounds. Of the 34 who’ve played at least three NFL seasons, 18 posted a best-season reception share above 10%. The other 16 did not. The higher-reception group outperformed their draft-capital-based expectation (via B2S) by 4.2 PPR points per game. The lower-reception group underperformed by 1.3 points per contest. Coleman’s senior-year receiving profile places him on the right side of that split — assuming the draft capital cooperates. And draft capital will matter here. If he hears his name called inside the top 100, you’re looking at a well above-average prospect with size, tackle-breaking ability, pass-blocking competence, and a growing receiving résumé. That’d be a profile worth betting on.
players.elevator_pitch:
Coleman is a nimble back with a low center of gravity. He’s a patient enough zone runner, reads 
leverage well, and finds lanes to bounce or cutback. 
Coleman also manages the game well as a ballcarrier, taking what the defense gives him and limiting his riskier decisions to 
down-and-distance and game-script scenarios that are open to creative license. He’s not as patient of a runner with Duo 
blocking as he is with zone. 
Once Coleman is past the line of scrimmage, he must leverage his size and get downhill. He’s prone to bouncing plays 
against outside pursuit slanting inside, thinking he’ll find openings against the grain of the defense’s flow. Coleman is 
neither that explosive nor agile enough for this ploy. He’s big and he should be sticking his foot in the ground and forcing 
the pursuit to hit him from angles that won’t bring Coleman to the ground as easily. 
Coleman has enough acceleration and speed to reach the open field, but linebackers routinely catch Coleman. What would 
be a field-flipping gain for many backs is a chain-moving gain for Coleman. What would be a breakaway run of 70-80 yards 
for his peers is a field-flipper for 35-45 yards for Coleman. 
If Coleman can prove more explosive from what I have observed, he could be worth the pre-draft buzz I’ve seen from him 
since last summer. Based on what I’ve seen, Coleman’s burst and speed are befitting an NFL’s RB3 or RB4. 
Combine this with pass protection that lacks sound technique at the point of attack and power and contact balance that’s 
contributor quality, and Coleman’s acclaim doesn’t match his film at this point. He’s a reserve runner who will get what’s 
earned and protect the ball who might develop his passing-down skills to become useful on the field. 
Vision: Coleman is a patient runner who works within 1-2 steps of his blocks. He presses advantageous leverage for the 
defender to set up the block to the favorable side for the blocker. He’s willing to press within a step of a defender in his face 
to set up a block. 
Coleman must do this more often with Duo. He abandons the press too soon and it doesn’t allow his other combo block to 
set before bounces or cuts back toward it. He leaves yards on the field in this situation. Overall, Coleman is a decisive Duo 
runner who reads interior penetration and finds the bounce or cutback lane. 
Coleman rides the wave well on zone runs, incrementally working his way inside-out to keep pursuit honest with their 
angles as he goes along his path. 
When Coleman reaches the flat, he tries to bounce outside flat defenders slanting inside, rather than sticking his foot 
downhill and having the leverage advantage to run through contact or even accelerate past it. In this respect, he can 
improve his decision-making with bounce-outs. 
Coleman reads leverage and penetration between the tackles and finds the bounce lane. He’s effective at setting up double 
teams on inside zone and hits the crease with confidence. He’s effective working with his pullers on sweeps—reading the 
heads of the defenders positioned against the blockers. 
159
players.pre_draft_advice: Monitor Coleman from afar despite your peers considering Coleman a draftable player.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Coleman falls short in terms of size and play strength but makes up for it

players.name: Chris Bell
players.lateround_profile: You’re going to hear this theme throughout the guide: big-bodied X receivers with average to above-average production profiles tend to bring variance. When they hit, they tilt leagues. When they miss, it’s rough. Chris Bell fits that archetype. At 222 pounds, he played almost exclusively on the boundary at Louisville and is projected to go as high as Round 2. His 48.2 Breakout Score is comfortably above average. Historically, that combination lives in boom-or-bust territory. In the ZAP Model database, 10 wide receivers drafted after Round 1 and before Pick 100 weighed at least 215 pounds and posted a Breakout Score between 40 and 60. The list includes Courtland Sutton, DK Metcalf, Michael Pittman, and Tee Higgins — but also Greg Little, Devin Funchess, Dorial Green-Beckham, Chase Claypool, and Jonathan Mingo. This is a go big or go home type of profile, folks. Bell spent all four collegiate seasons at Louisville, and his production climbed each year: 0.29, 1.00, 1.73, and 2.59 in receiving yards per team pass attempt. That steady progression — paired with a tougher senior-year schedule — is doing heavy lifting for his Breakout Score. There is an added wrinkle: Bell tore his ACL late in his senior campaign. That clouds his draft capital outlook and makes the evaluation a bit trickier. Given his size, it’s no surprise Bell lined up in the slot on just 8.9% of his routes. He’s a true boundary receiver. The concern is efficiency. His perimeter yards per route run was only slightly above the class average, and 26.6% of his targets were charted as contested by PFF, a top-five rate among Combine invites. That could reflect quarterback trust, but it may also hint at separation challenges. There are several big, high-variance perimeter bets in this class. Bell is firmly in that bucket. He’s not at the top of the archetype for me, but he’s certainly not at the bottom either.
players.elevator_pitch:
If there’s a player in this draft where I have equal fear about passing or drafting, Chris Bell is a strong 
candidate for that designation. He may be the only candidate I feel this way about this year. 
Bell’s game sears a hole into the film one moment and needs adult diapers the next. Sometimes these alternating thrills and 
horror are part of a learning process. After all, progress may appear more linear at times, but it never is. 
Add Bell’s surgically reconstructed ACL to the mix – a ligament he tore late in the 2025 season – and it’s just another layer 
to the internal drama surrounding Bell’s game, his potential, and his timeline to make an impact in the NFL. 
92
players.pre_draft_advice:
If Bell falls to the second round in fantasy drafts, he’s worth the risk. You’ll have to wait a 
year to see the athlete he was before his injury, so maintain realistic expectations – including a risk that Bell never returns 
to pre-injury form. This is rare with first-time ACL injuries. They are usually just a time-consuming rehab process.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Bell calls himself a “positionless player,” which is both intriguing and concerning for his projection. He needs to land in the right situation, with an NFL staff that sees his value as a catch-and-go weapon.

players.name: Mike Washington Jr.
players.lateround_profile: Mike Washington took the scenic route through college, playing five seasons across three programs. He opened at Buffalo, where he never cleared 150 carries in a season. After a year at New Mexico State — splitting work with 2026 classmate Seth McGowan — he finished at Arkansas. Volume was never overwhelming. Even at his peak, Washington didn’t reach 170 carries in a season, topping out at a 41% rush attempt share in 2025. What he did have that year was efficiency, averaging 6.4 yards per carry — comfortably the best mark of his career. At 223 pounds, Washington fits the bigger-back mold. That archetype doesn’t always bring receiving juice, but his pass-game profile is at least acceptable. He posted a 12.1% reception share in 2025 — a career high — though it translated to just a 0.67 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt rate. Fine. Not special. Some of the deeper metrics raise eyebrows. Despite his size, Washington ranked bottom-five in the class in missed tackles forced per attempt, per PFF. His 15-plus-yard run rate was also below the class average. Which makes what happened at the Combine all the more jarring. At 223 pounds, Washington ran a 4.33 40-yard dash. That gave him the second-best Speed Score in ZAP Model history. It was one of the more eye-popping testing performances we’ve seen from a back of that size, and it likely boosted his draft stock in a meaningful way. Historically, there isn’t a glaring red flag attached to backs who test fast but didn’t post elite explosive-run rates in college. The real hope with a profile like this is that the testing nudges draft capital upward. The model gives him a small bump for that weight- adjusted speed, but it’s just one input among many. We can’t draft solely off a 40 time. Still, in a class without a ton of obvious RB2 candidates, Washington’s Combine pushed him firmly into that conversation. The overall profile isn’t flawless, but it’s sturdy. And that’s not always the case with workout warriors.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Washington is a good-sized athlete who gets through the crease and can be a punisher behind his pads, although he will need to improve his third-down skills to increase his NFL value.

players.name: Antonio Williams
players.lateround_profile: Antonio Williams has kind of a weird production profile. As a freshman at Clemson, he caught 56 passes for 604 yards and 4 touchdowns. On the surface, that looks strong — especially since he led the team in receptions and yards. But Clemson threw the ball 480 times that season, which muted the impact. His receiving yards per team pass attempt was just 1.26, so the Breakout Score didn’t get much of a boost. Injuries limited him to five games as a sophomore. Then, over his final two seasons — again at Clemson — his receiving yards per team pass attempt never exceeded 1.70. In other words, Williams was consistently productive. He just never delivered a high-end season. That resulted in a 38.4 Breakout Score (48th percentile). Historically, that’s a tough needle to thread. Across the ZAP Model database, only seven wide receivers have posted a 12-plus PPR points-per-game season within their first three NFL campaigns after failing to record a max-season receiving yards per team pass attempt above 2.0. Williams topped out at 1.70. Lower the threshold to 1.80, and the list shrinks to Tyreek Hill, Hunter Renfrow, Terry McLaurin, and Ladd McConkey. Hill won with generational traits. Renfrow’s peak was short- lived. McConkey, ironically his top comp, had elite per-route efficiency that wasn’t fully reflected in his raw team-share numbers. McLaurin is simply an outlier. That context suggests Williams has an uphill analytical battle. There are positives. He offers versatility — 25 carries for 187 rushing yards during his Clemson career and more than 40 punt returns. That multifaceted usage hints at ways he can carve out a role early. The concern is role type. Williams ran nearly 80% of his routes from the slot in college. That type of deployment in the NFL would really limit his fantasy upside. Williams looks like the type of player who can help a team move the chains and contribute in multiple phases. We should question how that translates to fantasy football, though, unless he finds a team very willing to pepper him with looks. At least there’s a nice top comparable to keep us interested.
players.elevator_pitch:
Jayden Reed has been an RSP fave since he was playing at Michigan State. Williams compares well to 
Reed at the same points of their careers. 
Primarily a slot receiver, Williams has the speed, leaping ability, and skill after the catch to become a productive NFL flanker 
– perhaps a leading producer in his passing game if he continues refining his skills. 
Williams’ grade isn’t far from a designation of an immediate starter. He’s a reliable pass-catcher who wins the underneath 
zones and makes plays working across the field that leverages his skill as a ball carrier. 
101
players.pre_draft_advice:
Williams is worth consideration in the second round and a potential bargain in the third 
round. Depending on his NFL landing spot and draft capital, you could bump those valuations up by a round. 
104

players.name: Max Klare
players.lateround_profile: Many big board rankings (including the ZAP model) list Max Klare as the TE3, but there’s a sizable gap. Klare is generally regarded as a Round 3 pick, and while we’ve seen plenty of stellar fantasy tight ends taken in that range, he’s far from a surefire prospect with that level of draft capital (if that’s where he lands). Klare’s stock has risen dramatically over the last two seasons. As a redshirt freshman at Purdue, he logged 22 catches for 196 scoreless yards in five games before an ankle injury ended his season. The next year, as a redshirt sophomore, Klare posted a 51-catch, 685- yard, 4-touchdown line, leading the team in all three categories. He transferred to Ohio State the following season and recorded 43 receptions for 448 yards and 2 touchdowns while catching passes from Julian Sayin. Klare’s market shares at Ohio State dropped noticeably. After serving as Purdue’s go- to option in 2024, he had to compete with Jeremiah Smith, Carnell Tate, and others — including another Combine-invited tight end, Will Kacmarek. Given the competition, the decline is understandable — Klare still finished as the third option behind Smith and Tate — but the usage dip is notable. Klare didn’t run the 40, but he projects as a passable athlete compared to historical Combine-invited tight ends, putting more emphasis on his receiving production to project his NFL outlook. In a class full of strong testers, he’s a bit of an outlier — not in a negative sense, but simply projecting as average athletically. Accounting for that, he still looks quite good, which helps explain why he stands out in the tight end model. He was also used out wide fairly often (13.4% wide rate, per PFF, in 2025 — highest among tight ends in the class). He’s likely a more capable receiver than he showed at Ohio State, though he’ll probably face strong target competition in most NFL landing spots. Perhaps not the highest-ceiling option, Klare profiles as a fairly high-floor player. His average depth of target and run-after-catch numbers are subpar, which isn’t surprising given his athletic profile and average frame (which also leaves some uncertainty about his blocking). Still, Klare has the highest Breakout Score in the class. It makes sense that he lands as the TE3. With slightly better-than-expected draft capital and the right role as a pass catcher, he draws some Zach Ertz vibes in the comparison model. JJuummpp TToo:: ZZAAPP MMooddeell || YYeeaarr 22 MMooddeell 110055
players.elevator_pitch:
A converted quarterback, there’s a lack of muscle memory with areas of Klare’s new position that should improve 
with experience, but it could limit his playing time early in his career. There are also questions about his size: Will he add muscle and 
will it make him more explosive or slow him down? 
Klare makes starter-worthy plays as a pass receiver, but he has lapses that could be due to lack of experience at the position. This 
could be a muscle memory problem that improves with practice reps and time. 
58
players.pre_draft_advice:
Klare has some boom-bust baked into his game because of his inconsistencies with his hands, 
limited size as a blocker, and how team fit could impact his opportunities. Is he his new team’s future TE1 or is he a talented TE2 
with limited volume in an offense that doesn’t prioritize him in the receiving game? 
60
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Klare has a low ceiling as a blocker and needs to continue adding refinement as a route runner, but his combination of athleticism, toughness and ball skills gives him a modern-day skill set. He projects as an F tight end, similar in ways to Dalton Kincaid.

players.name: Germie Bernard
players.lateround_profile: The ZAP Model likes Germie Bernard’s versatility. Last season at Alabama, he frequently aligned in the backfield, finishing with 18 carries for over 100 rushing yards. But Bernard didn’t spend all four collegiate seasons in Tuscaloosa. He began at Michigan State in 2022, catching just four passes as a freshman, before transferring to Washington in 2023. There, he joined a loaded receiver room featuring Rome Odunze, Jalen McMillan, Ja’Lynn Polk, and fellow 2026 Combine invite Denzel Boston. Impressively, Bernard carved out a role anyway. As a sophomore, he caught 34 passes from Michael Penix and added 13 carries for 43 yards and 2 scores. He out-targeted Boston 44 to 7 that season. Per PFF, Bernard posted a 1.90 yards per route run mark compared to Boston’s 0.89. After 2023, Bernard transferred again, finishing his career at Alabama. There, he cleared the 2.0 receiving yards per team pass attempt mark just once prior to strength-of-schedule adjustments. His Breakout Score settled at 40.4 — a 51st percentile figure. Solid, but not dominant. Given the variety of ways Bernard was deployed, it’s no surprise some of his touches were manufactured. That’s not inherently negative — coaches were clearly trying to get him the ball — but there’s risk baked into that archetype. Since 2020, 15 Day 2 wide receivers have seen 20% or more of their targets come from behind the line of scrimmage, like Bernard. The only clear hits (or projected hits) from that group are Rashee Rice, Wan’Dale Robinson, Ladd McConkey, and Luther Burden. The rest have largely disappointed. NFL Mock Draft Database projects Bernard as a Day 2 pick. Historically, receivers drafted in that range who receive the rushing bonus in the model average about one additional PPR point per game across their first three seasons compared to those who don’t. The concern lies in the efficiency. Bernard’s 1.90 career yards per route run is below the class average, and recent second- and third-round picks with sub-2.00 marks have struggled. Since 2020, Day 2 wide receivers in that bucket have underperformed their draft-capital-based B2S expectation by 1.6 PPR points per game. Receivers like Bernard often need the right ecosystem. They’re rarely plug-and-play dominators, but in creative offenses, they can become useful chess pieces. Bernard isn’t built on overwhelming production. But the versatility — and his ability to carve out roles in crowded rooms — keeps him somewhat intriguing. Though his comps leave a lot to be desired.
players.elevator_pitch:
I’m hesitant to place a receiver like Stevie Johnson or Robert Woods into a comparison spectrum for 
Germie Bernard without reminding readers that while a part of these comparisons is about talent, the point of the exercise 
is about style of play, and the players listed are checkpoints with how the player’s development path could evolve, plateau, 
or regress. 
I heard from a friend that NFL.com’s Lance Zierlein used Robert Woods as his comparison for Bernard. I know and respect 
Lance, and I understand how he would consider Woods because there are common elements between how Woods was used 
and the potential fit Bernard could have in an NFL offense. 
111
players.pre_draft_advice:
Bernard has a medium floor with a high ceiling – not an elite (top 3-5 at his position) 
ceiling. He’s a solid second- or third-round pick in rookie drafts this year.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Bernard has a well-rounded, pro-ready skill set, with the frame, ball skills and smooth athleticism to be a dependable target. He projects as a starting Z receiver and as an NFL team’s second- or third option.

players.name: Emmett Johnson
players.lateround_profile: When you look strictly at ZAP Model inputs, Emmett Johnson may have the strongest pure production profile of any running back in this class. The dude can catch. Johnson proved that over four seasons at Nebraska, hauling in 85 receptions across his final two years. That translated to a best-season prorated reception share of 19.2% and a peak adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt of 1.13 — both top-three marks in the class. For fantasy purposes, that’s huge. The question is what he brings as a runner. Among the 20 Combine-invited running backs this year, Johnson’s career yards after contact per attempt ranks third-worst, per PFF. His explosive run rate also sits in the bottom half of the class. On the surface, that suggests limited tackle-breaking and chunk-play upside. There’s some nuance, though. Per Rich Hribar of Sharp Football Analysis, Johnson was hit at or behind the line on a class-low 33.9% of his runs. And when he was hit at or behind the line, he gained yardage on a class-high 75.3% of those carries. That hints at some subtle efficiency that box-score metrics don’t fully capture. Johnson’s unlikely to be a big-play threat in the NFL, though — you can see that with his career explosive run rate. He also ran a 4.56 40-yard dash at 202 pounds, which led to a mediocre 93.4 Speed Score. But that doesn’t automatically disqualify him. Since 2011, 10 running backs weighing under 210 pounds have produced a 12-plus PPR points- per-game B2S after being drafted outside Round 2. Only 4 of those 10 had a Speed Score above 94.0. In other words, elite straight-line speed hasn’t exactly been a prerequisite for a back like Johnson. We’ve seen receiving-centric backs with modest athletic testing hit before — Kyren Williams, Bucky Irving, Aaron Jones. We’ve also seen it fail, of course, and it fails more often than not. But the range is wide. Realistically, Johnson likely projects as a third-down back in many offenses. But in the right ecosystem — where receiving usage turns into full-scope opportunity — there’s at least a path to something more. And in this running back class, that kind of path isn’t incredibly common.
players.elevator_pitch:
Emmett Johnson’s pre-draft trajectory reminds me of Harold Fannin’s. They gained acclaim during the 
season based on their on-field performances. 
There was legitimate excitement to see them perform at the NFL Combine. When they didn’t perform to expectations of the 
public, the excitement plummeted to the point that you could hear an echo out in the world if you broached their names. 
Last year, I detailed how Fannin’s “bad NFL Combine” was an overstated and overrated analysis point. Fannin had a strong 
rookie year and could be taking over a lead role in the Browns’ passing game. 
The gist of the analysis was about interpreting the tests, knowing how much weight to give them compared to other sources 
of information, and who else compared stylistically to Fannin in the NFL with similar production. 
Johnson is this year’s candidate for “The NFL Combine Threw a Red Herring into the Collective’s Draft Analysis.” 
Let’s begin with Johnson’s 4.56-second, 40-Yard Dash. It’s the same time as Aaron Jones and they had similar dimensions 
at that point in their careers. The number is fast enough. 
So is the 4.29-second, 20 Yard-Shuttle. A 4.30-second mark is the RSP’s minimum standard guideline for the Starter Tier. 
Both marks are in the Starter Tier, for whatever that’s worth when considering that the timing technology is either second- 
or third-tier technology with a high variation that makes it much less reliable than they want you to believe. 
These were underwhelming marks for Johnson because the media and draft culture have been brainwashed to overvalue 
Relative Athletic Score percentiles. RAS is an entertaining and informative resource. 
There’s value to knowing the percentile that an NFL Combine participant’s performance is relative to his peers. It 
unintentionally creates noise for the signal. 
That signal is knowing what the minimum expected times should be for each position based on physical dimensions, style of 
movement, and expected role (elite starter, starter, rotational contributor, situational contributor, reserve, and free agent. 
These factors provide valuable context well beyond RAS percentiles. When Johnson ran a 7.32-second, Three Cone Drill, the 
RAS Percentile Apostles of the Pseudo-Science Church of Oblong Money Ball that Bounces Too Funny for Predictability in 
Everything Baseball Does immediately cited the 32nd percentile like they were saying, “A-men,” from the pews of Lucas Oil 
Stadium. 
Let’s dig deeper than the good book, shall we? 
A Three-Cone Drill time commensurate with a starting RB in the RSP is seven seconds-flat or less. Here are backs who 
contributed at a high level with no better than Reserve Tier Three-Cone marks of 7.20 seconds. 
Kenneth Gainwell – 7.26 seconds 
• 
• Dalvin Cook – 7.27 seconds 
• 
• 
Samaje Perine - 7.25 seconds 
Jamaal Williams – 7.25 seconds 
65
players.pre_draft_advice:
Johnson might be available in the third or fourth rounds of rookie drafts ahead of the NFL 
Draft – maybe even the fifth round if the Apostles of the Oblong Money Ball poisoned the well. After the NFL Draft, there
