might be a rebound in his ADP that goes as high as the second round. 
The likely range is rounds 2-4, depending on the size of your rosters and round allotments. If I had to narrow it further, late 
second to mid-third.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Johnson is quick and balanced between the tackles to stack cuts, and his pass-catching savvy will be an asset at the next level, ideally for a zone scheme. He is an intriguing change-of-pace back who will bring versatility to an NFL backfield.

players.name: Zachariah Branch
players.lateround_profile: Zachariah Branch weighs just 177 pounds, so it’s no surprise that 77.5% of his routes came from the slot during his three-year collegiate career, the third-highest rate in this class. That deployment was consistent across stops. At USC, his slot rates were 74% and 79% during his two seasons played there. After transferring to Georgia for his final year, that number stayed at 79%. The speed and shiftiness pop immediately on film. That elusiveness is almost certainly driving his projected draft capital. But analytically, there are red flags. Branch’s production never truly got there. His raw receiving yards per team pass attempt failed to reach 2.0 in a season, and even when adjusted strength of schedule, he maxed out at 2.11. The result was a very ordinary 31.2 Breakout Score. The usage profile is even more concerning. An enormous 45.5% of Branch’s career targets came behind the line of scrimmage. That doesn’t mean he can’t grow into something more at the next level, but it does mean the projection requires a significant leap. Historically, that’s been dangerous territory. If Branch is selected inside the top 100 — as NFL Mock Draft Database currently projects — he’ll be the 11th player drafted in that range since 2020 with a career behind-the-line target rate above 30%. And the first to exceed 40%. Only two players in that bucket — Wan’Dale Robinson and Luther Burden — posted Breakout Scores above 75. The others? Lynn Bowden, Amari Rodgers, Anthony Schwartz, Kadarius Toney, Rondale Moore, Velus Jones, Tre Tucker, and Malachi Corley. Big yikes. A large driver of that is screen usage, with over 35% of Branch’s career yards came on screens. Among top-100 picks since 2020, only two wideouts saw over 30% of their career yardage total stem from screens: Malachi Corley and Anthony Schwartz. Bigger yikes. To put it bluntly, this archetype has burned teams before. If Branch receives strong draft capital and dynasty managers push him up rookie boards, it’ll be a tough sell. That’s especially true in an NFL that’s leaned into heavier personnel packages, reducing the standalone fantasy dominance of pure slot specialists. You can squint and see a Tyreek Hill-like prospect with Branch, but that’s not something we should ever bank on.
players.elevator_pitch:
Branch has a boom-bust profile embedded into his grade that requires an explanation. He’s explosive 
enough to eventually play inside or outside in the NFL. To do both, there’s some development Branch will need with his 
game. In fact, there’s additional development Branch will need to play either role at a full-time level. If he doesn’t handle 
his business here, his career will take the shape of Mecole Hardman’s as a special teams option who can deliver as an 
offensive gadget. 
The issue is Branch’s routes. They are good enough to get on the field, but not good enough remain on the field every 
down. 
124
players.pre_draft_advice:
Branch will have a volatile ADP range pre-draft. Post-draft, his draft capital could dictate 
how much of boom-bust risk he is. Leagues that offer points for returns mitigate some of the risk Branch’s profile offers. 
Despite this volatility, there aren’t any prospects ranked below Branch that I’d clearly take ahead of him. 
The candidates are Ja’Kobi Lane, Eric McAlister, and Bryce Lance. After the NFL Draft, Kevin Coleman might join this list.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Branch has dynamic speed and the linear twitch to threaten the defense before and after the catch, but there is a lot of good-faith projection required to envision him as more than a gadget player. He projects as a versatile slot receiver with return value (in the Rondale Moore mold).

players.name: Bryce Lance
players.lateround_profile: There’s no “bloodline” metric in the ZAP Model, which is probably a good thing for Bryce Lance, whose brother is former first-round pick Trey Lance. There is a Breakout Score, though. And Bryce Lance didn’t record one. Lance spent five years at North Dakota State, redshirting as a true freshman before primarily playing special teams as a redshirt freshman and sophomore. Through the 2023 season, he had caught just one collegiate pass. At a powerhouse FCS program, he didn’t see meaningful production until his fourth year on campus when he was past his 23rd birthday. Lance’s final two seasons were a different story, to be clear. He posted a 2.70 prorated receiving yards per team pass attempt mark in 2024 and followed that with a massive 4.00 rate in 2025. That’s where the model’s adjustments come in. Once strength of opponent is overlayed, that 4.00 drops below 2.50. And it stands as the only meaningful adjusted season of his career. With such a late spike, there’s no Breakout Score for Lance. Failing to register a Breakout Score isn’t rare. Failing to do so and still becoming fantasy relevant is. Among 100-plus wide receivers in the ZAP Model without a Breakout Score, only Hunter Renfrow, Tyreek Hill, and Terry McLaurin have posted a double-digit PPR points-per-game season within their first three years. Hill is a unicorn. McLaurin had strong draft capital. Renfrow’s peak was short-lived. Lance does have traits. At 6’3”, 204 pounds, he brings size, and his career 3.07 yards per route run ranks at the top of this class. But that number lacks context. Since 2020, wide receivers without a Breakout Score who entered the league with a career yards per route run above 2.0 — a much lower threshold than Lance’s — have produced zero hits out of eight players. Even expanding the pool to those with a Breakout Score of 30.0 or worse, Christian Watson — another North Dakota State product — is the only one to deliver even an eight-PPR-point-per-game season early in his career. Could the adjustments be too harsh? Possibly. We should be open to the idea that Lance gets good capital and is someone we’re fine with drafting, as long as his average draft position is reasonable. The size-speed combination is evident. But this is undeniably a late-breakout profile. And historically, that’s been a tough bet in fantasy football.
players.elevator_pitch:
Trey Lance’s younger brother has the promise to make a name for himself to the degree that media 
will either lead with, “Bryce Young’s older brother,” in a few years when introducing Trey, or they’ll use “the Lance 
brothers,” if Trey gets a second chance to emerge. 
Bryce, like Trey, has the physical skills of a top-notch NFL starter. He’s big, fast, quick, and explosive, which gives him a 
high ceiling as an outside receiver. 
146
players.pre_draft_advice:
Lance is a must-draft option for leagues with at least 35 roster spots. For smaller rosters 
with practice squads, Lance could be available in the late half of the third or the fourth round and worth stashing for year. 
There is a chance a team falls in love with Lance’s ceiling and selects him on Day Two of the NFL Draft. If this happens, 
Lance’s draft capital will likely force him into the lineup early. There will be heightened danger for Lance to fail for the 
reasons described above and the draft capital will likely raise Lance’s fantasy ADP. There will be safer options at this point, 
but I’d still recommend him as a consideration if you have the luxury to take a chance on his ceiling.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Lance is a good-sized athlete and

players.name: Ted Hurst
players.lateround_profile: Ted Hurst (not related to Limp Bizkit’s Fred Durst) joins Albert Wilson and Robert Davis as the only Georgia State wide receivers in the ZAP Model. While Hurst owns the best projected ZAP score of the trio, he also carries the weakest Breakout Score. That’s because the peak production never truly popped. He was steady across four seasons, but his best receiving yards per team pass attempt mark topped out at 2.14 — just 2.08 after strength-of-schedule adjustments. A 42.0 Breakout Score (54th percentile) is respectable, though it’s more solid than special. What stands out is in his usage. Hurst brings length and was deployed aggressively downfield during his collegiate career. His 28.6% career deep-ball target rate ranks top- 10 in this class, and more than 21% of his receptions came on deep throws, fourth-highest among Combine invitees. That naturally drove a top-10 average depth of target. The question is translation. Much of that production came against lower-tier competition. Historically, that archetype has struggled to fully convert. Since 2020, 22 non–first- round wide receivers entered the league with at least 20% of their receptions on deep balls and a 25%-plus deep target rate. Only one — Nico Collins — posted a 12-plus PPR points-per-game season within their first three years. Interestingly, Collins is one of Hurst’s top-three comparables, even though he weighed a little more at the NFL Combine. So the potential is definitely there. Hurst can find a role on a team, as his statistical comparables suggest. It wouldn’t surprise me at all if he’s a Day 2 pick given his size and the story you can tell. But we should at least be cautious about how this type of profile translates to the next level. Even with solid statistical comparables.
players.elevator_pitch:
Hurst’s development could go in two distinct directions. If he emphasizes precision, refines his routes, 
and adds a little muscle to his frame, Hurst could become an aspiring A.J. Green – a big-play primary receiver who can 
defeat press-man coverage and run the full route tree. 
If Hurst bulks up further and his route tree is limited, he could become a big-play possession option like Tee Higgins who 
isn’t asked to run as full of a route tree and wins with his size at the boundary. 
151
players.pre_draft_advice:
Hurst is worth considering as a mid-to-late pick in rookie drafts. The NFL will draft him 
and likely before Round Five. Consider him a patience play who might deliver an early taste of what he could eventually 
become at various points during his rookie year.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Hurst faces a steep learning curve versus NFL press and coverage traffic, but his physical traits and ball-tracking skills — especially as a downfield target — are exciting. He is a developmental X/Z receiver with down-the-road starting upside.

players.name: Nicholas Singleton
players.lateround_profile: The Senior Bowl is invaluable for getting to know prospects on a deeper level. Unfortunately, injuries still happen there. That’s what happened to Penn State’s Nick Singleton in January, when he broke his foot during practice. The injury could cause him to slide a bit on draft day. According to the ZAP Model, it probably shouldn’t. Among running backs invited to this year’s NFL Combine, Singleton ranks sixth in best- season prorated reception share, fifth in adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt, and sixth in adjusted total yards per team play. That, to reiterate, is out of 20 players. All of that’s even more impressive when you consider he shared a backfield with fellow 2026 draft hopeful Kaytron Allen for all four seasons at Penn State. Singleton was clearly the superior pass-catcher between the two, highlighted by a 41-catch season in 2024 compared to Allen’s 18 receptions that year. There are some non-model concerns. Per PFF, Singleton avoided tackles at a below-average rate in college. And despite flashing explosive athletic traits, his rate of generating 10-plus-yard runs was below the norm among Combine invitees. The traits are evident — the consistency just wasn’t always there. Still, the broader profile is encouraging. In ZAP Model history, only 13 drafted running backs past Pick 100 have entered the league with a prorated reception share above 12%, a best-season total yards per team play mark north of 1.6, and an adjusted best-season receiving yards per team pass attempt above 1.0. Singleton cleared all three thresholds comfortably, posting a 15% reception share, a 1.74 yards-per-team-play season, and a 1.07 receiving yards-per-team-pass-attempt mark. Of those 13 backs, 9 exceeded their draft-capital-based expectation (via B2S), and on average, the group outperformed expectation by 3.5 PPR points per game. This archetype has historically beaten expectation. It’s not a lock that Singleton will follow suit, but his production profile — particularly through the air — is stronger than the injury storyline might suggest.
players.elevator_pitch:
Singleton burst on the college football scene a few years ago as one of the top recruited backs in the 
country. By year’s end, Singleton and the less-heralded Quinshon Judkins were the marquee freshman performers. 
My former podcast co-host, Felix Sharpe, asked me to watch the two later in the year. I thought Judkins was a more 
powerful runner with twitchier movement. Still, I liked Singleton’s game and believed he deserved his early acclaim. 
Fast-forward to the present and Singleton played in a committee, his coach got fired, and he was paired with a quarterback 
who didn’t perform to his physical potential. This may contribute to production declines and lead draft analysts to be down 
on Singleton’s draft stock. 
Singleton isn’t flash, but he’s a smart, patient and scheme versatile runner. His footwork is well connected with what his 
eyes see and his brain processes. 
Singleton has strong acceleration and good speed. He’ll beat defensive backs – including some cornerbacks – into the 
secondary and hold them off to flip the field, if not breakaway for a long score. 
He’s a well-built runner who can pull through reaches from all three levels of defenders. When he encounters defenders in 
his path that he can’t avoid, he accelerates into the collision with good pad level. 
Whatever the angle of the collision, Singleton can bounce off the hits of linebackers. He forces opponents to gang tackle 
him. 
Singleton shines as an outlet receiver as well as a first read on screens and RPOs. There are lapses with clap attack when 
reaching wide for a throw as well as focus drops, but these are first-world problems for otherwise high-volume receivers. 
Singleton’s blocking is competent and there’s room to clean up details that will make him reliable for the NFL. 
Overall, Singleton has a versatile game. He’s a candidate to build on his college career and become a capable NFL starter. 
Vision: Singleton is patient with gap blocking, reads the leverage of defenders in the box, and finds the open hole—this 
includes cutbacks. When running a gap play to the perimeter, Singleton gives a brief press inside to set up the outside 
crease. 
Singleton is patient with zone blocking, reading the leverage of defenders on the play side and finding a cutback to set up 
with a press of a block that gets within 1-2 steps of the blocker and defender. He sets up double teams on the backside of 
his approach with a deep press, demonstrating an understanding of the play design. 
There are off tackle plays where the backside crease is coming open where he can continue to press outside 1-2 steps 
longer. 
Singleton identifies penetration as he approaches the exchange, and he avoids some of the contact so he can continue 
forward. He also identifies linebacker run blitzes between the tackles pre-snap. 
80
players.pre_draft_advice:
Before we see a landing spot in the NFL, consider Singleton a second- or third-round pick 
in rookie formats. Any value change will depend on his NFL Draft stock. He’s probably higher on the RSP’s board than those 
enamored with the likes of Mike Washington, Jr. and Le’Veon Moss.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Singleton can be a frustrating watch because of his inconsistent run discipline and vision, but he offers big-play potential with an exceptional blend of size and speed. From day one, his pass-catching and kick-return skills will expand the ways in which an NFL team can utilize his talent.

players.name: Jakobi Lane
players.elevator_pitch:
Lane can become primary receiver in the NFL – possibly a star receiver. Details of his game grew as 
the season progressed, including his releases from the line of scrimmage, savvy methods for setting up breaks, and flatter 
speed turns. 
Lane isn’t far from developing into an NFL starter. At the same time, there are details lacking with his game that could hold 
him back depending on his landing spot. 
137
players.pre_draft_advice:
Lane is a medium-floor, medium-ceiling option because there’s consistently a role for tall-
rangy ball-getters in offenses who can win at the boundary or in a window of zone coverage, but his man-to-man game may 
never emerge to a level where he becomes a clear No. 2 option in the team’s pecking order. 
Lane will likely be a top 12 pick at his position in many rookie drafts. I’m willing to stretch my stance and consider him 
among the top 15 pass-catchers, but I’d be more comfortable taking him among the top 24. Draft capital and landing spot 
could persuade me to value him higher than the top 15.

players.name: Malachi Fields
players.lateround_profile: The concern with Malachi Fields’ profile is simple: the production never truly arrived. Fields began his collegiate career at Virginia in 2021, catching just 11 passes as a true freshman before suffering a foot injury that limited him to one game as a sophomore. His junior season showed promise — 58 receptions, 811 yards, and 5 touchdowns — resulting in a solid 2.24 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt mark. Unfortunately, that was the peak. Fields played two more seasons — one at Virginia and one at Notre Dame — but neither delivered a stronger statistical campaign. For a player with five collegiate seasons, the lack of a serious breakout is notable. And that’s the crux of the issue. Fields brings rare size at 6’5”, 218 pounds, but his Breakout Score sits at a below- average 33.6. His best season in yards per route run was 2.20, ranking 34th among the 46 wide receivers in this class. His career mark was well below 2.0. If Fields is selected on Day 2, as projected, he’ll need to buck recent historical trends. Since 2020, only nine wide receivers drafted between Picks 33 and 100 posted a best-season yards per route run below 2.30. Fields falls short of that threshold, as you just read. That group includes the following: Van Jefferson, Josh Palmer, Jonathan Mingo, Keon Coleman, Adonai Mitchell, Ja’Lynn Polk, Luke McCaffrey, Savion Williams, and Isaac TeSlaa. There are a whole lot of draft busts in there. There’s size to dream on, but the body of work suggests he’ll need significant development to outkick the profile. An older breakout and the absence of a truly elite season leave little analytical margin for error. Ja’Kobi Lane • WR Ja’Kobi Lane is one of just five early-declare wide receivers in this year’s class, spending all three collegiate seasons at USC. After catching just seven passes in six games as a freshman, he increased that total to 43 and 49 over his next two seasons. The production profile, however, isn’t overly impressive. His 2024 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt mark was just 1.14, rising to a respectable 2.35 as a junior. That best-season figure still ranked outside the top-20 in the class. The result was a 35.9 Breakout Score — a below-average number within the ZAP Model database. To be fair, Lane shared the field with likely first-rounder Makai Lemon throughout his career, so Teammate Score is giving him a modest boost. But even accounting for that context, the résumé is fairly ordinary. His career yards per route run, per PFF, ranks 26th in the class. He was serviceable at generating first downs, but nothing in the production profile screams difference-maker. Lane’s draft buzz likely stems from the role he could play for an NFL team. He has rare length for a prototypical X receiver, and that body type remains in high demand. There are concerns, though. While long, Lane’s frame is leaner than you’d expect for a true contested-catch specialist — and that was a major part of his game in college. Among this year’s Combine invitees, Lane ranked first with a near 33% contested target rate. If he’s selected inside the top 100, as projected, he’d become just the fourth wideout since 2020 drafted in that range with a career contested target rate above 30%. The other three? Josh Palmer, Nico Collins, and Alec Pierce. That’s not bad company. But each of those receivers entered the league with stronger Breakout Scores and more mass to support their contested-catch profiles. That likely aided in their transition into the NFL. Lane isn’t a bad prospect. Early declaration plus physical tools always provide a path. But analytically, he profiles more as a bet on traits than on demonstrated production. And those bets tend to carry more variance than upside certainty.
players.elevator_pitch:
Relative Athletic Scores (RAS) are a fun and informative layer of information because we can see how 
players compare to each other athletically over a history timeline. It’s a great conversation starter and an entertaining 
reference point. 
This is catnip for beat reporters and feature writers. Give them information and let them weave them into something 
magical. 
Here’s the thing about RAS. They are based on the NFL Combine. 
59
players.pre_draft_advice: Fields is a top-10 rookie pick in most formats.

players.name: Kaytron Allen
players.lateround_profile: When you first look at Kaytron Allen’s production profile, the lack of receiving output immediately jumps out. His best-season prorated reception share was just 8.6%, sixth-worst in the class, and his adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt sat at 0.50 — also sixth-worst. Allen spent all four collegiate seasons at Penn State alongside fellow 2026 Combine invite Nick Singleton. Singleton absorbed more of the passing-game work, which naturally suppresses Allen’s receiving résumé in the model. Teammate Score helps offset that context, but the raw receiving numbers still matter. Stylistically, Allen was the thunder to Singleton’s lightning. He’s a bruising, downhill runner who posted a stronger missed tackles forced per attempt rate than his teammate. That said, his tackle-breaking numbers were fairly ordinary relative to the rest of this year’s class, and his career yards after contact per attempt was similarly average. When you combine grind-it-out rushing metrics with limited receiving involvement, the profile can look concerning. And historically, it often is. The ZAP Model database includes more than 170 running backs who entered the league with a sub-10% best-season prorated reception share and a peak adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt below 0.60 — Allen, again, sits at 8.6% and 0.50. Of that 170-plus group, only 7 managed to score 14 or more PPR points per game in one of their first three seasons: Alfred Morris, Andre Ellington, CJ Anderson, Carlos Hyde, Jordan Howard, Chris Carson, and Isiah Pacheco. This is where adjusted total yards per team play becomes important. Allen was highly productive overall — in fact, he’s Penn State’s all-time leading rusher. Despite the weaker receiving marks, he posted a 1.96 adjusted total yards per team play rate. Among those seven historical “hits,” only Jordan Howard and Carlos Hyde reached that level. Add in the fact that Allen operated alongside legitimate backfield competition throughout his career, and the profile doesn’t look quite as bleak as the receiving numbers alone might suggest. There are real red flags here — particularly for true RB1 upside. But if draft capital is there, Allen has a path to carving out early-down value with contingent ceiling. It’s just a narrower path than some of his peers in this class.
players.elevator_pitch:
Allen should be a stalwart RB3/RB4 on several rosters for as long as he stays healthy and maintains 
his athletic prowess. He could have some years as a committee back or even a low-end starter for teams in a desperate 
situation because he’s a powerful runner with good decision-making, agility, and reliable ball security. 
Allen is physical. He breaks tackles, bounces off hits, and pushes piles. His pad level can be late at the point of attack, and 
it costs him yardage he’d otherwise earn. 
Whether it’s gap or zone, Allen earns what’s blocked for him and finds bounce lanes and cutbacks when defenders fill the 
gaps. Allen can overreact with his bounces in short yardage when a more incremental move to the next available gap is 
wise. Otherwise, he’s a smart runner who rarely places his offense in a bad situation with an unforced error. 
Allen has mobile hips to make 90-degree changes of direction efficiently. He adjusts his stride and pace to set up blockers 
and pursuit. When in the open field, Allen can deliver combinations of moves to set up unblocked defenders. If there’s a 
move, Allen has it and he delivers it with competence and occasional dynamism. 
Speed and acceleration are the weaknesses of Allen’s ball carrying. They are good enough for him to make an active roster 
in the NFL and even contribute as an RB2 – at least on paper. 
The current reality of the NFL is that there’s a glut of RB talent. Most backs on depth charts in the league have starter-level 
acceleration and speed – not what most NFL Combine followers think starter-level explosion is, but the RSP’s minimum 
standards for a starter and a committee-level contributor. 
Allen’s speed and acceleration are closer to the minimum for a contributor, and not a starter. Most reserves are closer to the 
starter tier. 
Think Trey Sermon, who has a lot of starter-level skills, but his lack of explosiveness hindered his play and his opportunities 
in the NFL. Sermon’s top speed in 2020 at Ohio State was 21.7 mph. This should be closer to his average and not his 
outlier. His top five plays were between 20.2 mph and 21.7 mph. 
Kaytron Allen’s top speed was 20.6 mph and low range of his top five plays was 19.9 mph. Based on what I’ve seen on film 
Allen isn’t fast enough to become a starter in most systems. 
The rest of his game is good enough to make a roster, but he may fall out of favor at the end of a season once coaches 
trust the younger player with more explosion in his athletic profile. Then, Allen will be on the next town as the RB3/RB4 
insurance policy who can handle volume, hold onto the ball, and execute the scheme, but isn’t fast enough for their long-
term needs. 
Vision: Allen reads leverage well and presses creases within a step of his blockers to access cutback lanes between the 
tackles. 
Allen finds bounces after attacking interior rushing lanes that get clogged. He doesn’t arbitrarily bounce to the farthest lane, 
but he rides the wave to the nearest outside lane and works his way there incrementally. There are some short yardage 
120
players.pre_draft_advice:
Allen is a late-round selection in most leagues in case Allen plays faster than he did at 
Penn State. He’s a good all-around back, but doesn’t project to become a good fantasy contributor.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Allen gave his draft profile a boost at the Senior Bowl, and he shows the quickness and ball skills that will stand out during training camp.

players.name: Skyler Bell
players.lateround_profile: Skyler Bell played just one game as a freshman at Wisconsin, but he emerged in Year 2 with 30 receptions for 444 yards and 5 touchdowns. After a statistical dip as a junior, he transferred to UConn. The production improved, as you’d expect. Over his two seasons with the Huskies, Bell posted prorated receiving yards per team pass attempt marks of 2.17 and 2.82. The problem is that UConn’s strength of schedule pulled those numbers down after adjustment, and because Bell didn’t hit his peak until later in his career, his Breakout Score sits at a mediocre 22.2. Bell is maybe slightly undersized, but he played both inside and outside throughout his collegiate career. Interestingly, his perimeter yards per route run rate ended up a little higher than his slot mark. The 2025 season is doing a lot of work for his draft projection. That 2.82 receiving yards per team pass attempt figure ranked top-10 in this class, and his 3.13 yards per route run was a top-five best-season mark among Combine invites. Bell also has stuff going on beyond receiving in his profile. As a redshirt freshman at Wisconsin, he logged 13 carries for 160 yards. That usage didn’t follow him to UConn, but it hints at his athletic profile — something that shows up in a fringe top-10 yards- after-the-catch mark within the class. That said, he wasn’t particularly strong at forcing missed tackles, per PFF data. Zooming out, despite the low Breakout Score, there’s legitimate intrigue here. Bell can align across the formation and was targeted at multiple levels of the field. The hesitation comes from the adjustments and the age. It’s easier to post numbers at UConn than at Wisconsin, and Bell will be nearly 24 at draft time. That shouldn’t remove him from our consideration set, but historically, many of fantasy football’s true difference-makers enter the league younger. Bell has traits, a strong final season, and a good top comparable. Keep a close eye on him throughout the draft process.
players.elevator_pitch:
The prospect who is the exception to the rule is a dangerous game in the world of prospect evaluation. One 
of the common “exceptions to the rule” invoked with wide receiver prospects is the productive college player who exhibits 
lackluster technique as a pass-catcher. 
Good scouting involves projection. Determining when one is going too far with the projection or not far enough is the 
challenge. Skyler Bell is an excellent example of this challenge in 2026. 
70
players.pre_draft_advice:
If you want low-risk selections, Bell’s clap-attacking rules him out. If you agree with my 
logic surrounding his likelihood to become an exception to the rule, Bell has a WR2 floor and a WR1 ceiling that makes him 
a first-round rookie pick.

players.name: Deion Burks
players.lateround_profile: Projected draft capital is doing heavy lifting in Deion Burks’ profile, because the production doesn’t stand out. Burks spent three seasons at Purdue before transferring to Oklahoma for two more. Across that five-year career, his adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt never reached 2.0. He did manage a 34.6 Breakout Score, but that’s more middling than encouraging. The deeper efficiency numbers are even tougher. Burks posted a 1.35 career yards per route run rate, third-worst in this class. Since 2020, the ZAP Model has tracked 24 wide receivers entering the league with career yards per route run below 1.50. The only one to provide any meaningful fantasy production from that group is Josh Palmer. And are we even rostering him in dynasty at this point? Burks’ deployment adds another layer of uncertainty. In two of his seasons, he ran over 85% of his routes from the slot. In two others, he was below 25%. Given his smaller frame, the slot feels like the probable NFL home. That’s where things get even more concerning. His slot yards per route run sits at just 1.14 — bottom-six in this class. There’s not much analytical support here. If Burks sneaks into Day 2, that will be the primary argument in his favor. Otherwise, he profiles as a late-round rookie draft flier. And even then, there may be stronger bets on the board.
players.elevator_pitch:
Burks offers big plays from the slot and has potential to do work as a flanker in an NFL offense if he 
can refine his routes and shore up potentially problematic technical issues as a pass catcher. Burks’ skills as a runner are 
the strengths of his game. He should contribute early as a situational threat on offense and a return specialist in the kicking 
game. 
Burks has emerging skills as a receiver. He has a variety of release techniques to work past tight coverage at the line of 
scrimmage. Burks executes a pair of those techniques with the contrast between patience and suddenness required of good 
142
players.pre_draft_advice:
The safe route is to value Burks as a big-play slot receiver who will earn 500-700 yards 
and 3-5 touchdowns as his usual production during his peak. That production bye-week fantasy value, at best and not a 
worthwhile investment. 
If the NFL team drafting Burks labels him as flanker or a flanker/slot, Burks could earn 700-900 yards and 4-6 touchdowns 
in this role. That’s WR3-WR4 material and draftable for dynasty formats with at least 35-men rosters. 
If Burks becomes the flanker, he could earn 900-1,100 yards and 5-7 scores and he’s in demand in any fantasy format with 
two receivers as starters. It makes Burks a safer post-draft commodity than pre-draft option.

players.name: Garrett Nussmeier
players.elevator_pitch:
Nussmeier is the volatile boom-bust talent in this quarterback class. He’s mobile and has the arm talent to deliver targets 
with velocity that belies his height and weight. 
Nussmeier has an aggressive, play-making mindset. This gets him into trouble, but he plays through the mistakes and doesn’t hesitate to 
attack at the next available opportunity. 
The son of former NFL, AFL, and CFL quarterback Doug Nussmeier, who is a veteran offensive coach in the NFL, Garrett has some 
inconsistencies with drop plans that impact his timing and accuracy. There are claims that Brian Kelly is to blame for this and other woes 
from 2025. 
Kelly has a reputation for pressuring players to play when they are injured and that he makes Jon Voight’s character from Friday Night 
Lights look like Pete Carroll. There’s talk that Kelly cajoled Nussmeier into play with injuries that impacted his play and scape-goated the 
quarterback. 
If true, this explains some of the erratic play from Nussmeier. Even so, there are constants that span multiple seasons and much of what I 
studied was from 2024. 
Nussmeier can underestimate the periphery defender on targets outside the numbers and get too aggressive. He must at least manipulate 
these defenders. This isn’t a bad habit everywhere. Nussmeier has moments that are very good in the middle of the field, accounting for 
that defender with placement that protects the target and the receiver. 
The place where Nussmeier has the most lapses is with downfield throws of 20 yards and greater, and the target is outside the numbers, or 
the defender is playing 10 yards under the target in the passing lane. 
Nussmeier capable of varying and combining stride lengths, pacing, and maneuvers to create space in traffic so he can throw an accurate 
target. The problem is that he creates more problems with his release and placement that he’s forced to overcome. This is due to the lack of 
uniformity of his drops and setups from play to play. 
Talented, but erratic, Nussmeier has good accuracy and pocket skills for a player whose feet aren’t giving him the reliable foundation for 
him to process and execute cleanly at the highest level. If he can clean up the leaks that spring from his game, he could become a starter. 
Drops: Nussmeier has a three-step drop, but he has a short stutter as the first two steps that isn’t fluid and could impact the consistency 
with his timing because the three-step pattern is different each rep and doesn’t need to be. One is as described above, the next is a short 
step preceding two large hitches. After that, he uses crossover steps for a three-step drop that appear more uniform. 
This could be an indication that Nussmeier is in an offense where his drop plan isn’t linked tight enough to the routes. At least this was the 
case in 2024. 
His one- and two-step drops on short passes are uniform. His set up out of a read fake can be wider than optimal. 
Nussmeier has a five-step drop that’s uniform and he adjusts the path to roll right at the top of the drop when interior pressure breaks 
containment. 
He delivers a well-paced and defined seven-step drop from center with a play-action fake mid-drop. 
Nussmeier displays all the drops but throughout 2024, his footwork wasn’t uniform from one drop to the next of the same type. The step 
width often had some irregularity that hindered a smooth and uniform process. This impacted the width and uniformity of his setups. 
His feet are often too wide when he sets up and this leads to throws that should be pinpoint, but are either catchable with an awkward 
adjustment or inaccurate. 
Nussmeier has good accuracy and pocket skills for a player whose feet aren’t giving him the reliable foundation for him to process and 
execute cleanly at the highest level. 
Ball Security: Nussmeier keeps two hands on the ball in the pocket and when he moves within controlled footwork in the pocket. When he 
breaks the pocket, he initially carries the ball loose and wide from his frame. When he passes the line of scrimmage, he realizes he should 
be protecting the ball, tucking it close to his chest but still loose from his elbow. He only uses his right arm. 
Nussmeier has lost the ball while maneuvering away from pressure in the pocket. The defender strikes the ball thanks to Nussmeier’s loose 
security. This has happened in multiple games and in the black zone multiple times. 
Nussmeier flips the ball unnecessarily while holding it in the pocket and surveying the field. 
57
players.pre_draft_advice:
A mid-round value in rookie drafts whose value might increase if an NFL team falls in love with the pre-
injury version of Nussmeier from 2024 and takes the risk earlier. If you need a quarterback, Nussmeier is more likely a patience play, so 
don’t get too aggressive with him as your target.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Nussmeier is an aggressive competitor who can throw you into or out of games, but his arm talent and command are foundational traits that generally translate well to the NFL. He projects as a high-level backup and spot starter.

players.name: Oscar Delp
players.lateround_profile: Entering the NFL Combine, Oscar Delp was probably the name I was keeping the closest eye on among all tight ends. His projected 40 times and game tape indicate mismatch potential in the NFL. Delp neither ran the 40 at the Combine nor participated in any other tests, so we’ve got just the tape and projections to examine (though he looks like an NFL talent on film). Understandably, Delp had some target competition at Georgia. Brock Bowers and Ladd McConkey were there for his first two seasons, and Darnell Washington was fifth on the team in receptions in Delp’s freshman season. With that said, Delp’s stat line remained pretty similar in his final year with Bowers and McConkey (24 catches, 284 yards, and 3 scores) as it was without them in 2024 (21 for 248 and 4) and 2025 (20 for 261 and 1). This makes for a pretty conflicting prospect profile. Obviously, there’s pedigree in that Delp is a Georgia product, but he spent plenty of time blocking when on the field and was used primarily as an inline tight end in 2025. The receiving metrics and Breakout Score were less than ideal (not breaking the 30th percentile in either). There’s definitely enough with Delp on the high end to deserve some legitimate comps, and if he lands in the right spot, I think we’re looking at a player who outperforms his draft slot. His name has been falling across average draft boards, so while he was a name I was watching entering the NFL Combine, he’s still very much in the spotlight for a different reason. Nothing is set in stone, though, and there’s a chance Delp is even the second tight end off the board, which would signify how much a team believes in his traits. JJuummpp TToo:: ZZAAPP MMooddeell || YYeeaarr 22 MMooddeell 111100
players.elevator_pitch:
There’s a lot to like about Oscar Delp’s game, but I was hoping I’d love it. The reason that love falls short has to do 
with his receiving performances against contact. 
Delp can earn the ball against tight coverage. He can take a wrap to his frame while catching a target. When a defender delivers a 
substantial hit, Delp has too much difficulty hanging onto the ball. 
70
players.pre_draft_advice:
Consider Delp a worthwhile mid-to-late stash in your drafts depending on the scoring system. 
Injuries, playing behind Brock Bowers, system changes, and instability at quarterback are potential reasons why Delp didn’t emerge 
as Georgia expected when they recruited him. 
There’s a lot more refinement to Delp’s game than his lack of buzz indicates. If he can’t add muscle, it might only lead to Delp 
having a long career as a skilled backup, but the price will probably be right to find out.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Delp has an underwhelming college resume, but grading to the flashes shows an NFL player who can impact games as a receiver and blocker. He is a prime candidate to be a better pro than he was a college player.

players.name: Eric McAlister
players.lateround_profile: Texas Christian wide receivers haven’t exactly inspired confidence lately. Since 2020, we’ve seen Jalen Reagor, Derius Davis, Quentin Johnston, Savion Williams, and Jack Bech enter the league from there. There’s a very real chance none of them ultimately outperform their draft-capital-based B2S expectation. Eric McAlister could be next up in late April’s draft. Interestingly, though, he owns the second-best Breakout Score of that group, trailing only Quentin Johnston. And just barely — Johnston posted a 76.6, McAlister sits at 75.0. McAlister actually has the second-best Breakout Score in this entire class. That’s either exciting…or mildly terrifying. Breakout Score is predictive, especially in the earlier portions of the draft. But once you move into the Day 3 range, chaos tends to take over. Draft capital itself barely matters after Pick 170 or 180. McAlister’s Breakout Score is impressive. It would just be more impressive if he were projected inside the top 100 picks instead of Round 5 or 6. How did McAlister get there? Well, he spent two seasons at Boise State before transferring to TCU for his final two years. As a sophomore at Boise, he caught 47 passes for 873 yards and 5 touchdowns in just nine games. That prorated to a massive 3.56 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt (3.65 raw), which supercharged his Breakout Score. McAlister never quite reached that level again, but his final-season adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt of 2.78 was still strong — it would rank 10th in this class. Historically, Day 3 receivers with strong Breakout Scores have at least offered mild optimism. Among 230-plus wideouts drafted after Pick 100 from 2011 through 2023 (that gives them three-year NFL samples), only 25 posted a Breakout Score above 60. That group outperformed its draft-capital-based B2S expectation by 1.8 PPR points per game. The rest, predictably, underperformed. The odds of McAlister becoming a long-term fantasy staple are slim. Day 3 receivers always face steep odds. But if you’re taking swings late in rookie drafts, betting on strong underlying production is rarely the wrong way to do it.
players.elevator_pitch:
McAlister is a big play threat with excellent length, good speed, and an acrobatic knack for winning the 
football. He’s one of multiple prospects in this draft who fits along the George Pickens stylistic comparison spectrum. 
McAlister’s speed is deceptive because he’s a long strider, but he has smooth acceleration and he’ll force defensive backs to 
give chase for long swaths of the field if he’s allowed a runway for takeoff. For this to happen more often, McAlister must 
develop more nuance with his releases from the line of scrimmage. 
132
players.pre_draft_advice:
McAlister will likely have lower NFL Draft capital than his current skills and ceiling might 
suggest. His route refinement is close enough that I could have made an argument to score him among the top 10 receivers 
on the board. Ultimately, I prefer to give prospects credit for things they’ve earned, not what they appear on their way 
toward earning. 
Still, this is worthwhile information for your consideration when taking his pre-draft ranking into context. McAlister is worth 
a selection in the middle to late rounds of your averaged-size league of 20-25 roster spots.

players.name: Demond Claiborne
players.lateround_profile: Size could be an obstacle for Wake Forest’s Demond Claiborne. At 5’10”, 188 pounds, he handled a solid — but not dominant — workload over four seasons. His high-water mark came as a junior with 228 carries, the only time he cleared 200 attempts. Claiborne did flash some pass-game involvement, posting a 13.1% best-season reception share. But the impact wasn’t strong. He never exceeded a 0.63 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt mark, and with the layer of age and strength-of-schedule fixes, that number falls below the class average. The straight-line speed is real. He backed that up with a 4.37 40-yard dash at the Combine. The problem is that the big-play profile doesn’t fully match the testing. Per PFF, Claiborne’s collegiate explosive run rate was 12.3%. The average among this year’s Combine-invited backs is 13.8%. Over the last five years, the only sub-200-pound back with a collegiate explosive run rate below 15% to even reach 4.0 PPR points per game in a season is Kyren Williams. And Williams brought much stronger overall production to the table. Claiborne also failed to stand out in missed tackles forced or yards after contact per attempt. For a lighter back, you’d ideally want one of those areas to pop. The odds of Claiborne developing into a true bell-cow are slim, particularly since he never fully owned that type of role in college. The more realistic path is a complementary or committee role in an NFL backfield. And for this archetype, opportunity tends to require both patience and the right ecosystem — if it comes at all.
players.elevator_pitch:
Claiborne has some LeSean “Shady” McCoy to his game. Definitely not the weight, we’ll get to that 
soon enough. It’s Claiborne’s pace changes, peripheral vision, ability to flip his hips to create sudden and sharp escape 
angles. 
Shady often looked like he was walking a tightrope, a matador facing off a bull, or Oberon performing acrobatics against the 
Mountain. Claiborne possesses that similar sense of control and bravado at the razor’s edge. 
Claiborne changes of direction are notably quick because he’s skilled at playing with pacing. He presses slow and deep 
before entering the crease with breathtaking suddenness. 
There’s a legitimate feel for the flow of the defense. Whether that comes from studying box counts pre-snap, his peripheral 
vision, or advanced processing that comes from extensive experience, Claiborne has it. 
As daring as Claiborne’s runs can look, he’s also a wise decision-maker who takes what the defense gives him and doesn’t 
take risks in the red zone and other short-yardage situations. This may not show up early in Claiborne’s game in the NFL, 
because he may have to recalibrate his gauge when it comes to the speed and quickness of box defenders. 
Claiborne pairs head and shoulder fakes with footwork better than most backs I’ve watched in recent years. Watching 
Claiborne move with the football is like listening to a musician who can tell a lyrical story with impressive technique versus 
musician who shreds with technical patterns but hasn’t learned to make it as musical. 
While Claiborne may need to recalibrate his gauge for defensive pursuit as a mover, he has excellent speed and 
acceleration, so don’t expect a massive struggle. Clairborne turns the corner all three levels of the defense, including 
cornerbacks. He’ll hold off most defenders in the open field with a clean lane – or clean enough to weave his open-field 
magic. 
Claiborne’s power is good for his size, but that power is ordinary by RB standards. He’ll run through reaches, bounce off 
glancing shots and win some direction collision as the aggressor into contact. He can drag or push defenders when wrapped 
high. 
At his current weight, Claiborne doesn’t pull through wraps, and his contact balance is limited. If there’s an area that could 
limit Claiborne’s ceiling as a professional, it’s the 188-pound frame. 
There will be legitimate questions about how much of a workload Claiborne can handle each week. Will the league prove too 
punishing? 
Jahvid Best was a great young running back, but concussions forced him to retire after three years in the league. Best, a 
dynamic speedster, was 5’9”, 190 pounds. 
There are questions about Bucky Irving’s workload moving forward. The 5’10”, 195-pound runner couldn’t get healthy last 
year after suffering an injury that usually doesn’t take so long to heal. 
71
players.pre_draft_advice:
Claiborne is a second-round pick in rookie drafts regardless of roster size. His draft capital 
might not be as early as his skills suggest it should be, but he’s a strong bet for big plays and eventually a role as a 
backfield’s big-play co-star or dynamic lead. 
74
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Claiborne is a bursty athlete (both linearly and laterally) who can give defenders the slip with his violent cuts, although his size profile likely will limit his role and landing spots. He has the mismatch-creating athleticism and receiving upside to be a diet version of De’Von Achane.

players.name: Adam Randall
players.lateround_profile: We seem to get a wide receiver-to-running back convert in every draft class. This year, it’s Adam Randall. The key difference between Randall and recent movers like Brashard Smith and Tyrone Tracy is size. Randall checks in north of 230 pounds. In ZAP Model history, he’s just one of 11 running backs at that weight to post a best-season reception share above 10%. Many of the players in that bucket — Saquon Barkley, Leonard Fournette, Eddie Lacy, Jeremy Hill, James Conner, Najee Harris — carried strong draft capital. Of the ones who didn’t, the NFL returns have been minimal. And that’s where things get tricky. Randall’s story makes you want to buy in. Big-bodied former receiver. Positional versatility. Untapped upside. But the underlying data isn’t overly impressive. His reception share cleared 10%, but his best season in adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt was just 0.59, seventh-worst in the class. His adjusted total yards per team play ranked fifth-worst. Randall only spent one of his four collegiate seasons at Clemson playing running back, logging 168 carries in 2025. With that limited experience, we shouldn’t expect a polished analytical profile. And we didn’t get one. Despite the massive frame, Randall ranked below average in missed tackles forced per attempt within the class. Same story with yards after contact per attempt. For a player built like a power back, the power metrics don’t really separate. For Randall to hit, there’s a lot of projection involved. He did give us a strong 113 Speed Score, which was third best at the Combine. But betting on him still requires you to make an assumption of meaningful development as a true running back, which can be a tough ask in a dynasty environment. Randall’s size is rare. The production and efficiency? Less so. J’Mari Taylor • RB Back in 2020, J’Mari Taylor was a walk-on at North Carolina Central. He stayed there through 2024, steadily earning more work each season and eventually topping out with a 53% rush attempt share and an impressive 18.6% reception share in his final year. Taylor then transferred to Virginia for the 2025 season and continued to show pass- game ability, posting a 14.7% reception share against stronger competition. That’s an encouraging signal — the skill set translated upward in level. On the ground, there are some positives, too. Per PFF, Taylor ranks sixth in this class in career rushing yards after contact per attempt. Despite weighing just 199 pounds, he forced missed tackles at an above-average rate. He wasn’t facing SEC defenses every week at North Carolina Central, but the data still reflects functional strength and balance. One big concern for Taylor is age. He’ll be 24 at draft time after five collegiate seasons. Historically, backs entering the league at that age haven’t found sustained fantasy success in the ZAP Model database. There’s also the frame. At 199 pounds, he’s unlikely to project as a true three-down option in the NFL. But that shouldn’t be the expectation anyway. Taylor’s statistical comparables lean toward satellite backs — players who entered the league as passing-down complements with a path to expanded roles if things break right. As a likely Day 3 pick, landing spot will matter immensely. If he joins a backfield with an established early-down grinder who needs a receiving complement, Taylor could carve out that niche. The ceiling probably isn’t feature-back dominance. But there’s a plausible path to situational relevance in the right ecosystem.
players.elevator_pitch:
Big and fast for his size, Randall earns buzz for his potential to become an excellent professional. 
Former pros in the NFL Draft media have praised Randall’s studious nature and approach to the game. 
A mature and professional approach to football can get a player far. Randall has some road to travel if he’s going to 
graduate from an RB3/RB4 or practice squad talent to a starter in 2-3 years. 
While Randall has speed, he needs a long runway to build that speed. Some will cite Randall’s size will help him break 
tackles as he makes room on that runway, but without excellent acceleration, he’ll rarely reach top speed with the ball in his 
hands. 
Every great back from Jim Brown to the present has mentioned acceleration as the chief physical characteristic that matters 
for running backs. It’s a form of speed and power combined, and Randall lacks it. 
A former wide receiver, Randall would have been moved to tight end if he had strong receiving skills. He doesn’t. He’s late 
with getting his eyes to the quarterback out of his breaks, and his 3-step breaks on timing routes need 1-2 extra steps. 
Randall has good hands when the ball is on his frame. When forced to extend high or away from his frame, his hands are 
too wide for him to attack the ball with reliability. 
Randall’s size should be an asset against contact. Sometimes it is. His pad level needs work. On some runs, his pads are too 
high, and he gets caught with hits into his frame that he could have run through. On other runs, Randall brings his pads 
over his waist and loses balance before he even reaches the point of attack. 
Randall has adapted well to the decision-making required of a between-the-tackles runner. He’s patient, finds cutback 
opportunities, and reads leverage well. 
Randall could become an effective gap runner soon. With time, he could also do some damage with inside zone. Randall is 
also a capable blocker, but there are a lot of small flaws with his game that needs precise details that are lacking. 
If Randall fixes the blocking improves his routes, and gauges how to use his pads better, he could become a situational 
producer. If his acceleration is worse than I’ve graded, and it’s possible, he might never emerge as a viable option in an NFL 
backfield. 
Presuming that Randall will play quicker as he learns the position better, we could see Randall emerge as a “B” back in a 
committee in 2-3 years. 
Vision: Randall presses within a step of a puller to the puller’s outside shoulder to set up a cutback to the inside. He 
presses with a two-step move downhill after bouncing outside to set up a safety and then bounce outside him. 
Randall identifies cutback opportunities on gap plays, pressing to the play side of the puller, reading the leverage of the 
defender, and accessing the cutback lane. 
Randall can press within a step of the line on an inside run and bounce the play one gap over for a significant gain. 
142
players.pre_draft_advice:
Randall is worth a stash in deep leagues with practice squads – think at least 30 roster 
spots. If the NFL Draft capital is somehow strong (and absurd), Randall could be worth a pick in the final rounds if he lasts 
that long, just because he’ll get opportunities and it could translate to his learning curve being faster than expected. 
I’m monitoring from afar, but I’m trying to be open to the good possibilities.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Randall plays like more of an athlete than a natural running back, but he has the versatility that can be useful at the next level. If he develops in pass protection, he can earn a role as a third-down NFL back with special teams potential.

players.name: Brenen Thompson
players.lateround_profile: Brenen Thompson spent one year at Texas, two at Oklahoma, and one at Mississippi State, where he finished his college career. He didn’t eclipse 250 receiving yards in a season until that final campaign, when he broke out for 57 receptions, 1,054 yards, and 6 touchdowns. That’s the textbook definition of a late breakout. And it shows in a 35.4 Breakout Score, a 41st percentile mark. Size is the bigger concern for fantasy. At 5’9”, 164 pounds, Thompson falls into a bucket that historically hasn’t produced many true difference-makers. In the ZAP Model database, the only sub-180-pound wide receivers to post a 15-plus PPR points-per-game season within their first three years are first-rounders DeVonta Smith and Jordan Addison. Smaller receivers can still offer usable production — think Josh Downs or Darnell Mooney — but many of those mini-hits came with significantly stronger Breakout Scores than Thompson’s. That said, his size shouldn’t automatically remove him from consideration. Interestingly, he barely played in the slot in college, running just 12.2% of his routes there, per PFF. On only 86 slot routes, though, he posted a ridiculous 4.15 yards per route run, the highest mark in the class. It’s a small sample, but it suggests some untapped deployment flexibility. And then there’s the 4.26 speed. His ability to stress defenses vertically — Thompson posted the fifth-highest deep-ball target rate in the class — could boost his draft capital. And if that happens, his ZAP score will rise with it. NFL teams covet that type of field-stretching juice, and stronger investment increases the odds he earns early snaps. Just don’t let the athleticism cloud the bigger picture. As we know, teams often overdraft this kind of speed. Thompson is intriguing — his comps are actually pretty fun, all things considered — but the overall profile still calls for tempered expectations.
players.elevator_pitch:
Thompson has elite speed, good route breaks that should get better, and sound hands with only rare 
technical glitches. Wrap Thompson in the open field and he’s done, but that’s the trick that is difficult for defenders to figure 
out because of his stop-start agility, decision-making, quickness, and speed with the ball in his hands. 
196
players.pre_draft_advice:
Monitor Thompson from afar in all but the largest leagues – 50-plus roster spots and 
practice squad slots – unless return specialists have value in your scoring system.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Thompson is undersized and not built for traffic but offers the score-from-anywhere type of speed to be a dynamic contributor in the quick game or downfield. If he stays healthy, he has upside as a No. 3 WR for an NFL offense looking for his specific skill set.

players.name: Malik Benson
players.lateround_profile: Malik Benson can absolutely run. The former track athlete clocked a 4.37 40 at the Combine, and when you watch him, the vertical juice is obvious. He’s built to threaten defenses over the top. The path to this point, though, has been anything but linear. Benson spent two seasons in JUCO before landing at Alabama in 2023, where he caught just 13 passes across 14 games. That led to a transfer to Florida State, where he managed only 25 receptions. He then finished his career at Oregon in 2025, his fourth college stop. That final season was technically his most productive, but even then, his adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt peaked at just 1.88. That’s a bottom-10 mark in this class. Deployment helps explain some of it. Over his final three non-JUCO seasons, Benson posted a 25.6% deep-ball target rate, 11th-highest in the class. He was used as a field- stretcher. The problem is that he was rarely featured. He never reached even a 15% prorated target share in any season, the third-worst rate among Combine invite wide receivers. So what we’re looking at is a 23-and-a-half-year-old prospect with plus speed, minimal target-earning ability, and no season that truly stands out from a production standpoint. The athleticism gives him a puncher’s chance. But analytically, this is a thin profile. It’s tough to get overly optimistic without meaningful draft capital to force opportunity.
players.elevator_pitch:
Benson is a speedster capable of stretching a defense in the college game, but he’ll need to refine his 
release tools to bait NFL cornerbacks enough to win up the boundary like he does at Oregon. At this point, he has one 
reliable move that he performs with the refinement capable of doing the job against tight man coverage. 
Fortunately, there is more to Benson’s game than winning go routes and fades. He has some skill with setups midway 
through his stems to work free over the middle, but he must cut down on lapses that tip off his intentions to the safety 
playing over the top. 
155
players.pre_draft_advice:
Benson is worth consideration as a late-round pick you intend on stashing for at least a 
year on a practice squad or at the end of a loaded roster where one spot taken with a seldom-used player is possible. 
Otherwise, monitor Benson from afar unless returns are part of your league’s scoring system and Benson is slated for this 
duty.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Benson is undeveloped in the quick game and intermediate area, but his gliding speed and tracking skills will be valued by teams looking for a pure vertical receiver on the perimeter. His usefulness will also show on punt returns.

players.name: Taylen Green
players.elevator_pitch:
If you’re not going to follow the RSP’s strategic approach to rookie quarterbacks as a fantasy GM or you’re in NFL personnel 
reading this material as a cross check of information, Green and Carson Beck – QB6 and QB7 on the RSP’s board – are realistically closer to 
QB4 and QB5 when accounting for factors other than on-field performance. 
The NFL evaluation community hasn’t been shy about leaking Diego Pavia’s exploits. Pavia is central casting’s pick to reprise Shane Falco 
with a bad-boy edge in “The Replacements.” If that doesn’t pan out, Pavia could be the QB of the dirty rival team in a Will Ferrell football 
sendup in a similar vein of “Talladega Nights.” 
Pavia has pissed away, literally, the NFL’s consideration of him as anything more than a late-round pick. It’s more than just the incident 
linked to here, but this incident is allegedly indicative of what they fear about Pavia. 
There are questions about Cade Klubnik’s velocity and Dabo Sweeney’s Clemson offenses being too basic. These factors will also limit 
Klubnik’s draft capital. 
It means Green and Beck will be included among the first or second tier of quarterbacks that the NFL will consider in April. Beck, as you’ll 
read, will likely earn value for what he already has to offer while hoping the immediate experience will season his game. 
Green’s value is a long-term bet on high-ceiling physical skills as he develops and/or refines the techniques and concepts of the position. 
Any quarterback that scores at least a 75 on the RSP’s Depth of Talent Criteria has the skills to develop into a starting quarterback. 
Green operates well from Pistol because the alignment supports a stronger ground game than shotgun while also leveraging a quarterback’s 
rushing ability from spread concepts. Green already possesses a sound play-action game with thorough and detailed fakes. 
These fakes can slow the defense and allow Green to assess whether he keeps the ball. When he does, he’s a dynamic runner. 
Green drops his pads into contact, bounces off glancing shots to his frame and keeps his feet moving to extend forward after the contact. 
Green beats unblocked defensive ends and middle linebackers around the far-side corner on a keeper. He has also shown that he can slide, 
but he should do it more often. 
Green can transition downhill with one step or use tight curvilinear bend around the short-side end. He hurdles defenders when he can earn 
that final meaningful bit of space to cross the marker or pylon. 
Green also manipulates pursuit with good footwork. His burst and speed help him turn the corner on perimeter runs, split defenders up the 
middle, and extend the runs into open space. He can break away from pursuit for field-flipping runs as well as full-field gallops of more than 
60 yards. 
We’ve seen this combination of play-action and running work as a basis for an offense when Jalen Hurts, Lamar Jackson, and Colin 
Kaepernick were the quarterbacks. Green must develop and refine the rest of his game to emerge as a starter. 
Green’s drops need refinement. He reaches a balanced stance, but there’s a lot of wasted movement that disrupts efficient flow from the 
snap to the setup as he’s trying to read the field. 
He’s off to a good start with his release mechanics, but he has lapses where he doesn’t point his front toe to the target and the ball travels 
closer to where his toe is facing. His arm motion is compact and he gets weight transfer, so velocity, balance, and stroke are all good 
enough as starting points for additional refinement as his QB coach will deem necessary. 
There is no question Green has arm talent. He also has moments of strong accuracy at NFL distances. There are strong examples of Green 
layering the ball over defenders with pinpoint accuracy in the intermediate and vertical ranges of the field. 
Green holds flat defenders and safeties in various areas of the field depending on the coverage. Green can cycle though a few reads against 
coverages like C3 and C2 and discern what’s covered before going off-script and finding an open option. He targets receivers in sync with 
the progression and route. 
If you’re an optimist, you can characterize Green as being on his way to developing more advanced quarterback skills that will serve him as 
a future NFL starter. Much of what’s left to fix are common ills with prospects: 
Predetermining check-downs when greater awareness and patience would have led to a better decision. 
Failing to account for coverage on the periphery of the targeted receiver. 
• 
• 
• Greater selectivity with targets based on his throwing stance, pressure, and field position. 
• 
• 
• 
• 
Poor judgment with the leverage safeties are playing over the receiver. 
Poor judgment with the leverage corners are playing while trailing the receiver. 
Taking unnecessary risks with low-odds efforts when in the black zone. 
Checking down more often. 
74
players.pre_draft_advice:
If you’re in a Super Flex format or league with big rosters, Green is a second-tier QB after Mendoza who is 
worth stashing on a practice squad for the potential upside.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Green needs to take better care of the football and refine his mechanics and passing rhythm, but he offers the physical traits and high character worth molding in a read-option, vertical passing game.

players.name: Cole Payton
players.elevator_pitch:
Payton is an enticing athlete who possesses good speed and strong acceleration. He hurdles defenders in the open field, 
and he has the size to work through contact. 
He’ll draw attention from the Robo-QB niche of NFL executives who inadvertently give double credit to athletic ability. There’s a lot of work 
ahead. 
Payton’s drops and releases lack precision and efficiency. His ball security exposes the team to turnovers. 
Payton identifies pressure pre-snap and finds quick solutions, but he has lapses where he overreacts to pressure and climbs pockets when 
unnecessary. He places himself in harm’s way when this happens. Otherwise, he has a good pocket clock. 
Payton retreats from interior pressure and then doubts his decision mid-escape. He has plays where he stops a rollout as if he might reverse 
field or square and throw, but he then continues a full roll to the far-side boundary. Hopefully, Payton doesn’t try this against NFL defenders 
with better quickness and angles of pursuit. 
Payton must become more selective with the maneuvers he chooses for the pressure that arrives. He chooses maneuvers that lead him into 
pressure he could have otherwise avoided. 
Payton cycles through 2-3 reads and breaks the pocket if he doesn’t target a receiver. He understand how to use his legs to reshape an 
area of zone that’s dense with coverage, stretching the window, and making the against-the-grain throw to the open receiver. He’ll also 
check the ball after exhausting downfield options. 
Payton is willing to get aggressive when targeting a receiver between two defenders, placing the ball away from the defenders. He also 
places the ball away from dropping linebackers, throwing the receiver open with pinpoint placement to adjust away from the adjacent 
coverage. 
Payton has more difficulty IDing peripheral coverage above the target and peeling off a receiver to attack downhill on the intended option. 
Still, he does this well against safeties on short slants. He’s also good at delivering a vertical route to a receiver breaking under a safety 
who is showing his back to the QB. 
Payton displays good anticipation in the quick game versus pressure. He can set up with one step and fire into a tight passing lane due to 
pressure. 
The upper limit of his accuracy is 47 yards (catchable, not pinpoint) to the middle of the field. He lacks a strong arm, and it may prove too 
questionable for him to become a starter if he cleans up his technical skills and refines the rest of his game. 
There’s talk Payton could be the third quarterback off the board. This is probably false. 
It would be an impulsive decision based on Payton’s one year as a starter at a lower level of football and the clear deficiencies he must 
address with his game. This would be an egregious upside pick based on athletic ability, and it would be better if Payton were a third-day 
pick and afforded time to work behind the scenes as a developmental project. 
Drops: He has a three-step drop with slow but long steps. He finishes with his feet at shoulder length to begin his setup. 
Payton has an effective seven-step roll to the left, getting the ball out on that seventh step to the flat. 
Ball Security: When scrambling, Payton carries the ball low and wide from his frame. When hitching or climbing to break the pocket, 
Payton holds the ball with both arms, but wide from his frame. There’s also exaggerated movement with is arms as he hitches and climbs. 
When he breaks the pocket and commits to running, he carries the ball high to his chest with his boundary-side arm and his elbow is tight 
to his side. He can take a hit to the ball or his ball-carrying arm. 
Play Fakes: Payton extends the ball fully as he works through his play fake toward the I-back from center. He turns his back to the defense 
as he retracts the ball, hiding it as he completes his turn on a sprint pass the opposite side as the fake to the back. 
From Pistol, Payton will slow his movement toward the RB to simulate an exchange before he retracts the ball and begins his roll or drop. 
Pump Fakes: Payton has full range of motion with his pump fake, but the violence is minimal. 
Release: Payton holds the ball closer to his stomach than his chest. When he begins his draw, the ball dips even lower – toward his hip -- 
and winds behind his back shoulder. The ball reaches the apex of his draw about a ball’s length behind his back shoulder. 
Payton takes a step with his front foot just before he begins his draw. As he brings the ball forward, Payton transfers his weight from his 
back foot to his front foot. The midline of his back foot and the toes of his front foot are aligned with the target as he begins to transfer his 
weight and bring the ball forward. 
86
players.pre_draft_advice: Monitor Payton from afar.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Payton is green and will require patience, but he’s a dynamic dual-threat quarterback. His physical tools and competitive makeup are promising qualities for a developmental prospect.

players.name: Justin Joly
players.lateround_profile: Justin Joly is a tough prospect to nail down, and the NFL Combine may not have done him many favors. His draft stock appears to be slipping. At the Combine, he measured 6’3½” and 241 pounds — about the same size as the class’s top tight end, Kenyon Sadiq — placing him firmly in the “small” bucket. But Sadiq ran a 4.39-second 40-yard dash and dominated the explosiveness drills. Joly didn’t test at all, and the tape — and his run-after-catch data — also don’t suggest that he is a game-changing athlete. Some estimates put his 40 time around 4.50, while others are less favorable, putting him in the 4.70 range. The weight is also surprising. North Carolina State listed Joly at 263 pounds as a senior, while other sources had him at 251. Coming in at 241 changes the size-athleticism conversation and reframes his promising receiving profile. At 263, he would have been among the heaviest Combine tight ends; instead, he’s one of the lightest. Still, his receiving trajectory is intriguing. A high school wide receiver, Joly began his college career slowly with 18 catches as a freshman at Connecticut. As a sophomore, he led the team with 56 receptions for 578 yards and 2 touchdowns. He then transferred to North Carolina State, continuing his rise with a team-high 661 yards and 4 touchdowns on 43 catches while playing alongside KC Concepcion in 2024. As a senior, Joly led the Wolfpack in receptions (49) and touchdown catches (7). That production built a remarkably consistent four-year résumé: Joly finished between 1.75 and 1.80 yards per route run every season, per PFF. Overall, he owns the fourth-best max yards per team attempt in the class, and a strong Breakout Score places him toward the top of the large tier of tight ends behind Sadiq and Stowers. Simply put, there’s a lot of “good” in Joly’s profile, even if no single trait stands out. That balance leads to solid comparisons and an overall positive projection. The goal is finding fantasy points — receiving production — at the NFL level. Joly should be capable of providing that. If he adds some size and earns snaps, he has a chance to emerge as the TE3 from this class purely from a fantasy perspective. JJuummpp TToo:: ZZAAPP MMooddeell || YYeeaarr 22 MMooddeell 110088
players.elevator_pitch:
Joly is one of my favorites in this tight end class. He’s an elite pass catcher with a route game that can become 
starter-caliber with a year of work. 
The biggest concerns with Joly are his stature, and his lack of top-end speed. Both issues place caps on his potential despite having 
technical skills that would elevate Joly’s grade further if the physique and athletic ability were present. 
38
players.pre_draft_advice:
Joly is worth a late-round pick in deep PPR formats and a mid-round pick after the third round in 
1.5 PPR systems.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Joly is a versatile pass catcher, because of his athleticism, ball skills and upside as a three-level threat. Built in the mold of Jonnu Smith, he is more Robin than Batman and ideally suited as a “move” tight end in a 12-personnel offense.

players.name: Eli Raridon
players.lateround_profile: Prior to the NFL Combine, some sources listed Eli Raridon at 6’7”. Others in the 6’5” range. Officially, he measured in at 6’6” and 245 pounds. He’s the second-tallest of 27 Combine-invited tight ends, but he’s pretty light despite the height. Raridon played four seasons at Notre Dame, yet despite his size, he scored just 3 times on 48 catches over 40 games. Now, we have to mention that Raridon tore his ACL as a freshman in 2022 and logged only 12 games in his first two seasons. That context is necessary. But, even with the big frame, Raridon’s blocking grades and metrics are pretty average, and his dropped pass data is enough to bring pause regarding his hands. On the flip side, Raridon is 70th-percentile or better in max yards per team attempt and Breakout Score among the tight end database, and there’s still a bit of that big-bodied, basketball-background archetype in Raridon’s profile that we’ve seen lead to some massive overperformances from late-round tight ends in the past. We shouldn’t ignore him (NFL teams won’t). Just know that there are red flags here, too. Ultimately, Raridon is interesting. In the right spot (and if a team likes his potential enough to take him earlier than consensus), there are shades of solid-to-strong fantasy football production here. But if the height and speed aren’t enough for a team to invest heavily in him, then he might be just another big, fast tight end who isn’t the total package. We’ve seen plenty of those over the years. A Colby Parkinson-type projection at the high end is interesting for the big-bodied Raridon. JJuummpp TToo:: ZZAAPP MMooddeell || YYeeaarr 22 MMooddeell 111133
players.elevator_pitch:
Raridon is what NFL scouts call a “projection pick.” NFL organizations often elevate a player’s grade ahead of his 
portfolio of film, because they like his athletic ability and his potential for additional physical development. Projection picks can work 
out, but sometimes the teams underestimate which skills are most difficult to develop. 
Raridon has the height of an every-down tight end and could have the frame to add the 15-20 pounds that he’ll need to handle that 
role. He’s fast enough to stretch a seam and beat corners outside due to his height and length advantage. 
90
players.pre_draft_advice:
Raridon may get that Notre Dame bump with his draft capital. If so, he could earn early 
opportunities based on that draft capital that aren’t commensurate with his current skill set. 
It means you might be overpaying for Raridon in dynasty formats. That said, if you see Raridon in the TE10-TE15 range in this class, 
there’s not a huge difference in grade among them. 
Monitor Raridon from afar. He might become a starter at some point, but not one you’ll have difficulty acquiring.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Raridon is still figuring things out, but he gets to top gear quickly and has promising ball skills and blocking chops. His college tape shows the building blocks of an intriguing pro-level tight end, although the medicals will influence his draft projection.

players.name: Seth McGowan
players.lateround_profile: Prior to this draft class, only one ZAP Model running back — Martez Carter — entered the NFL at age 25 or older. This year, we have two. One of them is Seth McGowan. Does age matter? Yes. The model accounts for it, and historically, older prospects have produced weaker outcomes. But context is needed. College football has shifted dramatically in recent years. Transfer rules changed. The pandemic extended eligibility. NIL deals altered incentives. As a result, we’re seeing older backs more frequently.
