Over the last two draft classes (excluding this one), 10 running backs either attended the NFL Combine or were drafted at age 24 or older. From 2011 through 2023, there were 11 total. Last year alone, RJ Harvey, Woody Marks, and Jacory Croskey-Merritt were all 24 or older at selection. Adjusted for draft capital, the hit rates for these older prospects haven’t been disastrous of late. As long as the production profiles were strong. McGowan’s profile isn’t bad — it’s just uneven. He began at Oklahoma in 2020 before being dismissed following legal issues, though the charges were ultimately dropped. After time away from the game, he resurfaced at Butler Community College in 2023, then transferred to New Mexico State in 2024. There, he logged 152 carries in a backfield that also featured 2026 draft hopeful Mike Washington. More importantly for his analytical outlook, McGowan posted a career-best 16.3% reception share that year. That season did a lot of the heavy lifting for his pass-catching profile, showing at least some ability to function on passing downs. The question is sustainability. When a receiving spike comes after multiple stops and years in the wilderness, it’s fair to wonder how sticky it really is. In 2025, McGowan transferred again — this time to Kentucky. There, he carried the ball 165 times, recorded a 9.1% reception share, and posted a career-best 1.22 adjusted total yards per team play. McGowan is a big, physical back who plays through contact. Among this year’s Combine invitees, he ranks top-five in career missed tackles forced per attempt, per PFF. His explosive run rate is average at best, but when you pair the tackle-breaking ability with an acceptable receiving profile, you can at least envision a path where he sticks as a quality backup with contingent upside.
players.elevator_pitch:
I’m betting you’ll see McGowan with a higher ranking elsewhere simply based on his size and timed 
speed. Fans of McGowan will be rooting for a redemption arc with McGowan. He arrived at Oklahoma as a 4-star recruit, and 
eventually served time for beating up an individual after a pot deal went awry. 
McGowan is a well-built back with straight-line speed who needs a big runway to reach that speed. If he can prove that his 
acceleration is better than it appears, McGowan could contribute to an offense that runs gap plays. 
McGowan has lapses with seeking cutbacks in situations where leverage his size with a downhill track is the better choice. 
McGowan’s cut-back ability is efficient, but the issue is his choice to do so. 
McGowan’s agility is good enough to contribute in a gap scheme, but he’ll struggle in a high-end contributing role if asked to 
run a zone scheme, because his footwork lacks the precision and balance for the baseline creativity needed there. 
McGowan’s power and contact balance is not as good as his size might lead people to believe. He can win against some 
linebackers, but most of his success against collisions comes against defensive backs. McGowan will drag box defenders and 
generate a push, but he lacks top-end power. 
McGowan could become a versatile pass protector and check-down receiver if he can clean up a myriad of fine details with 
positions, angles, and hand usage. 
McGowan could be a nice fit as a backup to a power running game who could eventually deliver some situational production 
or earn a rotational role as a first-call substitute for the starter. 
Vision: McGowan follows his pullers to the edge of the box and makes a timely transition downhill as his lead blockers 
engage. He’ll stick tight to his down blocks as he works downhill. 
McGowan adjusts his stride length, identifies penetration up the middle, and presses creases within 1-2 steps of the 
penetration to set up a cutback or bounce. 
McGowan is effective at making the first defender miss in the backfield – whether it’s edge or interior penetration. He’s 
creative enough with his feet to find solutions to unplanned obstacles. 
There are interior runs where he reads the leverage correctly in theory, but would get more yardage sticking with the design 
against the suboptimal leverage than attempting the cutback. Matching up one-on-one with a DB that’s partially blocked is a 
better choice than cutting back into an unblocked defensive tackle. 
Elusiveness: McGown can transition downhill to the short flat on a perimeter run with two-quick steps. When working to 
the edge of the box, he only needs one transition step to get downhill. When working to the far-side flat on a perimeter run, 
he needs three steps. 
McGowan has an effective head fake when heading downhill and setting up a safety. He delivers a sharp stick to set up 
pressure cuts. He uses a double up on approach to the line of scrimmage to set up a defensive tackle’s shot into the 
backfield before bouncing outside. 
He delivers a two-quick juke with a head fake against the high safety to set up a lane outside the defender. 
166
players.pre_draft_advice:
A late-round consideration in leagues with over 35 rosters spots and a practice squad, or 
for expanded offseason rosters until GMs can learn McGowan’s roster status by late summer-early fall.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, McGowan is a good-sized, coordinated athlete with adequate vision and run strength, although his lack of explosive traits and inconsistent third-down value will make it an uphill climb to prove his worth on an NFL roster.

players.name: Jeff Caldwell
players.lateround_profile: Jeff Caldwell might be the biggest size-speed outlier in this entire class. He’s 6’5”, 216 pounds, and ran a 4.31 at the Combine. On top of that, he posted the second-best broad jump and vertical among wide receiver invitees. That’s absurd movement at that frame. Those testing numbers probably shifted him from fringe-undrafted territory into “someone will take a shot” range. But Caldwell’s outlook is almost entirely traits-based. Caldwell began his career at Lindenwood in 2022, and he stayed there for three seasons. On the surface, a 3.24 receiving yards per team pass attempt mark in 2024 looks impressive. Once adjusted for strength of competition, though, it falls below 2.0. We have to always remember that raw dominance at a lower level doesn’t carry the same signal. Caldwell transferred to Cincinnati for his final season and finished with 32 catches for 478 yards and 6 touchdowns. His 1.31 receiving yards per team pass attempt there didn’t move the needle analytically. The result is a 29th percentile Breakout Score and a best-season adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt of 1.94. That’s not the kind of production profile that typically overcomes mid-to-late-round draft capital. However, there’s no denying the athletic profile. Caldwell’s one of the best athletes we’ve seen come out of the Combine. It’s rare. But historically, betting on testing without meaningful, translatable production is tough. Unless draft capital comes in far stronger than expected, Caldwell is more of a last-round rookie draft dart throw than someone you need to prioritize.
players.elevator_pitch:
Tool around the Internet, and you’ll see YouTube videos touting Caldwell as the wide receiver who 
“dominated the NFL Combine.” At Cadwell’s size, a 4.31-second, 40-Yard Dash and a 42-inch vertical leap are impressive 
marks. 
These two marks won Caldwell additional media buzz, and possibly another look from teams. Yet, the RSP is old enough to 
remember Chris Conley. 
245
players.pre_draft_advice:
In larger leagues, consider Caldwell in the late rounds. Otherwise, monitor from afar. If 
Caldwell earns NFL Draft capital this spring, he could be worth consideration for expanded offseason rosters as a free agent 
to monitor on your roster during camp.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Caldwell is still underdeveloped physically and fundamentally, but he offers rare length and speed, meaning he potentially could grow into a downfield threat. He will require a patient coaching staff that’s willing to invest in his raw talent.

players.name: RJ Maryland
players.lateround_profile: Based on age- and schedule-adjusted data, RJ Maryland is one of the better receiving tight ends in this year’s class — and, frankly, in the entire tight end database. But you wouldn’t know it based on the pre-draft hype, which is why the ZAP Score and the comps are uninspiring. Maryland, the son of Russell Maryland (the first overall pick in the 1991 NFL Draft), is projected to go undrafted most places you look. Maryland tore his ACL during the 2024 season, ending with just seven games played. But he bounced back with a solid senior year and ran a 4.51-second 40-yard dash, tied for second- fastest among runners in this year’s class. We have to point out that Maryland weighed in as the second-lightest tight end at the Combine at just 236 pounds. His weight-adjusted vertical was also poor, yet we’ve seen plenty of other pass-catching tight ends play well despite that being the case (Zach Ertz, OJ Howard, Dalton Schultz types) though those players were all bigger and heavier. Let’s look at some of that production I’ve mentioned. Maryland spent all four college seasons at Southern Methodist, churning out 113 catches, 1,495 yards, and 19 touchdowns in 46 games, finishing with a career 13.2 yards per catch rate. He also maintained at least 1.60 yards per route run, per PFF, for each of the last three seasons: really solid for a tight end. And for additional context here, Maryland put up these good numbers sharing time with Jordan Hudson (a Combine-invited receiver), Romello Brinson (a draft-eligible receiver), and Matthew Hibner (a Combine-invited tight end). Maryland was still pretty productive as a freshman with Rashee Rice logging 96 catches for 1,355 yards in 2022. But if none of this age-adjusted data and a still-young draft age (he won’t turn 22 until July) is getting NFL teams excited, then there’s not much we can do. Maryland is small, even compared to a light tight end class. He may not transition to a plus blocker in the NFL, which he wasn’t able to do in college. That might keep NFL teams from investing in his skillset. JJuummpp TToo:: ZZAAPP MMooddeell || YYeeaarr 22 MMooddeell 112244
players.elevator_pitch:
Maryland is the best pass-catching tight end in this class. The rest of his game is the problem. The real question 
with Maryland might be whether an NFL team should draft him as a wide receiver. More on this at the end of this section. 
The son of former University of Miami and Cowboys star DT, Russell Maryland, after seeing the film you would be inclined to think 
Maryland’s dad was Michael Irvin. Maryland wins the ball with ease because he has strong hand-eye coordination and fluid 
positioning to the football against tight coverage. 
100
players.pre_draft_advice:
Monitor Maryland from afar unless he earns the spring/summer Oronde Gadsden II buzz for a team 
that is inclined to use him as a fourth receiver more than a second tight end. The Chargers ultimately did this with Gadsden. 
Remember that injuries were a factor. 
Based on what I shared in the Elevator Pitch, Maryland’s score would be higher if the NFL considers him a receiver, but the collective 
scores of contributing receivers are also higher. It means the position won’t make a big difference for his outlook as much as the 
team fit.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Maryland is undersized by NFL standards and limited as a blocker, but he has dynamic pass-catching skills and the upside of a mismatch-creating weapon, if he returns fully to his pre-injury form.

players.name: Josh Cameron
players.lateround_profile: Josh Cameron often looks like a running back playing wide receiver. He’s got a thick, sturdy build, and Baylor leaned into that across his five seasons there. He wasn’t used out of the backfield much, but he also wasn’t asked to win deep — his career deep-ball target rate was the second-lowest in this class. His average depth of target sat a bit higher because he lived in that intermediate range. Cameron is more of a traits bet than a production one. He’ll be past his 23rd birthday at draft time, and he had five seasons to post a truly strong receiving yards per team pass attempt mark. It never happened. His best adjusted season came in at 1.90, and that translated to a 28.1 Breakout Score — just 30th percentile in the model. The ZAP Model isn’t overly enthusiastic. But one input does quietly like him: adjusted fantasy points per game. Among the 46 Combine invite wideouts, Cameron ranks 15th in that metric after age and schedule adjustments. That at least tells us he wasn’t totally unproductive. There are some interesting attributes here — size, play strength, and a profile that suggests he can handle physical work over the middle. Cameron just never showed high-end dominance. Cameron is the type of player who could hang around as a depth receiver and surprise if things break right. But unless the draft really swings in his favor, he’s not someone you need to aggressively prioritize in rookie drafts.
players.elevator_pitch:
Cameron shared in an interview that Larry Fitzgerald is the player he tries to model his game after. 
It’s a great choice, even if after watching Cameron I saw different players based on physical dimensions and athletic ability. 
Cameron and Fitzgerald are roughly the same weight, but Fitgerald has two inches on Cameron. While I don’t remember 
how many releases Fitzgerald had at Pitt, Cameron has the most thorough display of release types in his portfolio that I 
have seen from a prospect in many years. 
106
players.pre_draft_advice:
We should acknowledge that Cameron’s floor as a potential WR3 in an offense isn’t 
exciting, it’s better than most of the receivers whose ceilings are WR3 value. Cameron’s most realistic ceiling is team’s WR2 
who could deliver top 15 fantasy production in an offense with an elite route runner opposite him and a top quarterback 
distributing the ball.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Cameron plays well through contact and has functional strength and ball-winning size, but his struggle in separating and avoiding contested windows will be a limiting factor. Though he has traits that intrigue NFL teams, development time is needed.

players.name: Jack Endries
players.lateround_profile: Jack Endries has a strong tie to the 2025 Heisman Trophy winner, Fernando Mendoza. They played two seasons together in 2023 and 2024 at Cal before transferring to different schools. Endries, as a redshirt sophomore, led the Golden Bears in receptions (56) and yards (623) before transferring to Texas, where his yards per game number (26.6) was nearly cut in half (47.9) amidst tougher competition despite starting all 11 games for the Longhorns. Endries, in total, rates out with strong max yards per team pass attempt data (67th percentile) and Breakout Score (73rd percentile) despite raw yards-per-route rate numbers that may not grab your attention too much. He also ran a 4.62-second 40-yard dash at the NFL Combine, giving him a height-adjusted Speed Score of 116.0 at 6’5”, 245. On the flip side, despite a smaller frame and good athletic testing, Endries doesn’t pop from a yards-after-the-catch standpoint. That’s also tied to some average-to-below-average blocking scores from PFF and SportsInfoSolutions (and it’s worth noting that he graded out with around 15th-percentile arm length). After struggling with drops in his first season with Cal, his hands improved markedly in his final two seasons. That’s promising growth. He’s the exact type of player (decent athleticism for his size with questionable blocking traits but who might still be drafted early anyway) that the new tight end model finds interesting. His draft stock will greatly affect his ZAP score, as it’ll tell us a lot about how NFL teams are valuing his strengths compared to his weaknesses. That’s the case for every tight end outside the top two. Endries has a pretty wide range of pre-draft valuations, spanning from the middle of the second round to one of the first names being called on Day 3. If a team likes him and spends legitimate Day 2 capital on him, he becomes pretty interesting. JJuummpp TToo:: ZZAAPP MMooddeell || YYeeaarr 22 MMooddeell 111111
players.elevator_pitch:
Endries has a lot of Zach Ertz to his game. He works the middle and the flats with enough sophistication to develop 
into a reliable starter and check-down option who can also deliver big-play receptions off-play action. 
Endries knows how to sell routes with the techniques he has learned, and he’ll likely build on it as his career unfolds. He has good 
weight drop into breaks of routes that use return motion – think whip routes and certain variations of routes that work against zone 
defenders playing tight at the top of the stem. 
62
players.pre_draft_advice:
Endries is a high-floor/low-ceiling receiver for fantasy because he’s fast enough to stretch the field, 
but not explosive enough to become a high-end statistical mismatch for opposing defenses. If the NFL’s offensive trends make a 
large shift to 12 personnel sets, Endries’ blocking might need 1-2 years of work before he can see the field as a third or fourth option 
in the passing game’s pecking order. 
Endries is another late-round consideration worth stashing in 1.5 PPR formats who might be earning too much buzz as a mid-round 
value. His future will likely be that of a competent receiver but one without compelling target volume.

players.name: Kevin Coleman
players.lateround_profile: Kevin Coleman traveled everywhere in college. He began at Jackson State in 2022, transferred to Louisville, then moved to Mississippi State, and finally finished at Missouri in 2025. Coleman’s best season came at Mississippi State. As a junior, he posted a 2.53 receiving yards per team pass attempt mark, which jumped to 2.86 after strength-of-schedule adjustments. That performance fueled a 48.2 Breakout Score, seventh-best in this class. At 180 pounds, though, Coleman profiles as a true slot. Over 91% of his career routes came from that area of the field, the highest rate in the class. And that wasn’t scheme- specific — four different programs deployed him the same way, with slot rates of 87% or higher at each stop. That level of slot specialization can be limiting from a fantasy standpoint. Since 2020, wide receivers drafted with a 90% slot rate or higher include KJ Hamler, KJ Hill, Dazz Newsome, Jaelon Darden, Kyle Philips, Derius Davis, Tre Tucker, Malachi Corley, and Jimmy Horn. Draft capital wasn’t strong for that group, but it’s unlikely to be for Coleman either, with current projections placing him as a Day 3 pick. Coleman can carve out a role on special teams and in the slot at the next level. The question is ceiling. In a league increasingly leaning into heavier personnel and flexible alignments, pure slot specialists without standout draft capital tend to lack fantasy football upside.

players.name: Sam Roush
players.lateround_profile: I know. I know the comp you’re seeing. We’ll talk about it. We’ll talk a lot about Sam Roush, whose draft stock is on the rise after the NFL Combine. There’ll probably be a lot of talk about Roush on the draft broadcasts, too. He comes from an athletic family: his parents both played college sports, and numerous cousins and uncles played sports at a high level, including his great uncle, Pro Football Hall of Famer Merlin Olsen. He has lived in Malaysia and Indonesia and played rugby there. It’s a cool backstory. In one of the lightest tight end classes in the model’s history, Roush stands out as one of three names to weigh in over 253 pounds at the NFL Combine, and he led the way at 267. At 6’6”, 267, he ran a 4.70-second 40-yard dash, which works out to a 112.3 height- adjusted Speed Score. There’s more than straight-line speed, though. Roush’s 3-cone time was 7.08 seconds. I have only 44 players in my Combine database (since 2000) with a weight of 267 and a 3-cone of 7.08 or faster, and most are defensive ends. Again, going back to 2000, Roush is fifth among all tight ends in weight-adjusted vertical, coincidentally ranking third in this class alone. The leaders in this metric are Eli Stowers, Vernon Davis, Virgil Green, and Kenyon Sadiq. He’s also second since 2000 among tight ends in weight-adjusted broad jump. He’s big and athletic. What about the production? Roush had some steady production increases throughout his four years at Stanford, ultimately finishing with 49 grabs, 545 yards, and 2 touchdowns as a senior. In total, his max yards per team attempt and Breakout Score are sitting just above the 60th percentile in the model. He didn’t split out wide often but did slide into the slot at a near-average rate. He’s an inline tight end with some receiving potential. So, he should be able to find his way on the field, given his blocking, and with that speed and burst, he has more upside as a receiver than he was able to show at Stanford. George Kittle is the outlier example of being able to do more in the right offense than was asked of him at Iowa, despite mediocre draft capital; that’s where Roush shows some similarities. JJuummpp TToo:: ZZAAPP MMooddeell || YYeeaarr 22 MMooddeell 110077
players.elevator_pitch:
Roush runs effective routes, but it’s in a limited range of the field. It means he’s not a matchup threat in the 
receiving game. Roush will do his best work in zone and not as a primary option. 
Roush will be valued most for his blocking. When I arrived at this conclusion after watching Roush’s tape I also thought he’d be the 
best overall tight end in this class if the RSP’s Depth of Talent Score valued blocking on equal or greater terms as receiving skills. 
46
players.pre_draft_advice:
If Roush joins a team where the scheme uses multiple tight ends at a time, increase his value 
accordingly. If Roush joins a team where the scheme is predominantly three receivers, he’s a mid-round value who will be a patience 
play for fantasy GMs.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Roush isn’t without his warts, but he offers a traditional Y tight end skill set — competitiveness to handle dirty work as a blocker and athleticism to be a functional underneath target. He has the makeup and toughness to quietly become an integral part of an NFL offense.

players.name: Drew Allar
players.elevator_pitch:
Allar was an acclaimed high school prospect. The media and public anticipated Allar would eventually emerge as an 
acclaimed NFL prospect when he became an upperclassman. It never happened. 
112
players.pre_draft_advice:
Monitor Allar from afar. He’ll have a niche of touters because he looks like a quarterback built on an 
assembly line, but he may never have a mind for the game. The later his NFL Draft capital, the more appealing a cheap investment he’ll be 
for patient fantasy GMs.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Allar is a sturdy, strong-armed passer with promising intangibles,

players.name: Michael Trigg
players.lateround_profile: Michael Trigg’s profile is that of a well-rounded athlete. He played defensive end and basketball in high school and earned four-star recruiting status before heading to Southern California, the first of three NCAA schools for which he suited up. Trigg played only six games as a Trojan in 2021 and then seven and three games at Ole Miss in 2022 and 2023, respectively. Across those 16 games, he totaled just 28 catches for 330 yards and 5 touchdowns. But then at Baylor in 2024, Trigg notched 30 catches for 395 yards and 3 touchdowns and built on it in 2025: 50 grabs for 694 yards and 6 touchdowns, ranking top-three on the team in each of those categories in his final NCAA season. Even with that slow start, his best season by age- and schedule-adjusted max yards per team attempt is about average among the tight end database, as was his Breakout Score. We do have to note, though, that Trigg missed time not only for injuries but also for off-field incidents, and while it’s possible that the production profile could’ve been markedly better, film and scouting grades put his blocking into question. It could all be interconnected. I’m focused on the data, but this is part of the equation with Trigg, whose draft stock has been declining in the months leading up to the NFL Draft. For us as fantasy football managers, we care a lot about the receiving production, but we also know that poor blocking and commitment can mean reduced snaps. It can also translate into a new-age tight end who is tasked more with receiving than blocking. Trigg’s yards per route run data was good at all his stops, including a 2.18 mark in his last season, and he led this year’s Combine-invited class in slot rate, per PFF. That’s what makes him appealing as a fantasy football prospect. He could be a good-to-great NFL receiver. Trigg’s long arms and big hands, as measured at the Combine, help make for a highlight- level catch radius, which he certainly flashed on tape. The big board rank has been falling, but if an NFL team sees it differently and applies some capital in the late- Round-2 or middle-of-Round 3 range where he peaked, he draws some Tucker Kraft and Brenton Strange comps. Trigg could be legit if an NFL team uses him to his best abilities. JJuummpp TToo:: ZZAAPP MMooddeell || YYeeaarr 22 MMooddeell 110066
players.elevator_pitch:
Trigg might be the riskiest tight end in the draft. He has a flair for the spectacular, but difficulties with the 
mundane. He’s also limited as a blocker. 
While he has a great wingspan that could help him with specific blocks, Trigg’s physical dimensions are at their ceiling and he’s still 
undersized for work at the line of scrimmage. Trigg’s best work comes in open space as a lead blocker on perimeter runs. 
28
players.pre_draft_advice:
The safest path with Trigg is to wait to consider him after the NFL Draft shows us his landing spot. 
If you can land him in the third round of a rookie draft with 1.5 PPR scoring for the position, it’s worth the risk. 
Keep in mind that if Trigg or Kenyon Sadiq were wide receivers, would they be instant impact players? Brock Bowers would have 
been. I’m not as optimistic about Trigg and Sadiq. It means it’s probably safer watch from afar and let others take first- and or 
second-round shots on these guys.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Trigg needs additional refinement as a route runner and blocker, but he plays with the foot quickness, body control and catch-point talent to be a three-level weapon. Showing NFL teams that accountability isn’t a question will be key during the draft process and early in his career.

players.name: Jaren Kanak
players.lateround_profile: Jaren Kanak has a really noteworthy profile, but it’s not leading to much in the way of projected draft capital. Kanak, a high school track athlete and dual-threat quarterback, was a four-star recruit as an athlete with interest from a lot of big programs. He ran a 4.52-second 40-yard dash at the Combine, equating to a height-adjusted Speed Score of 109.2. While that’s not bad by any means, virtually every tight end who ran this year graded out well by that metric. Still, the athleticism alone is noteworthy. However, he measured in at the NFL Combine at 6’2”, 234 pounds, making him both the shortest and lightest of all 27 invited tight ends. Noteworthy, though. But get this: Kanak didn’t have a catch at Oklahoma until his final season…because he was a linebacker before converting to tight end ahead of the 2025 season. Noteworthy. So, if he got a Combine invite, he must have been relevant this year, right? Yep. He went out and caught 44 passes for 533 yards, both third-best on the Sooners, while playing alongside Combine-invited wide receiver Deion Burks. Because he wound up being significantly productive, his age- and schedule-adjusted max yards per team attempt and Breakout Score are actually above average since he’s still relatively young. Transparently, Kanak has an uphill battle to fantasy football production given the size and consensus draft projections, but we’ve seen plenty of fantasy-relevant tight ends enter the league with minimal reps at the position — guys who came over from basketball, for example. It’s the type of profile that — if NFL teams considered him a Round 4 guy — could easily be putting him in the mix. Without that buy-in, we can’t get out over our skis on the speed and potential. JJuummpp TToo:: ZZAAPP MMooddeell || YYeeaarr 22 MMooddeell 112222
players.elevator_pitch:
A former linebacker who transitioned to tight end in 2025, Kanak is undersized for a full-time starting tight end in 
this league unless he can leverage his speed with an elite receiving game. At this point, Kanak’s separation, routes, and pass 
catching are good enough to contribute. None of them are at a level that will help him compensate for his size and emerge as a 
quality starter. 
94
players.pre_draft_advice: Monitor from afar.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Kanak doesn’t have a large body of work, but he put a promising snapshot on film and offers some upside in the right H-back-style role. His ability to make an impact on special teams will help him stay on an NFL roster.

players.name: Eli Heidenreich
players.lateround_profile: The ZAP Model isn’t built to detect pure weirdness. You know, like Navy’s offense. Service academies simply don’t throw the ball. Since 2011, Army, Navy, and Air Force have combined to reach 300 team pass attempts in a season just twice. When that’s your environment, passing metrics can get… strange. And that’s exactly what happened with Eli Heidenreich in 2025. Heidenreich was a productive part of Navy’s offense for three seasons, but 2025 was the breakout. He caught 51 passes and posted a staggering 48.1% prorated reception share. That’s the highest mark in ZAP Model history. By roughly 23 percentage points. Heidenreich isn’t a traditional running back. Clearly. He’s more of a Swiss Army knife — a movable chess piece who can align in the backfield, in the slot, or out wide. He carried the ball 77 times last season but was deployed all over the formation as a receiver. Any team that drafts him would be doing so with that versatility in mind. He’s not projecting as an early-down grinder. Heidenreich’s receiving metrics are, frankly, absurd. His adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt sits at 4.96, best in the model’s database. Second place? Christian McCaffrey at 2.36. That tells you how distorted this profile is. The model will naturally inflate Heidenreich’s score because the inputs are extreme. But historically, tweener utility players like this tend to be more valuable to real- life offenses than to fantasy managers. And often, they don’t stick at all — think Jaylen Samuels. Heidenreich is fascinating analytically. In a class that lacks a lot of firepower, there’s at least something to latch onto with him. Just don’t get carried away.
players.elevator_pitch:
Heidenreich has special teams promise as a return specialist and/or coverage option who can 
contribute to an offense as a fourth or fifth receiver but more likely as a utility player who earns as many touches as a 
running back as he does a WR3-WR5. 
Heidenreich has the ball-carrying skills on the perimeter and the open field to deliver as an extension of the ground game 
from a variety of spots. He tracks, adjusts, and catches the ball effectively in the capacity of a zone receiver, an outlet from 
the backfield, or on schemed plays up the seam or boundary for big plays. 
For Heidenreich to become a wide receiver, he must develop a complete route game, and he’s not as far from that 
possibility as his role with Navy might lead you to believe. He has developed techniques for releases, setups, and breaks, 
but he must expand the precision of their execution beyond the shallow range of the field. 
Developing these skills may prove easier than trying to develop a ground game between the tackles and add 10-15 pounds 
of muscle to try to become an every-down back. Expect Heidenreich to begin his career as a return specialist or coverage 
player while competing for situational opportunities in an offense in a utility role. 
Vision: In the open field, Heidenreich is patient with luring the safety downhill before delivering a stick and spin 
combination at the boundary to the inside. 
He’s primarily an option runner taking tosses or jet sweeps. 
Elusiveness: When earning a run to the near-side edge, Heidenreich can transition downhill from a perimeter approach 
with one step. To the far-side edge, he needs two steps. 
In the open field, Heidenreich is patient with luring the safety downhill before delivering a stick and spin combination at the 
boundary to the inside. He gets his feet over low shots. 
Heidenreich has good curvilinear bend when working around end and still inside the hash. He has a nice two-quick cut inside 
downhill pursuit from a safety to get by the defender. 
He’s quick enough and has enough moves with spins, pressure, cuts and two-quick footwork to make multiple defenders 
miss during a run. 
Acceleration: Heidenreich can turn the short corner on linebackers, but the backside linebacker will run him down after 
getting 5-10 yards downhill. The same is true with runs to the long corner. He’ll turn the corner, but the backside linebacker 
will run him down 5-10 yards later. 
Speed: Heidenreich has the speed to flip the field and hold off defensive backs for long gains, if not breakaway runs 
depending on the angle. 
Power: Heidenreich can run through reaches from defensive backs. He uses a stiff arm to swath away from reaches. He 
keeps his feet moving when wrapped high. 
Direct Contact Balance: He can stalemate a safety. 
Indirect Contact Balance: Heidenreich will drop his pads and finish, colliding with a cornerback and running through the 
hit to extend past the defender. 
Releases: Heidenreich has a staggered stance with 80/20 weight distribution favoring his front foot. His hands rest at 
either side of his front knee. When he begins his release, he sinks into his stance and rolls off his front foot without waisted 
movement. 
Heidenreich uses sticks at the top of stems to set up a break. He has a read step that he pairs with a sudden two-quick 
inside to break outside on a speed turn. He counters the defender’s hands with a wipe or a swim. 
Heidenreich must create a greater contrast between patience and suddenness with his releases. He uses footwork to be 
patient and then accelerate into his stem, but he must develop patience and suddenness with the actual footwork move. 
Heidenreich uses a slow double up with a wipe and then stacks the safety up the seam. He steals a release with a read step 
outside to then break inside and shield the CB with his back on the backside of run. 
206
players.pre_draft_advice:
While there’s a chance he could develop in full-time offensive player with fantasy value, 
Heidenreich is likely a utility option with potential special teams value who you can monitor from afar in most formats. 
Heidenreich has a nominally higher score than some of the players in his tier who I like more as investments because of his 
versatility.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Heidenreich has dynamic pass-catching ability that can be weaponized in a hybrid role by an NFL offense (think closer to Danny Woodhead than Kyle Juszczyk). He won’t be a fit for everybody, but he will be a fit for somebody.

players.name: J'Mari Taylor
players.elevator_pitch:
Taylor is likely a career backup, but the caliber of backup that could have 1-2 strong seasons as a 
starter if called upon to substitute for a franchise-caliber back. Taylor runs with good power, breaking tackles, bouncing off 
collisions with linebackers and safeties, and drags and pushes runners who wrap him high. 
His decision-making and agility are good enough to get Taylor into creases and/or avoid unblocked defenders. Taylor takes 
what the defense and the scheme give him. He doesn’t take outsized risks based on down and distance and field position. 
Taylor has the footwork and cutting ability to create space with efficiency and dynamism. It makes Taylor dangerous 
between the tackles and in the open field. 
Taylor has the acceleration to reach the secondary, the power to run through defenders at this level, and the speed to at 
least maintain the separation he initially earned with his burst so he can prolong the run and force defensive backs to give 
chase. 
Taylor is a check-down/screen receiver who must prove he can do more if his new NFL offense demands a bigger route tree. 
Otherwise, he has capable hands if you’re optimistic Taylor’s focus drops will subside or remain at a low frequency of 
occurrence. 
Despite Taylor’s rugged work, he has a strong track record with ball security. His pass protection will improve as Taylor 
curtails his habit of overextending at the point of attack. 
Taylor should help a team and contribute on the field. He could develop into a starter if he adds more quick-twitch muscle 
that aids his power, speed, and acceleration. 
Vision: Taylor sets up his puller inside-out to work around penetration at the line of scrimmage and get into a crease. He 
has a feel for soft spots in traffic to keep plays alive after wrapped. 
Taylor sets up his double team on inside zone with a press to the inside shoulder of the inside defender within a step of the 
block before bouncing to the outside gaps. Once in the second-level, Taylor is decisive about splitting blocks in the 
secondary and using his low center of gravity to run through defenders. 
Taylor identifies penetration off the edge and mitigates it with his burst and movement. He also identifies interior 
penetration and can make a quick jump stop to avoid the lineman and reach the hole. 
Taylor presses within a step of his blockers before executing cutbacks and bounces. 
When bouncing a run outside, Taylor shows some skill to press the tackle to incrementally work to the edge. He also 
presses lead blocks in the open field within a step of the blocker. 
Taylor takes what the defense and the scheme give him. He doesn’t take outsized risks based on down and distance and 
field position. 
Elusiveness: Taylor has an effective jump cut to work across the back of a blocker. He also can slide across the face of an 
unblocked lineman penetrating into the backfield. 
102
players.pre_draft_advice:
Taylor is a mid-round consideration in a draft with 7-10 picks. He’s an end of round 
consideration in shorter drafts of 4-6 picks.

players.name: CJ Daniels
players.lateround_profile: My three-year-old son’s favorite wide receiver in this class is CJ Daniels. And that’s strictly because my son’s name is CJ. The ZAP Model does not share his enthusiasm. Daniels’ College Football Reference page reads like a 700-page novel. He spent four years at Liberty, where his only truly meaningful production came in Year 4. That season was legitimately impressive: a 3.14 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt mark — the fourth-best max-season number in this class — which helped fuel a strong 47.3 Breakout Score. The issue? Every other season he played was below 1.70 in that metric. After Liberty, Daniels transferred to LSU for a year, then finished at Miami. That’s six collegiate seasons. He’ll be 24 when the draft rolls around. Late breakouts can work, but they usually need draft capital support. In the ZAP Model database, 32 wide receivers failed to go top-100 while posting a Breakout Score below 60 but a best-season adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt above 3.0. In other words, they had one monster efficiency year, but age suppressed the overall breakout profile. Of those 32 players, 30 never even reached 7 PPR points per game in any of their first three seasons. Then there’s the age bucket itself. Prior to this class, 35 wide receivers entered the league at age 24 or older. Only one — John Brown — produced a 10-plus PPR points-per- game season within his first three years. Brown also had top-100 draft capital, something Daniels isn’t likely to receive. Now, could the model be a little too mean towards older prospects given the NIL era, transfer portal freedom, and COVID eligibility ripple effects? That’s fair to consider. College football has changed. But it’s also fair to acknowledge that Daniels really only gave us one high-end season across six years. There’s juice in that peak campaign. But the rest of the profile is just thin. De’Zhaun Stribling • WR With the modern transfer portal era, some players end up building résumés across half the country. De’Zhaun Stribling is one of them. He began at Washington State, transferred to Oklahoma State for two seasons, and finished in 2025 at Ole Miss. The analytical profile is…fine. He posted a 28.5 Breakout Score, driven by a 2024 season where his adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt cleared 2.0. That was his high- water mark. In 2025 at Ole Miss, he dropped back to 1.70. Nothing really jumps off the page. His 18.3% deep-ball target rate was below average in this class, yet his contested target rate was above average — a combination that can hint at separation concerns rather than pure vertical specialization. If there’s momentum behind Stribling, it may stem from timing. He had several strong showings on big stages: 5 catches for 79 yards and a score against Tulane in the College Football Playoff, 7 for 122 against Georgia, and 5 for 77 in a loss to Miami. Three of his top-four yardage games came in high-visibility spots, and he also went for 96 earlier in the season against Georgia. Those flashes matter for some evaluators. They just don’t override the broader body of work. Unless draft capital surprises in a meaningful way, Stribling isn’t someone you’ll need to prioritize in rookie drafts.
players.elevator_pitch:
Daniels is one of those players where it is hard not to like him when you watch his game on the field. 
He’s a physical ball-winner who displays acrobatic skill and terrific hand-eye coordination in tight coverage. Get Daniels 
within semi-realistic area of the football, and he’s better than most at making the difficult catch. 
Daniels also runs with physicality after the catch. He’s not explosive or sudden, but he’s powerful and determined. Get 
Daniels free in zone coverage or scheme routes that can work open with the help of misdirection, and Daniels can make big 
plays. 
221
players.pre_draft_advice:
Daniels is worth a late-round pick in leagues with large rosters with at least 45 spots, 
including a robust practice squad. Otherwise monitor Daniels from afar as a future desperation bye-week pick.

players.name: Carson Beck
players.elevator_pitch:
The NFL will covet Beck more than the initial press he earned in 2025 after transferring from Georgia to Miami. A top 
prospect heading into 2024, Beck had what the media has characterized as an up-and-down career at Georgia. 
Despite a respectable overall statistical profile in 2024, Beck didn’t follow up an even stronger 2023 campaign with similar highs in 
completion percentage and yardage. Beck also doubled his interception count from 6 to 12. 
Beck ended his career at Georgia after tearing a ligament in his throwing elbow before the college playoffs. Beck rebounded at Miami and 
either came close to matching or exceeding most box score figures media values and led the Hurricanes to the college football championship 
game. 
Still, the narrative makers cite that Beck was a fifth-year passer, and his strong statistical performance last year was a baseline requirement 
instead of an impressive layer to his evaluation as a pro prospect. The NFL will value these bullet points, especially team executives who 
subsist on them, especially when they’re infused with risk management data points that will cover their asses – I mean assets. 
Prototypical physical dimensions 
Top college programs that are playing winning football 
Arm strength 
• 
• 
• 
• Good box score statistics 
• Good NFL Combine and Pro Day performances with scripted football ballet vs air 
• 
Paid for interview training that comes with the correction dog collar and remote 
• 
Reasonable track record of health 
The more of these data points a GM, VP, and President can check off, the less risk they feel about taking the player in the earliest rounds of 
the NFL Draft. If things go wrong with the player’s development, they can at least point to the list of things about the player that were low 
risk. They can then point the finger elsewhere. 
Beck’s career checks most of these boxes on the list. There’s still a pervasive belief that because you can’t teach size, athletic ability, and 
arm talent at the levels they covet them, so you draft these Robo QBs. Then, you can teach them how to play the quarterback position at 
an advanced level. 
Except there’s a flaw to this mindset. There’s little to no teaching. Talk of coaches developing players is mostly lip service. 
The same scene plays out at most NFL organizations every year. A franchise considers drafting an early-round rookie quarterback: Owners 
and executives ask coaches if they can develop the quarterback. The coaches reflexively say, “yes,” because they know the question is the 
executives’ coded way of saying, “We want to draft him and you’re going to be the scapegoat if the player doesn’t develop.” 
The coach’s affirmative response of “Yes,” the coach’s version of only showing the tip of the iceberg. If you dive below the water’s surface, 
you’d see a longer response vocalized in the coach’s head. 
“Yes, I want to keep my job right now so I’m going to agree despite knowing we won’t have the time to do much with him. I’m just saying 
yes to end this painful meeting. 
I’ve known all along that I’m your scapegoat for just about everything that doesn’t work on the field. I’ll likely blame the player when he 
can’t figure out the game at this level. 
Not that it usually works, but I’ve seen that it can buy me a year while I make plans for my golden parachute elsewhere. I can live with 
your entrepreneurial-nepotistic-corporate cocktail of b.s. – like coming down to the sideline in the second half of games as if your presence 
is going to suddenly click all 316 team processes into place. Just please, stop sending me plays to run on cocktail napkins at halftime like 
you’re a hybrid of Ceasar and Madden.” 
Odds are likely this is the environment Beck will be headed to if he’s a first-round selection. Whether Beck earns a first-round pick depends 
on how much franchises are being real with themselves, or they’re leaning too hard the historical economics of position scarcity instead of 
working harder on figuring out what makes a good QB and what they can do to be a part of that process. 
As with most of these prospects, Beck needs time to refine his game. He has enough skills for decision-makers with personnel convince 
themselves he’s better than he is right now. 
Beck plays played in system with pro concepts and he’s well-versed in the drop-back and play-action game. Whether you want to use Beck 
under center, shotgun, or Pistol, he has done it and shown competent work with all the bells and whistles that come with misdirection out of 
these alignments. 
79
players.pre_draft_advice:
Beck’s fantasy value could increase if the NFL gives him first-round draft capital. Based on what we’ve 
seen play out with quarterbacks for decades, it’s better to target Beck in a few years via trade or as a free agent than spend capital on him. 
Target receivers.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Beck is a cerebral pocket passer who can lead drives when the offense stays in rhythm, but he isn’t a proven second-reaction quarterback when things don’t go according to plan. He projects as an NFL backup and potential spot starter.

players.name: Roman Hemby
players.lateround_profile: The advanced numbers don’t paint Roman Hemby as a dynamic runner. His career avoided tackles per rush rate is tied for the third-worst in this class. He gained 15 or more yards at the second-lowest rate. His yards after contact per attempt also sits in the bottom half among Combine invites. Those are rushing indicators. And they’re not flattering. The ZAP Model is a bit more forgiving because it leans heavily on receiving inputs. At Maryland, Hemby posted an 11% reception share or better in three of his four seasons — the three in which he played more than two games. His adjusted total yards per team play and adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt were below average, but not alarmingly so. Hemby then transferred to Indiana for his final season, where he set a career high with 230 carries. Once you account for offensive environment, though, his attempt share wasn’t meaningfully different from what it had been at Maryland. He never exceeded a 40% rush attempt share in college. Put it all together and the profile becomes fairly clear. Hemby projects as a committee back, likely with some pass-catching utility. That’s exactly what his statistical comparables suggest: a complementary piece who can contribute, but probably not anchor a backfield on his own.
players.elevator_pitch:
Hemby is one of those players who has a high floor of potential, but a lower ceiling than some of the 
players ranked below him whose floor/ceilings are volatile by comparison. Hemby is a capable runner in zone and gap 
schemes. 
Regardless of the scheme, Hemby has a feel for setting up good angles for himself and taking away good angles from 
defenders. 
Hemby attacks lanes decisively on gap runs. He sets up double teams on Duo with good stride length and pacing 
adjustments. When he’s forced to bounce, he opens his hips efficiently. When he reaches the edge of the defense, he 
decelerates efficiently to transition downhill. 
Hemby finishes runs with starter-level power and contact balance. His ball security track record is excellent. Hemby can 
earn volume and deliver. 
Hemby’s speed and acceleration are between committee and starter grades. He’ll get some corners on perimeter plays and 
he can often reach the secondary and force a long chase. It depends on the play type and the personnel he’s facing. 
Overall, Hemby is explosive enough to contribute on a statistically relevant level. 
Hemby’s pass-catching is good, but his blocking and routes can be better. Hemby can deliver as a check-down option right 
now. His blocking is not ready for a significant role on passing downs. 
Hemby can become a capable RB3 with an RB2 ceiling. 
Vision: Hemby reads the double team on inside zone and looks for advantageous leverage. When it’s not there, he cuts 
back or bounces to the next gap with a soft spot or opening and takes what is available. 
Hemby presses within 1-3 steps of the line of scrimmage depending on the blocking and leverage of the key defenders in 
the scheme. 
Elusiveness: Hemby adjusts stride length and pacing to set up combo blocks to the linebackers on Duo. When this doesn’t 
happen in timely fashion, Hemby opens his hips with the point of his boundary-side foot to the sideline and bounces the run 
outside efficiently. 
Hemby just needs two steps to decelerate and drop his weight so he can transition downhill from a perimeter approach on a 
toss play. 
He uses a stick to freeze and accelerate past a CB at the second level. 
Hemby’s ability to alter his stride and pace works well between the tackles on inside zone. 
Hemby can spin off contact. 
Hemby only needs two steps to transition from a perimeter approach outside the numbers to the short-side boundary and 
get downhill on a zone run. 
108
players.pre_draft_advice:
Hemby is a player to monitor from afar despite the high floor. This may change if Hemby 
earns a draft pick.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Hemby is a linear back with only average vision and power, but he is a decisive runner with acceleration to clear holes and get downhill. His pass-catching ability and contributions as a kick returner could help separate him at the back end of an NFL running back depth chart.

players.name: Jaydn Ott
players.elevator_pitch:
Ott is a good runner in space who catches the ball well enough on check-downs and screens to utilize 
his speed. He must improve his hands and routes to become a dynamic receiver in the NFL. 
This will be important, because Ott does his best work in space. He has sharp, efficient cuts and changes direction well 
enough to create separation from unblocked defenders. 
Between the tackles, Ott has some clear strengths and weaknesses. When running against light boxes, he’s patient, reads 
leverage well, and takes what the defense gives him. When running against heavier boxes, Ott makes ill-advised cutbacks 
and bounces. 
Ott is a competent blocker in chip-release, or scenarios where he’s double-teaming, handling a DB or off-ball LB one-on-one 
before leaking to the flat. He could compete for a third-down role in training camp, especially if he pushes hard for a special 
teams role as coverage or as a return specialist. 
Vision: Ott reads leverage on inside zone and sets up his full back and double team blocks. He identifies penetration into 
the backfield and finds efficient solutions. He’s patient with this concept and will take the tight crease and generate a push. 
Ott presses within 1-2 steps of the line of scrimmage to set up creases. He uses the double-up effectively to set up 
penetration before sliding inside the push into the backfield and into the open crease. 
Ott takes what he can get on gap plays that get foiled early. He doesn’t attempt unnecessary cutbacks or bounces away 
from the design when the boxes are light (6 defenders or less). When boxes are heavier (7 or more), he look for daylight 
with cutbacks that are ill-advised rather than following the play design that would have earned more yards with less 
physical exertion. He also tries these cutbacks deep in his own territory in the black zone. 
He’s better at reading leverage against lighter boxes when running Power or Trap. 
Elusiveness: Ott adjusts his stride length to set up lead blocks as well as make multiple penetrators into the backfield 
miss. He’s light on his feet and possesses sharp pressure cuts. 
Ott uses spins after contact to extend forward through wraps after the initial collision. He opens his hips with the point of 
the toe and can bounce or cutback with economy. 
Ott only needs two small and quick steps to transition downhill from a perimeter approach. Ott can spin off that two-step 
transition 
Ott can jump stop and jump cut in succession to avoid penetration and slide into an adjacent rushing lane. 
Acceleration: Ott has enough acceleration to beat a linebacker up the short-side boundary and accelerate past a safety’s 
angle of pursuit. This is also the case with runs to the far-side perimeter. He possesses enough stop-start quickness to 
avoid penetration. 
Speed: Ott can threaten a secondary because he can get angles on safeties and hold off some cornerbacks. Get Ott in 
space, and he can flip the field and even author a breakaway run. 
Power: Ott has effective pad level that helps him get skinny through tight creases that Duo plays often produce. This helps 
him earn a forward lean through wraps from box defenders. 
178
players.pre_draft_advice: Monitor Ott from afar.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Ott is a bursty, one-cut runner, with the versatility to add value on third downs and special teams as a kick returner. However, NFL teams wary of his durability and toughness will look elsewhere.

players.name: Jam Miller
players.lateround_profile: Jam Miller flashed some juice at the NFL Combine. At 209 pounds, he ran a 4.42 40, giving him a Speed Score just shy of 110. That might be the best part of the profile, if we’re being honest. Miller spent four seasons at Alabama and never reached 150 carries in a year. His best rush attempt share season topped out at 29.7%. Through the air, he never hit an 8% reception share in any campaign. That combination — modest workload and minimal receiving — is historically tough to overcome. There are 34 running backs in the ZAP Model database who failed to clear a 30% best-season attempt share and an 8% reception share. Just four of them reached 10 or more PPR points per game in one of their first three seasons: CJ Anderson, Chris Carson, Dameon Pierce, and Damien Harris. All four weighed at least 215 pounds entering the league. Miller doesn’t. Even if we loosen the thresholds — backs under 215 pounds with a best-season attempt share below 40% and a best-season reception share below 10% — there are 43 players in that bucket. Only Devonta Freeman, Elijah Mitchell, and Darrell Henderson produced meaningful fantasy seasons. And that’s before diving into the advanced rushing metrics. Despite the strong 40 time, Miller ranked dead last in this year’s class in collegiate explosive run rate, per PFF. It’s one of the lowest marks we’ve seen in the last five draft cycles. His yards after contact per attempt also ranked dead last. Alabama’s depth chart is never easy to crack, and they obviously play some tough defenses. But the overall résumé doesn’t suggest we should be pounding the table for Miller this offseason. The Speed Score is intriguing. The production profile is not.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Miller has the build and athleticism to be a reliable one-cut runner, but he must improve as a receiver and blocker to see quality NFL playing time. Scouts are bullish about his potential on special teams, which will help separate him on a roster.

players.name: Jordan Hudson
players.lateround_profile: Jordan Hudson was a highly recruited prospect who chose Texas Christian out of high school. He spent the 2022 season at TCU alongside a group that included NFLers Quentin Johnston, Derius Davis, Savion Williams, Jared Wiley, and Kendre Miller before transferring to SMU, where he played three seasons. The production at SMU was… fine. Nothing more. Hudson’s receiving yards per team pass attempt cleared 1.0 just once, and that came in 2025, his final year. He technically registered a Breakout Score, but it landed at just 27.8, well below where you’d want it for someone you’re hoping can outkick Day 3 draft capital. There aren’t many standout traits in Hudson’s profile. His first downs per route run were slightly above the class average, and his 1.93 career yards per route run is respectable enough for a likely late-round pick. But dig a little deeper and you see the imbalance: his perimeter yards per route run was just 1.77, while his slot yards per route run was a strong 2.94, sixth-best in the class. Oddly, he barely played in the slot. Only about 14% of his collegiate routes came from that alignment. If an NFL team leans into that more heavily, maybe there’s something to unlock. He was also slightly above average in missed tackles forced per reception, which at least hints at some after-the-catch juice. As it stands, though, he projects more like a traditional boundary receiver without the boundary-dominant efficiency you’d want to see. And for late-round bets, you usually want at least one clear, bankable trait. Hudson’s profile just doesn’t offer that hook.
players.elevator_pitch:
Hudson is a possession receiver with enough speed to stretch the field against favorable coverage 
matchups. His release work against man coverage has enough craft for him to have success at the line of scrimmage and 
the speed is there to beat cornerbacks on intermediate and vertical routes. 
Hudson has more work ahead with route setups and breaks. The setups he uses are good, but he needs to develop more of 
them. Hudson’s breaks are better with shallow routes and less precise with some of his intermediate routes. Both vital 
phases of route running are heading in the right direction. 
164
players.pre_draft_advice:
Hudson is a high-floor, medium-ceiling option with a better thanking than several players 
below him who have higher ceilings but slightly lower floors within the same tier. Monitor from afar unless Hudson lands 
enviable NFL Draft capital. If this happens, consider him for your expanded offseason roster.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Hudson isn’t a polished player at this point in his development, but he is a good-sized athlete with a bionic way of adjusting to the football. The NFL talent is there. Hopefully, he’ll land with a coaching staff able to get the best out of him.

players.name: Dae'Quan Wright
players.elevator_pitch:
Wright has a large catch radius with targets arriving above his waist, big hands, and he tracks the ball like a starting 
NFL receiver. When the ball is in his hands, Wright is a compelling runner who can change direction with creativity, precision, and 
suddenness. 
107
players.pre_draft_advice:
Monitor Wright from far. If he shines in camp, consider for a summer-long addition to expanded 
rosters to monitor from your roster.

players.name: Cade Klubnik
players.elevator_pitch:
Like Drew Allar, Klubnik was a nationally acclaimed high school prospect. Unlike Allar, Klubnik’s game took a promising 
statistical leap forward in 2024, and the NFL Draft media touted Klubnik last spring as a potential first-round pick, if not the top prospect at 
his position. Klubnik didn’t sustain that production in 2025 and led to a precipitous drop in the perceived NFL Draft stock. 
Allar is the Robo-QB who hasn’t developed the craft of the position necessary to warrant an early-round pick. Klubnik is the inverse of Allar. 
He lacks prototypical size, arm strength, and athletic ability. These traits are all good enough to make an NFL team, but these are some of 
the questions teams will raise about Klubnik’s potential as a starter. 
Another concern I’ve heard from those in the scouting game is that Clemson’s offense and coaching regime doesn’t advance the conceptual 
and technical skills of its passers while they are with the team. College football is big business, and development is a low priority with many 
big-time coaches. Winning is their primary job and there’s a lot of pressure to do so. 
While this observation shared with me sounds like logo scouting, and my source admitted as much, one can make the subtle distinction it is 
not. These players all worked in a similar offense in the Dabo Sweeney regime. Logo scouting would be saying Oklahoma State has a history 
of great running backs but grouping Barry Sanders and Thurman Thomas with Chuba Hubbard and Ollie Gordon. 
When examining Klubnik’s game, accuracy and velocity are the chief concerns. Klubnik has a habit of sailing passes in the intermediate 
zones, especially in the middle of the field. 
There are two potential reasons this is happening. Klubnik begins his release with a wide stance out of a three-step drop. His feet are wider 
than shoulder width. This contributes to targets that sail high in the short and intermediate ranges of the field. 
Another potential reason for his targets sailing is what happens when Klubnik rotates his front foot during the release. Klubnik pushes down 
on the toe of his front foot. This creates elevation upward as he follows through. This may contribute to the ball sailing on downfield routes 
in the middle of the field, and it was problematic throughout 2025 – most often early in the season. 
Klubnik must generate more velocity with far-sideline throws on intermediate outs and in the vertical game. Klubnik’s late-outs require 
precise footwork to generate velocity, and while he has shown he can generate the velocity, he must do it with greater frequency. 
Klubnik has lapses with velocity where the ball is catchable, but the lack of zip forces the receiver to slow his pace and turn back inside with 
his break. This opens the passing lane for the cornerback to play a play on the ball. 
The middle of the field and timing routes to the intermediate and vertical boundary are areas where NFL starting quarterbacks must thrive. 
Klubnik’s game raises questions whether it can. 
If Klubnik corrects these facets of his game, there’s a lot to like – enough to like him as a future starter. His drops are efficient and have 
some advanced skills that could help him work well from center. 
Klubnik’s lower body needs tweaking during his release, but his stroke is efficient and he can throw off platform. 
Klubnik exhausts three options and makes correct reads of leverage that leads to him holding the ball, resetting his position in the pocket, 
and finding an open receiver off script. When the sailing targets aren’t a problem, Klubnik has demonstrated good placement in the middle 
of the field because he accounts for underneath coverage that is potentially in position to cut off the target. 
Klubnik also displays good anticipation under pressure in the pocket because he sees the leverage of defenders against zone routes that will 
break open. He’ll split defenders with low placement so the receiver is the only option who can earn the ball in the shallow flat. 
Klubnik patient with the zone triangle – even after pivoting from a different quadrant of the field. He’ll allow his leak route to influence the 
triangle so he can make a tight window throw a viable target with good placement against the defender’s leverage. 
When breaking the pocket on the move, Klubnik is aware of backside receivers and can find the wide-open option across the field with no 
defender around. He’ll also throw the ball away when routes are covered. 
Klubnik doesn’t just play it safe, he makes layered throws over a dropping linebacker into the vertical ranges of the field. He even does this 
while side-stepping to his left from pressure. 
Klubnik hangs in compressed pocket for three reads, not panicking to escape as he pivots to the opposite side of the field to access that 
third read. 
These are promising field-reading skills paired with moments of advanced pocket management. The league covets players who can do this 
type of work. 
Klubnik has lapses and holes when reading the field. It’s rare, but Klubnik has had moments where he doesn’t account for the leverage of a 
defender on an intermediate out and tests the defender squatting on the route. 
62
players.pre_draft_advice:
Klubnik is worth a late-round pick in Super Flex formats or one-QB leagues with large rosters and practice 
squads. He’s a luxury for most rosters as patience play, so most of you will monitor from afar.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Klubnik is a smooth-twitch athlete with the arm talent to make big-time throws, but his inconsistent pocket presence and urgency reading the field need to be addressed. Projecting as a backup, he would benefit from a rollout, rhythm-based passing game to help open throwing lanes.

players.name: Dean Connors
players.elevator_pitch:
Connors is a skilled pass-catcher and space player who has success as a gap runner. He could begin 
his career as a return specialist and work his way into a contributing role on passing downs because of his excellent hands, 
acrobatic skill at the catch point, and athletic ability in the open field. 
As good punt return specialists are, Connors understands when to use efficient and/or dynamic moves to make defenders 
miss while still attacking downhill with aggression. This is also what makes Connors a capable runner behind gap blocking. 
Connors finishes runs like a pro, using his hands, pads, and his legs to work through reaches, wraps, and hits. His ball 
security needs work if he’s going to translate these skills to the NFL. 
Connors must also iron out the flaws as a pass protector. He has a lot of little flaws that are correctable but could hurt him 
if he’s doing something different that gives up pressure on multiple reps during a series or a game. 
Unless Connors proves a quick study from his college tape, or he has been working on the flaws of his game behind the 
scenes, it may take him 1-2 seasons before he can emerge as a situational contributor. If Connors shores up his blocking 
and his ball security, he could earn a situational role soon. 
If Connors gets a little faster and improves his decision making between the tackles, he could emerge as a rotational starter 
with statistical value. 
Conceptual: Although he needs work with sticking with play designs—Connors has the running style, quickness, footwork, 
and aptitude to deliver as a punt return specialist if he can catch punts. 
Vision: Connors reads edge penetration slanting inside to the backfield as he takes the exchange. 
In the open field, Connors will make a defender with combinations of movements, but he also knows when to drop the pads 
and take what’s in front of him. He’ll freeze one defender than then split that defender and the inside pursuit with good pad 
level and determined downhill running. He has a good feel for pursuit and veers away from the flow that pursuit in the open 
field. 
As an inside runner, Connors tends to run to the immediate open space rather than manipulate defenders into blocks as the 
play design dictates. It leads to wasted movement and unnecessary bounces/cutbacks that limit the potential of the design. 
He’s at his best as a gap runner who sets up lead blockers. Lead draw is one of his best plays. 
Elusiveness: Connors has effective curvilinear speed to bend inside a cornerback’s run blitz within 2-3 steps. He has a 
double up to juke edge containment and combines it with a head fake. Connors can follow up these jukes with a spin inside. 
Connors sets up creases and unblocked defenders with change of stride length. He has the quickness to give and take away 
a leg in the crease and make a linebacker miss in traffic. 
Connors has routine success layering moves in succession to defeat defenders in space. His use of the head fake is a central 
component in the combinations that he uses. 
Connors only needs two quick steps to transition from a perimeter approach to a downhill approach to the far-side edge of a 
play. He only needs one step when transitioning at the near-side edge. 
145
players.pre_draft_advice:
Monitor Connors from afar until we see if he earns better draft capital than expected. If he 
has low or no draft capital, as expected, consider Conners if he competes for a return role in leagues that score for returns. 
Otherwise, watch, wait, and add to expanded rosters if there are signs he’s in the mix for a complementary role in 1-2 
years.

players.name: Robert Henry
players.lateround_profile: Robert Henry’s best-season reception share was 12.3%, which is respectable on the surface. The problem is that it came with major inefficiency, largely due to adjustments. In that same season, his age- and schedule-adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt was just 0.40, the fourth-worst mark in this class. Context doesn’t really save it, either. Henry played at Jones College and UTEP, and he reached higher than a 7% reception share in just that one campaign. He wasn’t facing elite weekly competition — the production simply wasn’t there in an impactful way. Athletically, there’s not much of a boost. At 196 pounds, Henry ran a 4.52 40 at the Combine, resulting in a below average 93.9 Speed Score. Historically, that’s a tough combo. In the ZAP Model database, 111 running backs have posted both a sub-100 Speed Score and a sub-0.5 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt rate. Only nine of them reached 10 or more PPR points per game in one of their first three seasons: Alfred Morris, Stevan Ridley, Andre Ellington, Alex Collins, Thomas Rawls, Jordan Howard, Chris Carson, Ronald Jones, and James Robinson. Every single one of those backs weighed more than Henry at the Combine. Add in the age — he’ll be 24 after five collegiate seasons — and the odds shrink even further. Older running back prospects haven’t produced much sustained fantasy success historically. Even in a favorable landing spot, Henry doesn’t project cleanly into a three-down role. He’s not built like a classic early-down bruiser, and the receiving profile doesn’t suggest a natural passing-down specialist, either. That’s an awkward middle ground to live in. And it’s not where you want your Day 3 dart throw to sit.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Henry lacks a true distinguishing trait, but he is a productive, one-cut runner with functional quickness, vision and toughness. Although he isn’t the same level of athlete, his tape is reminiscent of Elijah Mitchell’s when he was coming out of Louisiana.

players.name: Aaron Anderson
players.lateround_profile: There are four LSU wide receivers at the NFL Combine this year, but all of them are projected to go on Day 3 — including Aaron Anderson. Anderson began his career at Alabama, redshirting as a freshman before transferring to LSU. He then spent three seasons in Baton Rouge, where his production profile settled in as below average. His best adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt was 1.88, which translated to a 25.5 Breakout Score. That’s not moving the needle much analytically. To his credit, Anderson posted the best yards per route run figures among the LSU wideouts in this class. His 2.07 career yards per route run is slightly above the class average among Combine invites. But that efficiency came almost entirely from the slot — he ran over 88% of his routes from there, the second-highest slot rate in the class. That archetype hasn’t treated us well recently. Since 2020, there have been 12 wide receivers in the ZAP Model database with a Breakout Score below 40 and a career slot rate above 80%. None of them have reached 10 PPR points per game in one of their first three seasons. And two of those players were top-100 picks. Anderson likely won’t get that kind of draft capital boost. It’s hard to see a clear path to fantasy relevance here. Anderson could carve out a depth role as a slot specialist, but from a rookie draft perspective, this isn’t a profile you need to prioritize.
players.elevator_pitch:
Anderson is a slot receiver with potential as a return specialist. He has speed to become a big-play slot 
option, but he hasn’t figured out how to ingrain it into his vertical routes so he’s maximizing separation. 
Anderson’s underneath routes are a different story. He uses stem work and setups to turn around opponents before he’s 
executing his break. 
229
players.pre_draft_advice:
Anderson could be worth a late pick in leagues with rookie drafts with at least five rounds. 
Otherwise, monitor him as a free agent addition after your rookie draft to see what his NFL Draft capital will be.

players.name: Kaelon Black
players.elevator_pitch:
Black is a smart runner with cutback ability who also attacks downhill with ferocity for his size. He 
finds cutbacks with deep presses into the line, but when it’s time to hit a tight crease as designed, Black gets skinny. 
Black has good footwork, understands how to manipulate defenders with pace and stride changes, and he has dynamic 
jump stops and spins to avoid late shots. If Black can get his feet higher in traffic, he could become even tougher to bring to 
the turf. 
Black is quick enough to reach the secondary and fast enough to make defensive backs chase him down after flipping the 
field. At the end of runs, Black delivers contact with excellent pad level. He forces defenders to wrap him, and he’ll push and 
drag defenders, bouncing off multiple reaches and/or hits to his frame. 
Black has enough skills as a pass-protector and pass-catcher that these facets of his game shouldn’t hold him back from 
competing for playing time this year if the depth chart is thin enough or Black impresses fast enough. 
Black reminds me of Jordan James or a slower version of J.K. Dobbins without the same refinement, but enough versatility 
to continue growing into committee-level asset. 
Vision: Black displays enough patience with heavy run boxes to adjust his approach to buy time for creases to open but 
when they don’t, he takes the nearest soft spot at the line available and makes the most of the play. 
Black also presses stalk blocks at the second level, pressing within a step of one side of the blocker to set up the bounce or 
cutback to the open crease. 
Black identifies interior penetration as he approaches the line and bounces the run away from the defender. 
In short-yardage situations, Black has attacked the designed crease hard, hitting it decisively with his pads low. There are 
short-yardage runs where he should have done this but opted to press deep and bounce it when there was a crease to hit 
decisively up the middle. He got the yardage, but the decision wasn’t optimal. 
Black presses the back-side hip of the puller to set up gap runs working to the outside. He presses inside gaps to set up 
perimeter designs. He’s a decisive gap runner whether it’s an interior run or a perimeter design. 
Black gets skinny through creases after attacking the press point at a good pace and cutting back. 
Elusiveness: Black adjusts his gait and pace as he approaches the line of scrimmage on interior run designs. He points 
outside foot to the boundary to open his hips for 90-degree turns. He can do this to make transitions at the edge of the box 
with two steps and make the force defender miss. 
Black has a capable jump stop at the edge of a crease to adjust his gait and pace so he can set up a block at the second 
level. 
Black only needs two quick steps to transition from a perimeter approach to a downhill path. He spins off glancing shots to 
get downhill for an extra 1-3 yards when he doesn’t keep his feet. 
123
players.pre_draft_advice:
Black is a patience play, but he could pop if there are training camp injuries on a depth 
chart. He’ll likely endear himself to coaches and teammates with his competitive demeanor, smarts, and physical playing 
style. 
Black would be an unlikely full-time starter, but he has the profile of a player who might have 1-2 massive years due to a 
season-ending injury as the reliable replacement behind a good offensive line. He’s a late-round value.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Black runs with light feet and punches above his weight class to consistently get more yards than expected. Once in an NFL camp, his ability to prove himself as a pass catcher will be critical to carving out a part-time role.

players.name: Eric Rivers
players.lateround_profile: Eric Rivers owns the second-best single-season yards per route run mark in this class. Seriously. His career yards per route run sits at 2.40 — eighth-best among Combine invite wide receivers. So… do we have something here? Probably not. Rivers will turn 24 in September, putting him on the older side of the prospect spectrum. He played two years at Memphis with minimal production, transferred to Florida International for two more, and then finished at Georgia Tech. That eye-popping 3.57 yards per route run season came in his second year at FIU. The seasons surrounding it? 1.32 and 2.07. One of those years is not like the others. That outlier campaign was strong enough to earn him a 32.8 Breakout Score, which isn’t nothing for a projected Day 3 pick. But it’s not particularly impressive either — especially for a 176-pound receiver. We saw a ceiling season, sure. But it came at FIU, not exactly against elite competition. When you zoom out, this looks more like a one-year spike than a sustainably dominant profile.
players.elevator_pitch:
Rivers is a slot receiver with some down-field ability, but the strength of his game is underneath 
routes. He delivers breaks with good snap, finds the open area, and generates yardage after the catch with good stop-start 
quickness and patient setups of blocks. Rivers extends well for the ball over the middle, and he withstands physical play. 
Pair his work on offense with his return skills and Rivers could deliver production in a role we’ve seen for Josh Downs and
