Jamison Crowder. That’s not exciting for fantasy football, but it’s necessary when offense use three and four receivers. 
171
players.pre_draft_advice:
Monitor from afar. If your league includes scoring for special teams returns, monitor 
Rivers to see if he earns a role as a return specialist. Eventually, he might earn a dual role that makes him worthwhile.

players.name: J.Michael Sturdivant
players.lateround_profile: We actually did see a flicker of early-career promise from J. Michael Sturdivant, the least user-friendly database name in the class (if you know, you know). Sturdivant began at Cal, and in his second season there, he posted an adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt mark that barely missed the (low) Breakout Score threshold. Unfortunately, that ended up being the high point of his collegiate journey in that metric, so he never officially recorded a Breakout Score. After Cal, Sturdivant transferred to UCLA for two seasons and then finished at Florida. The cumulative profile is rough: a career 1.40 yards per route run, fourth-worst in this class. Over the last five years, no wide receiver in the ZAP Model database with a sub- 1.50 career yards per route run and no Breakout Score has produced more than 6.0 PPR points per game in a season. There are legitimate physical tools. At 6’3”, 207 pounds, Sturdivant ran a 4.40 40-yard dash. His Speed Score is excellent, and his broad and vertical jumps were strong as well. On paper, that’s a really appealing athletic foundation. If those measurables belonged to a 21-year-old prospect, you could talk yourself into a developmental upside case. But Sturdivant will be over 23-and-a-half at draft time. At that age, with that age-adjusted production, it’s a difficult sell. The physical tools are there. The track record isn’t. And historically, that combination — especially for an older prospect — hasn’t led to fantasy relevance. players.elevator_pitch:
Sturdivant is a deceptive evaluation. Watch him play at Florida, and he’s used in a role that doesn’t 
maximize his tools. He looks like a tall and fast player with limited route skills. Completing a pass for a big play, that was 
the extent of a good day for Sturdivant. 
The same felt true at UCLA. A big program out West recruited a big receiver to transfer and used him to get one big play a 
game. 
120
players.pre_draft_advice:
Sturdivant is the first of several receivers on this list with potential to become starters, 
but the volatility of their games make the timeline difficult to predict when it will happen – if it happens at all. Consider 
Sturdivant for the third round before the NFL Draft. You may have to adjust his ADP based on his NFL Draft capital in 
formats the select alter.

players.name: Cyrus Allen
players.elevator_pitch:
Allen is a shifty slot receiver with big-play ability after the catch and in the vertical game against zone 
defenders. He’s at his best against underneath coverage where he can serve as an extension of the ground game or as a 
receiver who works across the middle into open space as he earns the ball in the intermediate game. 
Allen has potential to become a viable boundary receiver, but there are details to address with releases, stems, setups, 
breaks, and positioning against defenders before he attacks the ball. The likelihood for Allen to develop all these skills and 
overcome the label as a late-round or UDFA space player is small. 
188
players.pre_draft_advice:
Monitor from afar until we see if he earns NFL Draft capital. Regardless he’s wroth 
stashing on an expanded offseason roster. 
190

players.name: Reggie Virgil
players.lateround_profile: Reggie Virgil spent three seasons at Miami of Ohio before transferring to Texas Tech in 2025. The early production was quiet — just two receptions across his first two years. As a junior, though, he flashed with a 2.20 receiving yards per team pass attempt mark, which translated to a respectable 40.9 Breakout Score. The problem is that the production didn’t meaningfully elevate once he moved to the Big 12 in 2025. Virgil has a lean frame and was frequently deployed as a vertical threat. Among the 46 wide receiver Combine invites this year, his career deep-ball target rate and average depth of target both ranked third-highest. That role helps explain why his first downs per route run was so low — he was operating in a true boom-or-bust capacity. From a fantasy perspective, that’s concerning. Since 2020, there have been 12 wide receivers in the ZAP Model with a career deep-ball rate above 30% and a first downs per route run rate below 8%. Not one of them has become close to fantasy relevant. Could Virgil buck the trend and carve out a field-stretching role in the NFL? Sure. Teams always need ways to thin out defenses. But historically, that archetype has struggled to translate into consistent fantasy production. More likely than not, Virgil will end up as a depth receiver who helps an NFL offense in specific packages. He’s probably not someone who will meaningfully impact rookie drafts.
players.elevator_pitch:
Virgil is possession-plus receiver who can win downfield thanks to his length, ability to earn position 
on defenders at the boundary and win the ball, and capable hands. Virgil is a limited route runner who must develop a 
better man-to-man and timing-route game to emerge as more than a situational player or reserve. 
Virgil’s routes are better in the shallow range of the field than the intermediate range. It’s this area of the field that is the 
difference between having every-down value and being limited to situational football. 
257
players.pre_draft_advice:
Monitor Virgil from afar. If Virgil earns NFL Draft capital this spring, he could be worth 
consideration for expanded offseason rosters as a free agent to monitor on your roster during camp.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Virgil is a tall, silky-smooth athlete with the long-striding speed and adequate ball skills that give him a fighting chance as an outside pro receiver. His versatility on special teams coverages will increase his value.

players.name: Barion Brown
players.lateround_profile: Barion Brown is fast. Legit fast. A former track athlete, he was heavily involved as a kick and punt returner across four collegiate seasons — three at Kentucky and one at LSU. He showed off that speed at the Combine, running a 4.40 40. And, all things considered, the production profile isn’t terrible. At his size and with his speed, he likely projects as more of a gadget or space-oriented receiver at the next level. Historically, those players aren’t consistent fantasy league- winners. But there’s at least something to work with. Brown posted a 1.97 adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt mark as a freshman — the best season of his career — which helped generate a 47.6 Breakout Score (66th percentile). That’s a strong number. Brown’s usage looks exactly how you’d expect for a player with his profile. He ranked top-10 among Combine-invited wide receivers in both behind-the-line and deep-ball target rates. Roughly 18% of his collegiate receiving yards came on screens — comfortably a top- 10 figure in this class. In other words, most of his opportunities came on manufactured touches or vertical shots. That doesn’t scream nuanced route technician — it suggests a player offenses were trying to scheme into space or send downfield to generate explosives. Again…speed. There’s intrigue because traits like his are rare enough to warrant attention. But this archetype tends to require overwhelming athletic dominance to truly hit in fantasy. Brown has juice. The question is whether it’s enough to overcome the limitations in his route profile and overall usage history.
players.elevator_pitch:
Brown is an athletic flanker-slot receiver with good route setups but lackluster breaks. He has 
impressive catches on tape against tight coverage in the vertical game, but there are lapses with tracking the ball that 
create a bad chain of mishaps. When Brown leaves his feet unnecessarily because he doesn’t have a confident bead on the 
ball, he lapses into clap attacks or uses the suboptimal attack position for the trajectory of the target. 
Otherwise, Brown earns the ball against tight coverage and hard contact with a reliable frequency that one can envision an 
NFL team valuing him Brown as the third or fourth option in 11 personnel sets who can generate big plays up the seams 
against zone, manufactured plays outside against man, and from the backfield as a runner or screen receiver. 
249
players.pre_draft_advice:
In larger leagues, consider Brown in the late rounds. Otherwise, monitor from afar. If 
Brown earns NFL Draft capital this spring, he could be worth consideration for expanded offseason rosters as a free agent to 
monitor on your roster during camp.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Brown is more specialized than well-rounded as a wide receiver, but his speed and special teams impact can be a differentiating factor in the NFL. He projects as a WR4/5 and kick returner extraordinaire.

players.name: Noah Whittington
players.lateround_profile: Noah Whittington played six seasons of college football and never reached 1,000 total yards in a single year. Whittington’s most encouraging stretch likely came in Year 3, his first season at Oregon after two years at Western Kentucky. That year, he posted his best adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt rate and his second-best adjusted total yards per team play mark. He also tied his career-high reception share at 7.1%. And he did that in the same offense as Bucky Irving. You’d hope the arrow would continue pointing up from there, but it didn’t. Whittington tore his ACL in 2023, the following season, and the profile never really recovered. His best-season reception share ranked bottom-five in the class. His adjusted receiving yards per team pass attempt was also bottom-five. His top adjusted total yards per team play mark — 1.20 — ranked third-worst among Combine-invited running backs. Even if we soften the thresholds by 20% — pushing them to roughly an 8.5% reception share, 1.45 total yards per team play, and 0.50 receiving yards per team pass attempt — Whittington still falls well short. In the ZAP Model database, 66 running backs have failed to meet those boosted baselines. Just two — Andre Ellington and Chris Carson — produced even one fantasy-relevant season in their first three years. That’s not encouraging. The profile is iffy, and the lack of any true ceiling season makes it difficult to sell Whittington as anything more than a depth guy. As always with running backs, landing spot can manufacture short-term opportunity. But analytically, this is not a profile you’d want to aggressively bet on.
players.brugler_summary: Overall, Whittington is a compact, muscular runner with vision and burst through the hole and toughness as a finisher and blocker. Given the questions about his size and durability, he projects as a rotational back in an NFL backfield.
